chapters.title: Pench, Maharashtra: Tiger Haven in a Power Plant
paragraphs: The grass was all burnt out by the heat of the summer of Central India. The trees wore a barren look waiting for the rains to arrive and hardly took the trouble of providing any shade. Still she hopefully nudged the earth in search of a succulent tender shoot or maybe a tuft of grass which had eluded the scorching sun, She was a young deer just grown out of fawn-hood. Her coat of white spots and gold shone brightly with the promise of life. Her herd lazed about. The proud stag with magnificent antlers stood chewing a mouthful of dry grass. His harem threw him adoring glances from time to time. The fawns did not care about the heat and chased each other in a game of catch me if you can. They were a contended lot in the jungle where the tiger ruled. They feared not since they had security from above. A group of langurs were dozing on the branches of a jamun tree but their sentry sat alert high above on the topmost branch. He scanned the deep gullies and the golden grasslands for any signs of danger.

She was content though a little more grass, tender and green, would have been most welcome. She kept on searching. As she neared the corner of a large boulder the alarm rang out from top of the jamun tree. ‘kharrr khaaarrrr kheack kharrrr...’. The sentry had spotted danger and was looking at a bush directly behind her. The call galvanized everybody into action.... the langur pack scrambled higher up. Mothers clutched their babies while the males took up the call. Her herd scattered like the dry leaves in a storm. She knew the meaning of the call and knew by instinct that if unheeded she would meet a terrible fate.

With a fluent flick of her leg she soared over the boulder... another flick and she cleared the danger and landed on the other side. That landing was not fluent. Fate had placed a hole dug up by the wild boar at exactly the same spot where her left hind leg landed. The sound of breaking was audible as the tibia splintered in two. She crashed to the ground but instinct told her to get up and run. However, run she could not. The left hind leg uselessly dangling and limp did not permit anything but an extremely painful limp. She managed to drag herself to the grassland bordering Boat Camp dam and slowly lowered herself down in the only patch of greenery for miles around. She vanished in the tall grass but her vanishing trick had not gone unseen. This was the domain of the Jackal and one of the new mothers had seen the vanishing trick of the Jackal and she moved in for the kill.

This is where the Jackal mother met the Hulk of Boat Camp. He was the bull Gaur of a large herd and he was pursuing a young Gaur lady with all his massive but clumsy charm. He and his lady were very near the grassy patch where the fawn had taken shelter. The Hulk saw the slinking form of the Jackal mother. He resented any slinking creature near his lady love and charged the Jackal mother. Those thundering hooves were too much for the puny jackal and she bolted. Having seen the Jackal off, the Hulk turned back towards his lady love and the Jackal made her move again. She was discovered and again the charge took place. The Jackal ran for her life but came back again as soon as the Hulk turned.

This went on for some time but by this time the fawn had understood her danger and limped across as stealthily as she could towards the road from where I was watching the tragedy unfold from my Gypsy. The Hulk at last lost interest in the Jackal and decided to leave the scene and disappear over a hillock. Finally, the Fawn was at the mercy of the Jackal mother. The Jackal immediately trotted across towards the Fawn which looked at approaching death with terrified eyes. Just as the Jackal was about to pounce upon her prey, she noticed the Gypsy and retreated but did not disappear but sat down nearby under a tree. The waiting game began. The Fawn was unable to move anymore and the Jackal knew that her time would come. I waited unable to move away from the drama unfolding. However, shortly it dawned on me that since I could not tamper with the laws of Mother Nature, it would be best to leave the scene and let nature take its course.

As the sun was getting low, I drove on towards the dam. I shall never ever forget the look in those liquid eyes of the Fawn as it watched helplessly towards my vanishing trail and then turned towards the Jackal who was about to get up, to face her fate. A quarter of an hour later I returned the same way as dusk was gathering. The space was quite empty. An eerie silence and the throbbing of the Gypsy engine greeted each other as | searched in vain for a little spotted form, brave yet doomed. I was too tiny a spoke in the huge wheel of Nature and I knew the futility of my searching eyes.
Pench or for that matter, any other place where man has not stamped his uncivilized hands has many stories to tell if one has the ears to listen, eyes to see and a heart to feel.

chapters.title: The Realm of the Red Panda
paragraphs: Pradeep means lamp. He was my guide but from time to time he was getting extinguished. The month of July in Eastern Himalayas sees torrential rains but, on this trail, luck was in our favour... no rain till then. However, thick clouds at 8000 feet (approximately) kept pressing us from all the sides. We had left the little village of Dhotrey of some 30 huts, behind, and climbed at a rate which would have made a MIG proud. A narrow mountain path wound up through the Pine and Oaks. Wild flowers carpeted the forest floor wherever they found space.

Pradeep and I were accompanied by a small group of village ladies who had ventured out to gather firewood but soon we left them behind. Somewhere up in the clouds I spotted movement to the left on top of a Fern tree. They were Brown Parrotbills a lifer for me. Both of us left the track and descended down the slope in hot pursuit. The birds acted coy and let us approach near till I was about to raise my camera and then took flight to alight on another branch a little farther away. Suffice it to say that after half an hour’s unrelenting pursuit when we two emerged back on the track we were cursing a little about the obstinacy of the species. I did not get a single decent image.

We had reached the top of a knoll where an old man sat chewing tobacco. Pradeep sat chatting by his side and I drew deeply from a bottle of salted tea to quench my thirst. Another woodcutter from the village passed by and waved us a happy smile, his sunburnt Mongoloid features beaming. The weather, which was misty till then, started to turn into clouds. The ancient Pines started to lose their texture as the moisture laden mist cloaked them. It was time to move into the Red Panda’s realm.

We left the narrow path and Pradeep plunged headlong into the extremely dense thicket. The soil was soaked with moisture and the path was barely discernible and sometimes vanished altogether. Balancing my Camera and lens I groped for a suitable piece of wood to keep me from falling down. After much searching, I got one covered with moss and as slippery as a frog ona rainy day. However, it served the purpose somewhat and we carried on through that almost impassable terrain. Frankly speaking I would have given up after a kilometre but Pradeep kept on pushing me, “Saab, just a little bit more and we shall reach the tree”. That little bit more stretched on interminably through heavy fog, almost no visibility, dripping moss and lichen covered ancient conifers soaking me. The weather was cold but I sweated profusely as I looked down at the steep drop looming a few inches from my feet as I tried to squeeze myself against the facing rocks and vegetation on the opposite side. One false step or a chunk of soil giving way and the drop would have been a long one.

All nightmares have an ending and we reached the tree where the Pandas sometimes come to rest. The cloud was dense and there was absolutely no movement from the direction of the upper branches where they generally stay. Pradeep climbed up the slippery trunk to look for signs like excreta but there were none. Our search was futile. It was time to climb down and the trek started again.

Climbing down is generally not a difficult proposition in the mountains as long as there is a semblance of a path. In our case we lost our way in that grim dark forest twice. At one point there was a tree whose bark was shredded into fragments by a Himalayan Black Bear and it did not seem very old. Meeting a bear in that situation would have been most unpleasant to say the least. At another place some heavy animal had dug a hole in the ground and Pradeep almost disappeared inside it. However, we persevered, sometimes on all fours and reached the safety of the road which leads from Maneybhanjan to Sandakphu.

Imagine my distress when I returned to Gautam’s homestay where I had put up, to find the woodcutter who had smiled at us had actually captured a video footage of a red panda with his Nokia mobile phone. He had gone in the opposite direction taken by us. The next few days were washed out by rain and I returned to base empty handed but with a swear that I shall nail the small chap next time when the clouds clear.

chapters.title: Manas: Alluringly Beautiful
paragraphs: The sub-adult elephant was wild. I was convinced about it by the way it grazed next to the forest camp. It did not have the tell-tale mark of the captive elephants’ shackles around the ankles. Vinit and Anirudh were from Central India and hence were not that familiar with elephants. Sougata however, was dead sure that it belonged to the forest department and was tame. Hence, to prove his point he made a peculiar chuck chucking sound at the elephant probably expecting it to say hi to him with a wave of its trunk. Instead there was an ear-splitting triaaaaaaaank and the next moment the hulk was thundering down the road towards us.

The driver put the engine of the Gypsy in reverse and we put a decent distance between us and the pachyderm. Having been foiled in its efforts to get a closer look at us, the young fellow glared at us standing in the middle of the road, then reluctantly left the path and slowly... very slowly strolled towards a grassy patch inside the forest.

This gave us the chance and our driver revved up the engine and sped down the road, past the point where the charge took place. We were merrily careering on when a gust of wind caught Vinit’s beloved jungle cap and tossed it off his head onto the road. A stalemate ensued.... Vinit being too fond of his cap, which had stayed with him for years in his tiger terrain, refused to budge without attaching himself back to it... the elephant was still inside the grassy patch, though it was some distance away by now.

Finally, Vinit got down with the guard.... walked down the road, picked up his cap, gave a withering look at the elephant in the distance and walked back to us proudly holding his capped head high. Such was the severity of his look that the elephant departed... or maybe he had other urgent matters to attend to.

A little distance down the same road and we saw two gentlemen walking down the road with gunny bags full of groceries and stores. They were forest guards; they were not even armed. One carried a hefty pumpkin but as a matter of protection from charging elephants, it hardly counted.
“What will they do if the elephant decides to charge when they are walking on the road?”, Vinit asked the guard.

“Saab if they meet the elephant on the road, they will not need to do anything... whatever needs to be done will be done by the elephant”, replied the guard laconically. We drove back pondering in silence. These guards are unsung but their valour is probably no less than the bravest of the brave our country has produced.

Vinit, Sougata, Anirudh and myself had landed up in Manas National Park one fine November evening after an epic journey in Vinit’s Bolero. Vinit and Anirudh had driven from Nagpur and Sougata and myself joined them at Gorumara National Park in North Bengal. From there we drove to Dibru Saikhowa, Kaziranga and Pobitora before landing up at Manas near the gate Vinit parked his Bolero and we had a refreshing bath and a hot dinner of chicken curry and chapattis followed by some delectable kheer for dessert. As we lazed around in the resort, we saw some small shadows near the gate. A herd of Hog Deer were grazing in the half-light thrown by the moon and stars.

When we entered the forest in the early morning through the central Bansbari Gate, it was like entering Jurassic Park. Probably the most picturesque of all the forests I have been fortunate enough to visit, it is a journey that will remain etched in the memory of any nature lover.

We drove through a mixed deciduous forest onto a vast grassland. The Eastern Himalayas of Bhutan towered over us. Elephants dotted the landscape at a distance. From there, the journey took us to Mathanguri Forest Rest Bungalow, one of the most picturesque forest rest houses though basic as far as facilities are concerned. The Manas river bifurcates just adjacent to the bungalow which sits atop an incline where the Himalayas start. The Bhutan border post is just a short distance from the bungalow and we had the pleasure of walking in a foreign country with the highest GNH (Gross National Happiness) for about 300 metres and we could not be happier. The sky was azure blue. The Himalayas of Bhutan rose in waves. Manas river flowed in all her blue glory rippling over the glazed rocks. ‘he Royal Manas National Park of Bhutan gleamed across the river, basking in the autumn sunshine. However, the sad part was that the Golden Langurs for which Manas is famous had all immigrated towards the Bhutan side leaving only their cousins, the Capped Langurs on the Indian Side. We were yet to see them but that void was filled in a short while.

On our next leg we were passing some amazing forest with trees the size I had never seen except in movies. It would have taken at least ten men or more to encircle them at the base. Amidst those tall trees were a patch of trees relatively shorter and the troop of Capped Langurs were basking in the sun. As we pointed upwards with our lenses one of the Langurs tuned around disdainfully and peed on us missing us by a whisker. Later I found that my lens had managed to freeze the action.

That evening we were driving back through a dark and damp forest which resembled a rainforest. The forest floor was dank and the tree trunks were covered thickly with moss. Wild buffalos looked at us curiously wallowing in the swampy ground... and then the tyre went flat.

By the time the tyre was replaced it was dark and some leeches found their way inside our socks. We just offloaded them with a pinch of salt. “Tata Namak’ to the rescue! In the distance owls hooted from the tree tops. The sky, from a fiery orange, changed to dark green and then black and the stars twinkled above the canopy. Fireflies made their way around the bushes in a frenzied dance. Night descended on Manas.

The icing on the cake took place on our way back from Manas. We were travelling towards Alipurduar from where Sougata and I were to catch our train back to Calcutta while Vinit and Anirudh would be driving on their way to Nagpur. We were having tea in a shop outside the forest where we started chatting with an old gentleman who knew Manas well. We were saddened that we did not find the Golden Langurs and told him so.

“But they are there... but not in the forest. There is a small village a long way down where the villagers have protected a troop of them around their village. You can go and see if they are still around”, said the old man and gave us the directions.

Following his directions, we landed at the village hoping against hope to have a glimpse of the elusive Langurs. The villagers received us quite enthusiastically and soon we were threading our way through a dense bamboo grove squinting our eyes towards the canopy of the bamboo fronds. Soon something bright and golden peeped at us from the thicket. We had our first sighting of the Golden Langur.
Mad with joy we tried to get a better look when the whole troop started moving across the bamboo grove and we followed at a mad pace. They reached a tree line and there they rested.

What an amazing sight.... about fifteen of them spread out across the tree tops and simply lazed there. The sun set their golden furs on fire. Mothers caressed their babies. Males lazily plucked some ripe figs and like monarchs, crossed their legs across the branches and chewed with utmost concentration. The village dozed nearby. Cattle grazed placidly in a field. Our SD cards filled rapidly with images while the hearts filled with joy. I marvelled at the conservation awareness among the villagers who had saved one of the most exquisite species in the world from getting extinct in India.

Manas might not have high profile leaders from across the world come visiting. Manas does not have proper facilities for tourism like some of the major Tiger Reserves and National parks. The forest staff is inadequate to keep vigil across the forest but still they carry out their jobs with dedication... or at least most of them do. Manas does not have lines of Gypsies lining the entrance with hordes of tourists spilling from every nook and cranny. Manas only has pristine beauty.... many places untouched and unspoilt by humans. Manas looks what this earth was supposed to look and probably did from a time long forgotten in our modern world. Pygmy Hog, Bengal Florican, Hispid Hare.... Manas is one of their last homes on this planet before they vanish. It is worth saving.

chapters.title: 12 Days in a Stable with 30 Cows and 1 Bull
paragraphs: Khunti... that is what a stable is called in Dibru Saikhowa, Upper Assam. I was on a budget and I had to shoot the Western Hoolock Gibbons and during the same trip wanted to observe the arrival of the migratory flocks in Maguri Beel. Balancing the budget with a longish stay proved somewhat difficult and I asked Sanjay, my henchman and brother in lens in Dibru Saikhowa to look into the matter. He asked me whether I was willing to stay in a khunti in which case the costs would be minimal. Not knowing what a khunti was, all I asked him that whether there would be a cover over my head which he confirmed. The tour was on.

A sleepy early morning late in October 2013 my train chugged into Tinsukia station where Sanjay picked me up and drove me to his delightful little house near Guijan Ghat, the entrance to Dibru Saikhowa National Park with the Lohit river flowing by. After spending the day with his family, towards late afternoon we arrived at the ghat where a wooden boat was waiting to ferry people across. Sanjay and I hopped in with my rucksack and camera bag and shortly the boat was huffing and puffing across the swift flowing river. The Himalayas of Arunachal stood against the sky with the sun going down.

The boat touched down at a sand bank and disgorged an assortment of about 12 passengers including the two of us. The bank stretched for about a kilometre and a half. After the bank there was a sharp rise in the slope and a small village of fishermen of some 30 huts stood there. Sanjay belonged to this village. The sun had almost set and the village was surrounded by forests on all sides except that of the river. The hens and ducks were being herded inside their coops. The evening lanterns were about to be lit and I was wondering where my khunti was. As we walked to the edge of the small hamlet, there spread before us were a few stables if they could be called stables. They were flimsy grass walled structures with polythene as ceilings. The grass wall was woven between bamboo work and somehow the structures stood. Here I came to know that this is what a khunti was. Sanjay was looking for Manoj’s khunti and we made our way to one of the last stables.

Manoj, a middle-aged herdsman from Bihar, settled for a few generations amidst the tea gardens and forests of upper Assam had lost most of his Bihari roots. A darkish man, sinewy and wearing an infectious grin met us at the “door” of his khunti where he was corralling his cows for the night. The “door” consisted of a few thin bamboos cut vertically in half and held together by 3 sticks. The latch was a piece of string. Inside, on the sandy floor were two wooden planks on either side. This was the sleeping quarter, one for Manoj and one for me. I was carrying Sanjay’s sleeping bag. I dumped the sleeping bag and my rucksack on the plank. 'I'here was a pillow too. ‘I'he camera bag nestled against the pillow. I was ready for my stay in Dibru Saikhowa National Park.

Sanjay departed. Manoj lit a storm lantern which fought a losing battle with the shadow inside the hut. The cows shuffled, separated from us by a dried grass curtain. There was no bad smell since the floor was actually sand brought in by the annual flood of the river. The attached space behind our sleeping quarters had a hole in the ground where a pair of chopped logs kept a fire going constantly. Manoj dumped a few chipped pieces of wood and blew on the fire and lo.... we had a roaring small neat fire. It was growing cold outside.
Manoj milked one of the cows, put the milk in a tumbler, boiled it on fire with some sugar and tea leaves and our chai was ready.

We sat down and Mangj instructed me on the do’s and don’ts of stable life in the jungle. The only source of water was a hand pump outside our residence. The toilet... well...... it was anywhere because outside was the jungle. However, it was preferable to walk about 50 meters to the water’s edge of the river for the heavy-duty purging. Smaller calls of nature could be answered anywhere nearby and the sand will leave no trace or smell. Whatever the reason for stepping out of the grass filigree residence, the first step without fail would be to take my torch and shine it around to make sure that there were no elephants or wild buffalos to bung a spanner in my plan of works. Other point was to take a stick and thump it on the ground to scare away any snakes lying in the grass outside. In case I wanted to answer the call of nature early in the morning then I was to call Manoj to stand guard over me.

I kept the glass down after finishing the hot sweet liquid. Manoj started preparing for dinner. The smoke from the fire made the light of the lamp go weaker in the knees. I picked up my trusted Eveready, unfastened the string of the bamboo network which was our door and peered out. The darkness almost blinded me and I shone my torch around hoping to pick up the silhouette of a tusker or the bulk of a bull wild buffalo. There were none and I stepped out gingerly and switched off the torch.

The river flowed with a subdued gurgling sound. A half-moon floated near the horizon. A cold draught blew in from the mountains rippling the grass. The sweet smell of approaching winter mixed with the wood smoke from the fire inside the hut and permeated the forest. Somewhere an owl hooted and fell silent. I looked up at the universe and the stars came down to meet me. If ever I felt at one and peace with the universe, it was at that moment just before my mobile rang.

The signal was erratic to say the least but now it was showing two bars of signal and Sanjay was calling me. It was to inform me that one of his goats had been taken by a leopard behind my hut and he was asking me to stay indoors for the time being. I did not require much persuasion.

Three days went by and I got used to my routine. Get up before the sun comes up, walk up to the riverside, flash my torch to see if I would be disturbed in my duties by one of the wild rowdies, answer the call of nature and come back. By the time I got ready with my camera and accessories, Sanjay’s brother would be at my door as the light came up the horizon. Cross the village, trek across the river bank for 1.5 km, catch a rickety boat and cross over to Guijan Ghat. A breakfast of puffed rice or bread omelette and pillion ride on Sanjay’s motorbike to Maguri Beel to watch the migration. A whole day on a dugout floating amidst the water lilies, walking through the mud and grass, getting roasted in the midday sun and thoroughly enjoying myself. Evening time the actions would reverse and by the time I reached my million-star accommodation, I would be dead beat but not dead enough to look up and touch those stars while sipping my evening chai.

On the 4th afternoon, the weather decided to intervene. I, as a former sailor, am used to seeing and facing storms but that evening, the clouds piled up above our heads in heaps I had never witnessed before even in winter Atlantic or the Bering Straits. By four o clock the deluge started. Asterix’s village could have been pardoned if they thought that the sky was falling on their heads. I sat inside my water proof sleeping bag waiting for the wind to blow that crazy structure off to the Himalayas. Thunders broke more frequently than Captain Haddock’s curses. A hen landed on my feet and flatly refused to budge. Manoj soothed the frayed nerves of the cattle finding his way with the storm lantern. One part of the ceiling gave way around eight in the evening but that was on the side of the cows. The cows were re-tethered but the rest of ceiling miraculously held. | was half prepared spend the night on my wooden plank sitting under the Niagara. I kept my camera gear secured with layers of plastic.

That deluge lasted full four days without relenting. Fourth morning the sun rose through the clouds. I fastened camera gear to my back and made my way to the sand bank but the sand bank was nowhere to be seen. A furious river roared and smashed the sandy shores. The sand bank lay under a fathom of thundering waves. There was a single dugout and the boatman agreed to carry me with another 4 passengers to Guijan Ghat. The batteries of my camera and mobile had to get charged and I had to get across to the nearest electricity point available which was at a grocer’s shop in Guijan Ghat on the other side. I clambered in and the boat and we started. By the time we reached the middle of the river the water was rising inside the boat at a furious pace. It was a matter of touch and go whether we would reach the shore first or the boat will go underwater. The oarsman gave it all he had and when I touched down at Guijan Ghat the boat resembled a German U Boat diving under the Atlantic.

Dibru Saikhowa is a mesmerizing place. The migratory flocks had begun touching down at Maguri Beel where the water lily opened their petals to welcome them. The smell of water and grass and mud clung to me like a lover. The Wild Buffalos posed a problem at certain times especially the time when I went to the river early one morning to answer natures call. My torch did not pick up any trace of any buffalo and I squatted down peacefully when a snorting sound came from the tall grasses just behind me up a steep sandy incline. | flashed my torch and picked up a bull buffalo who was descending the sandy bank. I did not wait to argue with those massive horns and beat a hurried retreat. It was not the most dignified of retreats since I was caught with my pants down, literally. However, the Gibbons amply compensated me later for all the slight and thoroughly enjoyable inconveniences.

The food was from the jungle... yams dug from the forest... elephant apples lying under the trees... rice and plenty of milk. The accommodation was million-star. The devotion and zeal with which Sanjay worked for conservation and his knowledge of the land was something to learn. Manoj and Sanjay’s brother Shankar made me feel that this is where I belonged and probably, I did. The Gibbons co-operated though the rain gods did not. Still... stars touching your head.... the wood smoke.... whispering breeze... lightning searing the sky.... the flowing river... the call of the Gibbons... the wings of the migratory Gadwalls splintering the sunlight... freedom is a feeling heard by many, felt by few.
I am fortunate.

chapters.title: A Rogue Wild Buffalo and Me in Pobitora
paragraphs: I was hanging on for dear life to a branch in a tree some 10 feet off the ground. My companion Binod was also hanging on to the same branch. The forest guard was hiding in a hole on the ground and a cold and calculating wild buffalo was staring at us from an uncomfortably close range no doubt wanting to get a closer acquaintance. That buffalo had killed two people over the last few days and was definitely trying to get his tally higher. At that critical juncture my call phone rang. I fished it out from my pocket and answered.
“Dada... did I leave my jacket with you last time?”, my friend Amit from Bombay enquired from across the ether.
Since I was not sure whether I am going to live or die on that branch I was a bit sharp with my answer but later when I told Amit about the incident, he pardoned me.

The migratory flocks had gathered in their thousands over the wetlands of Pobitora Wildlife Sanctuary where the concentration of rhinos is the highest in the world. They arrived in their thousand to spend the winter... Ruddy Shelducks, Northern Pintails, Garganeys, Pochards, Lesser Whistling Ducks, Northern Lapwings all had congregated on this small patch of forest land. I had brought my wife and two kids to witness the spectacle.
However, that particular afternoon I had to go in myself to photograph the flocks. Binod and myself were accompanied by a forest guard. Nripen drove us in and left in his Gypsy.

As we stood on the forest track, on our left was a large patch of grassland beyond which lay the prize.... wetland teeming with birds. On the right were short trees and slightly undulating land. We left the road and plunged into grass which was almost our height. The grass hid the terrain which looked deceptively plain. Once inside it was tough going since the earth was dotted with pits created by rhinos and wild buffaloes during the wet season and then hardened into bone breakers if got your feet in the wrong position.

As we were plunging in, the Forest Guard said “Saab... today we have to be a little careful... a wild buffalo has killed two people and might come this way.” I could hear the sound of the whirring wings of the birds anda mere mad buffalo was not going to hold me back now since I was quite close to the wetlands and was not detected yet. I signalled Binod to sink low inside the grass and while sinking in myself, I told the guard, “you keep a look out and let me know if the buffalo comes anywhere near us”.

The guard stood there scanning the grassland while Binod and myself with my camera, crawled on all fours till my head just about cleared the grass bordering the water. The birds had not noticed me and were all over the place. I just waited for them to get used to my presence in case they could detect me. Binod lay behind me hidden inside the grass.

It was December and the grass and ground were cold. The water gave out a smell which is typically that of winter. The cold wind created small ripples in the water as it caressed the grass fronds. In front of me was playing out a kaleidoscope of colours... the dazzling whites of the Bar Headed Geese... flaming oranges of the Ruddy Shelducks.... the reds of the Whistling Ducks... the splash on the water by Northern Pintails.... the shining sheen on the feathers of the whirling Northern Lapwings.... and it took me a while to shake off my stupor and start clicking.
Thad hardly focussed on the darters sitting on a mound near me when the guard whispered in an urgent voice, “Saab.... come back... the buffalo is coming this way”.

Getting up from a prone position, with my heavy lens in the thick grass and soft ground bordering the wetland was no mean feat but I managed that and took my first step to running like the wind but put my front foot heavily in one of those blasted rhino pits and landed flatly on my face but managed to save my camera. Binod picked me up and we started running towards the tree line across the forest path.

While running I tried to spot the buffalo but could not see it and so I asked the guard about its whereabouts. The guard told me that he could not see it either but had seen the forest department elephants driving the buffalo inside the grass and had seen the grass moving. We reached the path without any more mishaps and I stopped to catch my breath. Before I could spot the buffalo Binod urgently whispered, “Dada... climb up that tree, the buffalo is heading this way”.

I looked at Binod and the tree he was pointing at, incredulously. I was an expert climber but that was when I weighed about 20 kilos less. Trying to climb the tree with my present weight would be committing suicide. “Binod.... if you are expecting me to climb that tree, forget it... find some other way”, saying so I turned around just in time to see the buffalo emerging from the grass and coming straight towards us. I could have beaten Edmund Hillary at climbing at that moment. It was true that Binod hauled me up and the guard gave me a hefty push on my backside but in a second or two I was standing on a branch and holding onto another with Binod by my side, ten feet above the ground. The guard did not get enough time to climb and I had thrust my lens in his hands. He found a hole in the ground and took refuge inside it.

The buffalo crossed the forest path, entered the treeline to our left and was almost passing when Binod whispered in my ear, “Dada, I think it has not noticed us”. As if the buffalo heard Binod, it stopped in its tracks about 15 feet away, turned around, looked at us and stood his ground.

There the matter rested. The buffalo refused to budge and we had no intention of budging either. ‘I'he buffalo lowered its massive horns and stomped its feet in mock charge from time to time just to remind us that the tree was our safest bet. However, even though we were up on the tree, there was one very real fear. Just behind the tree on the opposite side of the buffalo, the ground was elevated and the height difference was only about 7 feet. If the buffalo found that out and decided to come around then a toss of its head would be sufficient to bring me and Binod down. We had no hope of climbing higher since there were no other branches nearby and the next branch was a vertical climb without any grip anywhere.

Forty-five minutes passed and the stalemate continued but my arms and legs now started to protest by going numb. Clinging on to a branch with my weight and standing on another for so long does not improve your blood circulation. In desperation I let out a hoarse whisper to the Guard to do something. Next minute he poked his head out of the hole and with flailing arms started to sing, “Aja aja... main hoon tera deewana”, in a voice which would have raised the dead. The buffalo baulked for a moment but then lowered his head, gave out a loud snort and advanced a few feet. The Guard promptly disappeared back inside the hole. That was the time when network returned for a few seconds and Amit called me.

An hour passed and the situation started getting desperate. The Guard finally decided to go to the extreme and pulled up his gun, aimed somewhere just above the buffalo and pressed the trigger. Nothing happened except for a loud thud which again enraged the buffalo. The bullet was a dud. The buffalo shook its head, snorted but gazed steadily towards Binod and me. 'l'aking a desperate measure, the Guard started crawling out of the hole towards the road. Binod and myself shouted at the buffalo to divert its attention towards us. It was successfully and by the time the buffalo spotted the guard, he was sprinting for his life and out of range. He ran towards the forest outpost which was not very far away.

With the departure of the guard we were left without the benefit of the gun... even though the bullet was a dud one. The staring game started again. Shortly a lot of drumming came to our ears. The guard had called the rest of the forest guards and they were approaching us with beating drums and loud shouting. The buffalo looked at their direction once and then returned at gazing at us. I doubt whether even Majnu had looked at Laila with that amount of devotion.

When the group got sufficiently close, the guards started throwing loud fire crackers one of which landed near the buffalo and burst with a loud bang. The stubborn chap gave the smoke from the cracker a look of disdain and resumed his longing gaze. Our forest Guard had now procured a new bullet now. He walked up and let loose a shot from close quarters just above the buffalo’s shoulders. We waited to see the sudden turn of tail and galloping hooves of the one-ton muscle and horns as it ran away from the bang of the gun.
All we got to see was one more look of disdain towards the shooter and the eyes again got attached to Binod and me. Now it was more than one and a half hours and fatigue was taking over.

My hands were slipping and I knew what awaited me if I slipped. Those horns meant deadly business but I was at the point of not caring much anymore.... just thought about my wife and kids who were not aware of what was going on and the fingers found a little more strength... just long enough to hear the roar of the Gypsy engine roaring and coming nearer.

The news had reached Nripen and he roared down with his Gypsy fully revving up the engines. At that sound the buffalo took its first step back. Nripen hurtled his 4x4 through the trees and stopped under our branch with the engine going full throttle. Our tormentor retreated one step at a time faced with the sound of the engine and the horn. Binod and I jumped straight into the Gypsy from our branch and we beat our strategic retreat with the buffalo watching from a distance with wistful eyes. I am not sure but I think I saw the hint of regret in those eyes.

The buffalo was shot dead in a few days but not before he had killed one more villager. I got to know afterwards that he had been driven from his herd by the dominant bull and probably turned towards humans to vent his anger. Nature had taken her toll.

Pobitora Wildlife Sanctuary, a short distance from Guwahati, hosts the largest density of wild rhinos on the planet. Hemmed in from all sides by villages this small sanctuary is a miracle in the sense that it not only exists but thrives in spite of all adversities.

chapters.title: Lepchajagat
paragraphs: I met them for the first time on a dark stormy night in Lepchajagat. It was the month of September and monsoon was in full swing across India. I had landed up in the Forest Bungalow, the only available accommodation those days. Lepchajagat is a small sleepy hamlet on the main road leading from Darjeeling to Mirik via the town of Sukhiapokhri. The bungalow is located on the brink of a steep gorge about 100 metres down the road slightly downhill. It is totally invisible from the main road and was almost fully surrounded by dense conifer and rhododendron forests but has recently thinned down due to wood cutting. This bungalow is built in a sort of cove and is frequently surrounded by clouds though the surroundings might remain clear.

The power lines had snapped due to the rains and the bungalow had no electricity for four or five days when I arrived. I have been a regular visitor to this bungalow since 2007 and I specifically chose the monsoons to avoid other guests who at times can hinder my bird photography.

By eight in the evening Karma, the caretaker had cooked his signature khichri and scrambled eggs accompanied by a fabulous fresh salad and papad, served me and vanished towards the village uphill. The cold as usual was intense and I lit my candle and settled down in a pile of blankets on my bed. The candle threw a diffused light and the whole building was empty except me. The clouds thickened all around the bungalow and the large rhododendrons behind the door dropped the moisture incessantly. The air itself was heavy with cold and rain and the faint candlelight that percolated through the window panes only intensified the darkness outside. These are the types nights when Count Dracula is on the move and for some reason or the other, I have always loved these atmospheres. Snugly reading my Gerald Durrell (My Family and Other Animals) in the candlelight, my senses were brought back from the sunny island of Corfu to the dark cloudy Himalayan night by the alarm call of a Barking Deer quite close at hand.

As I mentioned before, Lepchajagat is quite surrounded by forests though close to Darjeeling and the forest holds a sizeable number of leopards and reportedly some Himalayan Black bears too. The Barking Deer had voiced its call quite close by and I grabbed my torch, opened the back door near the gorge and flashed the light across the fencing towards the location of the call. To my utter surprise and glee, the beam fell squarely on three Yellow-throated Martens walking nonchalantly on the fence. Though they are not a rare or threatened species yet spotting one is quite tough due to the terrain in which they live, mainly the Himalayas and the foothills. Spotting three together in the dead of the night (yes... ten at night in Lepcha is definitely dead of the night), alone, with the clouds swirling around in the dark slopes of the mountains was exhilarating to say the list.

Since that night I tried to spot and photograph them for three years at regular intervals without much success. A scat here... a little fur there... some chickens missing... that was all that they left as signatures of their presence.

One late January morning I lay flat on my stomach on the dew drenched lawn in front of the bungalow photographing the Himalayan birds which came to forage on the tit bits in the garbage dump by the side of the kitchen. They came flitting in from the dense undergrowth picked up a piece of potato or a crumb of bread discarded by the cook, squabbled and went back into the bushes. I clicked away to my heart's content, a lazy winter sun shone through the mist, Karma fought a running battle with a large slab of dough inside the kitchen and the chowkidar gossiped with a village belle behind me outside the gate.

All of a sudden, the birds cackled and disappeared en masse and a small delicately beautiful dark head popped up in my camera view finder. The alert beady and soulful eyes could melt butter at sixty paces. The head rose up above the gradient of the lawn and Theld my breath trying unsuccessfully not to bat my eyelid to avoid detection. I was lying just about 15 metres from the Marten but the eyes were not looking at me. They looked directly behind me at the chowkidar warily and kept on looking. To his credit, the chowkidar was more focused on the belle than on peeping Martens.

When she was assured that there presently was no danger from the chowkidar, she started foraging in the dump without noticing me. Now my chance came and my camera went berserk. The minutes flew and suddenly another head popped up... smaller, more delicate and infinitely beautiful. The cub had joined the mother. Both started feeding when Karma decided that the dough had taken enough beating and decided to pop out of the kitchen right beside the feeding Martens. I hoarsely whispered for him to go back but Karma’s left year was almost deaf and my cries went in vain. The mother Marten immediately reared up in alarm, my shutter went click and the next moment mother and child was there no more.

Since then the hamlet of Lepchajagat has expanded into a small village with homestays. The forest bungalow has put on a fresh coat of paint and tourists visit all the year round. There are hardly any power cuts anymore. Food has been revamped to cater to the urban taste. The trees have thinned having fallen to the axe. However, still the swirling mists covers the little building like a blanket and the hog deer still barks on lonely nights when the spotted cat is on the prowl in the gorges. The windows still frost up in the early mornings and the delicate magnolia still blooms in spring. Somewhere among those enchanted mossy pines and oaks, I hope, that beautiful little furry family and their progeny still roam happy and free.

chapters.title: Miracle of Migration
paragraphs: God found himself at his wits end. He placed all the Godwits here. The boat in which the rides are conducted have to, literally, navigate through a thicket of legs of the Black Winged Stilts. By the time you finish your stint with the Stilts you will not want to see another Stilt in your life. The number of Sandpipers is so mind boggling that I thought I had downed a bottle of Bagpiper before the start of the day and hence, was hallucinating. The phenomenon called Mangalajodi is easy to believe if you have not seen the place but very hard to believe once you have witnessed the spectacle.

On the right-hand side of the embankment where the trail ends and the boat ride starts is a patch of grass which looks like a chicken hatchery. The swampy patch of ground is overrun with Purple Swamphens. A few Open Billed Storks and Glossy Ibises accompanied by an army of Black Winged Stilts and Jack Snipes make an attempt from time to time to capture the mud patch but are soon summarily side-lined by the purple army. I sat by the side of the embankment to try to capture the action when a Bluethroat interrupted my endeavour by almost popping into my lens. T gave him my due respect by taking a few snaps and then moved farther away and concentrated on the war of the mud patch. I had hardly looked into my view finder when there he was, the Bluethroat, staring at me with innocent eyes and a pained look as if I had not paid enough attention to his charms. I felt ashamed and clicked a few more before moving away to what I presumed to be, a more secluded spot. This time, I gave up. He was back.

Disgustedly I picked up my lens without paying heed to the seductive call of the Bluethroat and walked down the trail watching the Ruddy Breasted Crakes foraging among the reed beds. Handsome chaps with blood shot eyes as if they had taken a few puffs of Ganja. A flock of Ibises floated lazily in the morning breeze over the vast wetlands and landed as softly as down on a patch of open water. Two Brahminy Shelducks circled overhead like two floating flames looking for a suitable patch of unoccupied property.

They called to each other in that questioning sort of way as if discussing the merits of the property on display. A flock of Godwits some thousands strong took to the air at a distance as an eagle flew high above. As the sun climbed overhead, the horizon simmered in lazy heat. The red dust of the soil of the embankment mixed with the smell of the decaying vegetation and sweat trickled down my shirt making it pleasantly damp.

A small rusty striped body flew low over the red earth and landed ona bush nearby. I have been trying to capture the Common, Hoopoe for quite some time but without success and here was one sitting hardly 20 feet away and preening away without a care in the world. Click click went my D-90. Nice... let me see if I can manage to get a few feet closer. ‘I'he distance shortened to about 15 feet as I clicked merrily away. As far as my model was concerned, I might as well have been a piece of twig. I went still closer and the handsome fellow preened his feathers, looked at the sky, scratched its head and behaved in a thoroughly spoilt manner. My travelling and photography partner, Avishek had been watching me from a distance and now he too decided to join the party. We approached to almost 6 feet of the bird and I started having problems maintaining focus at so short a distance. However, Avishek wanted me to take a picture of him and the bird with him patting the Hoopoe's crest, for his Facebook profile. With this singular aim in mind he boldly strode up to the astonished bird who looked at him as if affronted by his audacity. When Avishek had reached his to-be profile buddy, the Hoopoe could not take this any longer and with an admonishing cry took to its wings.

Mangalajodi is a fisherman's village which was a trapper’s heaven. The transformation from poacher to conservationist has seen spectacular results. However, the first signs of tourism generated revenue are slowly becoming evident. A school here, a few electricity posts there, a pukka road running through...
Plenty needs to be done but a starting has been made.

In a small corner of this world here in the marshes and swamps on the northern fringes of Chilika Lake in Orissa, among the brackish water, the feathered denizens of this planet have found a home without the persecution of civilization... till now.

chapters.title: A Strange Football Game in Guatemala
paragraphs: The brilliantly coloured Quetzal is the name of the national bird of Guatemala as well as the name of its currency. The port of Puerto Quetzal is on the Pacific coast of Guatemala in Central America. My ship, M.V. Evpo Ageos arrived there one July evening in the year 1994 from Tampa, USA. Out of the crew and officers numbering twenty-eight, we were three Indian Officers and two Indian Cadets and the fitter Maruti Narayan Parker was Indian. Other than myself, the Chief Engineer Mr. Sampath and Chief Officer Mr. Pereira were Indians. Rest of the crew was an eclectic mix of Greeks, Cypriots, Burmese and one German. This floating United Nations had one of the best crowds I have ever had the pleasure of sailing with.

We reached Puerto Quetzal quite late, around two in the morning. When the usual port documentation was completed and I finished reporting to the Head Office, I popped outside to get a breath the of fresh air. The sky had a wonderful and strange reddish hue. Apparently one of the volcanoes was in flow. Sometimes a distant staccato noise could be heard. The stevedore told us that a local civil war was in progress but quite far away.

Next evening Bara Saab (that is what the Chief Engineers are universally called by Indians) Mr. Sampath, Maruti Narayan Parkar the fitter and I went ashore. Fortunately, we found a cab just outside the gate. Since, after tasting ship’s food, which tasted like cooked blotting paper after a few days, for quite some time, we directed the cabbie to take us to a restaurant. The cab weaved through dark roads with dense trees standing on both sides interspersed with small villages. There were a couple of army checkpoints with smart soldiers holding smart looking guns who demanded to see our shore passes with granite expressions but relented on seeing that we were Indians and not one of the guerrilla fighters.

The crab drew up alongside a small eatery by the roadside where we were served a fiery and chunky Prawn Veracruz with various vegetables and some delicious small bread with some stuffing which melted in the mouth. After the meal we proceeded to the town. Most of the shops had closed but one sports shop, which was about to close, had a football (not the American type) displayed at the shop window. On the spur of the moment, I decided to buy it. The rest of the town was quite non-descript and had a faint dilapidated look about it.

When the cab dropped us back at the gate, it was past ten thirty. Just as we entered the gate, the heavens opened up as only can happen in a tropical country, It was not a downpour but more like a waterfall. The water blotted out everything else. Fortunately, the awning on top of the gate was enormous and covered quite a bit of area and after showing our pass to the sentry we stood under the shade waiting for the deluge to stop.

‘The rain had no intention of complying with our wishes and instead increased in ferocity. Great droplets crashed on the thick foliage outside the gate. Thunder rolled continuously. The awning under the gate stood like a small dry island in the middle of the deluge. The flood lights illuminated the raindrops and the leaves of the dense trees around the gate, giving them a strange phosphorescent appearance but could hardly penetrate the liquid wall. Returning to the ship through that waterfall was out of question. So, we decided to wait it out.

Time passed by but there was no let up. The guards behind their sand bags and machine guns lounged around and smoked. Three of us sat chatting and looking at the rain. It was getting close to midnight. I suddenly remembered the football in my hand and took it out of the bag. Both Bara Saab and Parkar were well advanced in years and so I started to juggle the ball with my feet, alone. I used to play a bit of football before I joined Merchant Navy and was an average left wing back. I have no doubt that I could have played for Mohun Bagan or at least Real Madrid if I received encouragement from my parents but that, alas, was not to be and the world lost a great wing back.

As had happened often before, I lost control of the ball while juggling and the ball shot off and landed in one of the sand bagged sentry post. I half expected the sentry to turn the gun, which looked more like a canon, towards me and let loose a couple of volleys but the sentry and apparently all other sentries were watching me while I was practicing my skills. Now he came out of his sand bagged castle and started juggling the ball himself. In no time the ball escaped his feet and landed inside the adjacent sand bagged citadel. 'I'here were two soldiers inside this one and they came out and started passing the ball to each other and to me. Ina short while all the soldiers had left their posts and a brisk game of passing the ball ensued. The rain continued unabated. Bara Saab and Parkar sat and watched the game from the top of a heap of sand bags perched on either side of a machine gun. As we got warmed up, a light came on from the first floor of the guard tower and everybody stopped in their tracks.

Heavy footsteps were heard coming down the stairs and shortly a short, bald and rotund man clad in heavy boots, camouflage shirts, an extremely brief brief and nothing else, stood glaring in front of us. The soldiers stood around sheepishly with the one holding the ball looking a little more sheepish than the rest. I stood in the middle of the camouflage clad gang in my red Tshirt and jeans looking a bit out of place. Bara Saab and Parkar didn’t budge from their high perch and looked down at us interestedly. Here, obviously, was the Big Boss. The glaring continued for some time and then a surprisingly thin tone rang out from the boss. I almost let out a laugh but, looking at the faces of my fellow players, thought better of it.

A volley of Spanish from the boss signalled the end of the glaring session. He kept up the blaring in that thin reedy voice of his as the nice paunch heaved to and from with each bellow. As laughter bubbled inside me, I wondered at the discipline of the soldiers and admired their restraint at not rolling around laughing. Slowly the tirade veered to the unfortunate chap holding the ball and he replied in Spanish and pointed towards me. The laughter inside me died a premature death.

As he came up towards me, Bara Saab and Parkar left their perch and came running. The boss gave me a piercing look and let loose a volley of Spanish. It was my turn to look sheepish. When I thought that I might get shredded to pieces by the boss’s bare hands, a huge grin split his face. He bounded across to the soldier holding the ball, plucked it out and shot it up in the air. There was a roar of approval as the hovering dark clouds lifted from our shoulders. Within minutes we were divided into two teams and even Bara Saab and Parkar were forced to join under jovial threats with one of the soldiers pointing his bayonet at Bara Saab’s nether region. Somebody brought out a crate of beer from the store room. The football battle raged with generous gulps of beer to revive any flagging spirits. The rain .... well, just kept on raining. The strange sight of a bunch of Guatemalan soldiers in fatigues, three Indians of varying ages in civvies and one rotund, half naked man bouncing energetically around with a football with a can of beer in one hand, under floodlights well past midnight in Guatemala, while a tropical depression vented all its fury on the jungle around, is one that will remained etched in my memory till my last breath.

‘Jolly chaps, those’, drawled Bara Saab as he wobbled slightly and limped up the gangway. Some energetic soul had kicked him on the sheen with heavy steel toed boots in the heat of the battle but the wobble might have been induced by the refreshments provided.

chapters.title: Where Humans Dare
paragraphs: The striped form stretched itself a grassy knoll. The great banyan tree under which she lay panting spread a soothing blanket of shadow in the summer heat. Towards the right, the grassy knoll disappeared into the thicket of Hental, Baain and Goran trees. The mangroves shot out their roots (pneumatophores) into the air in a desperate bid to breathe through the mud and saline water. The mud-covered bank disappearing into the Chora Gazi creek, resembled a deadly pin cushion. A crocodile surfaced briefly from the muddy waters, felt the heat and dived back into its cold domain. The breeze had stopped. The jungle was quiet. Only a Greater Flameback Wood pecker kept up its drumming on a nearby tree trunk accentuating the stillness. The creeks meandering into the heart of the forest showed the steep mud banks since the water had receded with the low tide. The fronds of the mangrove covered the creeks like a mother cradling her baby.

She lolled her tongue and panted. Her last hunt had ended in a failure. Prey was getting scarce. The dense mangrove hindered her charge and the herd of Chital Deer had no problems in eluding her ambush. She had not eaten for a few days and was hungry. Moreover, the midday sun shone mercilessly. The wind blowing in from the Bay of Bengal had stopped. Perhaps a storm was brewing. ‘Ihe place was surrounded by water on all sides but the water is saline. There was no drinking water except for the few sweet water ponds dug in the forest. Her throat felt parched. All her prey were probably miles away and she will have to ford quite a few rivers and creeks to have her next meal. She felt dizzy.

Moreover, the mating season had ended and little paws were kicking inside her womb. She was young and could not understand what it was all about except that her discomfort increased. Earlier she could have crossed a few rivers and picked up a nice plump cow from one of the fields but the two-legged animals had put up a fence and her route was hampered. Only a drink of cold water could alleviate her pain and distress before she could start out on her quest for prey. The nearest sweet water was about a few miles upstream along the Chora Gazi creek. She would have to cross a few mud flats and channels but that was the only option. She left the shade of the banyan tree and swung towards the Chora Gazi water hole.

Young Aminul Haq was at his wits end. His young wife had given birth to a bonny lass and he was very proud of her. However, as the young one grew, she showed signs of epilepsy and the nearest doctor in Goshaba town about 15 km away by boat, had promised to heal her. The medicines were costly. His small patch of land hardly bore any crops. The super typhoon Aayla had breached the mud embankment and salt water had entered his small paddy field. The land now lay fallow and barren like a widow. A small pond had fish just to sustain him, his wife and widow mother. They were honey collectors for generations.
His father had entered the forest to collect honey when he was a kid and never came back. His mother never let him venture into the forest since then. However, these were desperate times.

The village of Jamespur had a few huts of honey collectors. Aminul knew that they were going into the forest for the last honey collection drive of the season. When this collection was over there would not be another one for a few months.

He went to Jibon Haldar, the head of the honey collection team and asked his permission to join the group. The old stringy man with a flowing white beard wearing a loincloth knew Aminul's father well. He was apprehensive about taking the young man back into the place from which his father never returned. However, Aminul's daughter's plight moved the old man and finally he relented and gave his approval.

Aminul bade farewell to his small family. Apprehension jostled with expectancy and fear. His father had not returned before from a honey collection trip. One good haul of honey and he could find medicines for his daughter. The dark alleyways made by the creeks and the overhanging mangrove always fascinated and frightened him at the same time. He did not know what to think. His young wife was from a village which was at a distance from the Sunderbans and did know much about the forest. Only his mother, who grew up among the mud and typhoons... the striped terror that stalked the land... who lost her husband and brought up her lad in the grinding poverty... knew.

They started early in the morning. The fronds of the coconut trees and the arms of the small families swayed and bid adieu to the small expedition team. Three small wooden boats were pulled by paddles.
The boats were vintage and so were most of the men. Aminul looked around in wonder. He had seen the forest since childhood. Today he was going to join the ranks of men.

This was what his forefathers had done. When the Dutch came and set up their slave trading centres in the forest, one of Aminul's forefathers had pursued a bee into the thickets in pursuit of the golden harvest. When Robert Clive sailed up these waters with the fleet of the East India Company, his forefathers had watched in amazement from these boats. His father had seen the last British vessel sail down these waters as the sun set on the Empire. Aminul today would join their ranks. He knew that they were watching him and certainly were in approval. The rickety looking but amazingly sturdy boats were self-contained. Cans of water were stored in the stern. A small kerosene stove stood in all its ancient glory and cans of kerosene both for smoking out the bees and cooking the next meal were lashed to one side. Empty cans were ready to be filled up with the golden liquid.

The temple of Bonobibi and Dakshin Ray lay on a high embankment on the way. It was fenced off by nylon ropes from the forest. Bonobibi was the goddess of the forest. Dakshin Ray represented the terror of the stripes. She had decreed that anybody entering her domain without weapons would be under her protection. Dakshin Ray would not be able to harm them. The woodcutters entered with blunt axes to obey her decree. She had protected them, sometimes.

As the group sat in silent prayer in front of the thatched, broken down makeshift temple and lighted their perfume sticks, Aminul looked around at the grim faces of the men. Jibon Haider sat in supplication, his eyes closed. Binoy, Mohon, Tarak, Shamsul all were deep in meditation. Even the normally reticent Madan Sardar, the man who they said, could sniff out Dakshin Ray, sat in a trance. The Hindus and Muslims prayed together in a corner of 21st century India, united by a common prayer; a prayer to spare their lives. When the prayers were said and perfume sticks lit, the boats set out once again. Their bows pointed towards the creek adjacent to Chora Gazi where the honey bees were spotted. seer She crossed the creeks and climbed up and down the mud banks. The pin sharp aerial shoots made her progress difficult. So, she left the bank and climbed into the relatively drier part of the forest. By the time the Chora Cob pond came into view, her throat felt like bursting. Hunger and fatigue made her sleepy. She managed to reach the pond. Where was the pond? A familiar net of ropes met her on the bank of the pond. Unknown to her, those blighted two legged animals had put up the fence here in the middle of her territory (unknown to her it was done by the forest department to clean the pond). She could see the cool sweet water shaded by trees. She could almost smell the fresh water.

Her body and the smaller ones in her womb yearned for the lifegiving fluid but those infernal ropes would not let her near the water. She started circling the net looking for a chink. There were none. She tried to bite through but those massive canines were useless against man's invention. The nylon strands of the rope kept slipping in between her teeth.

Fatigue overcame her. She plodded towards the narrow creek and flopped down on the mud in the shade of some Gewa trees. She closed her eyes and started dreaming about clouds and rain when the drops from heaven would crash down on the forest and she would just stick out her rough tongue and all her troubles would be over.
Dakshin Ray probably heard her dreams. The southern corner of the sky became slightly darker.

Aminul and his team reached Chora Ghazi creek in the afternoon. The three boats turned into the narrow creek and made a landing in the slimy mud. The boats were tied to the bank. The men disembarked with their implements for collecting honey - dry cloth, jerrycans, sticks, kerosene. Only Aminul was asked to stay back. Somebody had to watch over the boat and cook a meal. The boat would be much safer for him anyway because of his inexperience.

Aminul sat back. As the men disappeared, he knew his time would also come. When they were all gone, he sat up inside the boat and looked around. ‘Ihe sky had grown quite dark. Rain clouds floated in from the sea and almost touched the trees. The muddy gullies looked darker and muddier than ever. Not a leaf stirred. The heat simmered and rose in vapours from the slime. The salt smell of the creek water mixed with the dank smell of the rotting vegetation. A White Bellied Sea Eagle flew swiftly overhead as if it was in a hurry to reach home. Even the ripples in the water had died down. Aminul sat and sweated.

The minutes ticked by and the young man felt drowsy as if drunk with the heat. He leaned against the side of the boat and closed his eyes. As the salty sweat trickled down his forehead, his thoughts drifted to the little girl he had left back home. She was coming running towards him, her tresses flowing in the wind.

The wind had picked up. All of a sudden, her locks went flying as the wind howled. The coconut tree in front of his house crashed with a large crack. He jerked awake from his slumber and looked up to the storm as it hit the top of the trees above the boat and made a sound like the death cry of a thousand witches. Then the massive paws and jaws severed the dreamy head. Aminul had joined the ranks of his ancestors who had entered the forest. see That night, the honey collectors hastily pulled their boat away from the spot of terror. The rain poured and poured in the forest. In the ancient tradition of this forest, the blood-soaked vest of Aminul drooped limply, tied from the Gewa tree below which he had dreamt about a little girt for the last time.

My guide Tarun finished his tale, stretched and looked at his watch. It was past midnight. Sanjeet - my photographer friend - and I had finished our dinner and sat listening to the tales as told by Tarun. Our boat had anchored in the middle of the river a little down the river from Sajnekhali forest office after crisscrossing the myriad waterways from six in the morning. The forest was about 200 meters away, dark and menacing. The small solar lights in the village in the distance did nothing to disperse the darkness. Starlight and a quarter moon had lit the night sky and the waves rose and fell in small ripples dark enough to make us hallucinate of heads of the striped terror swimming stealthily at our boat so much so that Sanjeet repeatedly flashed his heavy-duty flashlight to pick out the glaring eyes. Only the sound of the ripples against the boat replied back.

Man-eating Tigers... giant crocodiles... danger on land and danger in the water... honey collectors and fishermen risking their lives everyday... sticky, smelly and salty mud... reeking vegetation... intense stupor inducing humid heat... inaccessible forests penetrated by narrow and dangerous waterways... and as wild a place as you can imagine.
That’s Sunderbans.

chapters.title: Hoolongapar Gibbon Wildlife Sanctuary
paragraphs: I never drink when in field especially in elephant territory. This night was an exception. After a day’s successful hunt with the lens we were offered Rice Beer. Rice Beer is a local brew throughout Assam and though not something to write home about, this was different. This particular strain of paddy from which this brew was made was grown in a small patch of land adjacent to Diganta’s home. This brew was mixed with more than forty-two types of various herbs handpicked from the forest and the resultant elixir just numbed the senses (not literally since it has to be consumed in quite some quantity to achieve the state of nirvana). Diganta’s cook conjured up a dish of dry pork with garlic and a dash of chillies.

It was the month of October and the rains had not totally retreated. Night had fallen. Sougata and I reclined comfortably in the sofas in the sitting room of our home stay. The vast paddy fields starting from beneath our window was dark. Darkness stretched to the horizon. A late evening shower had stopped by then. Frogs croaked in unison urging on their mates while water droplets from the surrounding trees on the roof completed the sound effects. The monsoon gods threw flashes across the cloud laden sky from time to time to liven up things as the distant bamboo fronds got illuminated against the sky. Thunder rumbled like the ocean breaking the waves on a boulder strewn seafront.

We had hardly taken a couple of sips when all of a sudden there were excited voices outside the door. A group of youth rushed in and informed Diganta that a herd of elephants were raiding the paddy fields nearby. We picked up our torches and followed them into the night.

Soon we left the road and entered a narrow unpaved pathway that led through village huts and small patches of paddy and vegetables. The village did not have electricity. Light from the oil lamps in the huts spilled onto the path. Small rivulets gurgled down the sides, where children, drenched in water and mud in equal measure, caught small fish with strips of cloth. We hurried down the path and soon came upon a large field. Torches made from sticks wrapped in kerosene-soaked cloths were stuck in the branches of trees surrounding the field throwing a ghostly flickering light all around. The jungle surrounded the field on three sides and a paddy field was on the fourth. Shadows were darting around in the half-darkness shouting and brandishing sticks. A couple of crackers went off somewhere.

Since we were trying to do a video shoot of the place, Sougata stood me beside a torch tied to a tree for a descriptive dialogue of the goings on. With the pandemonium going on all around, my tongue got stuck to my throat and I made a thorough hash of it. When we reached the paddy field, the elephants had departed leaving behind patches of destroyed crops and some dung. Forest Department jeeps streamed in but the jumbos had already shown a clean pair of heels by that time.

Hoolongapar Gibbon Wildlife Sanctuary takes its name from the Hoolong tree and is one of the smallest sanctuaries covering an area of about 21 sq. km. Within these narrow confines reside some of the most astonishing fauna of the sub-continent. It is not the elephants or leopards that attracted me to this evergreen forest. This sanctuary is home to Western Hoolock Gibbons, Capped Langurs, Stump-tailed macaques. Pig-tailed macaques, Assam macaques, Bengal Slow Loris and Tokay Geckos in adequate numbers.

This is one of the very few sanctuaries where there are no vehicles. Even Bharatpur has rickshaws and horse drawn carts but here you have to walk. Walking in a dense evergreen forest with an armed guard escort has a charm which is seldom enjoyed when you are going after a tiger or rhino or elephant in a Gypsy. You are dwarfed by the massive trees on both sides of the pathways sometimes blotting out the sun. There is always the chance of meeting elephants but the armed guard keeps you safe. If you have the stomach for it, you can go for a night walk in the outskirts of the forest when the chances of seeing a Bengal Slow Loris is highest though we did not meet any even after repeated tries. What we met were the elusive and highly endangered Tokay Gecko. However, the heart-warming sight was a manmade iron structure over the railway line which runs through the jungle.

When the railway line was planned and built, nobody spared a thought about the denizens of the forest. Now, Gibbons are primarily arboreal and are not able to walk on the ground let alone traversing railway lines. They are immensely acrobatic in the forest canopy but depends solely on the intertwining branches of the trees for moving from one place to another. The railway line divided not only the forest but the Gibbon families as well. They could not cross from one side to the other. This led to inbreeding and the resultant deformities. Someone somewhere came to realise this.

This realisation led to the construction of an iron arch constructed across the railway line. When we visited, the arch was being given the finishing touches. Hopefully the Gibbons across the LOC would realize the potential of this arch and start using the arch to traverse the full length of the jungle using this arch and the families that were separated by a man-made construction would find another man-made construction to reunite. A forest North Korea finally has a chance to merge with a forest South Korea.

And then finally we went for our last walk in the jungle. We had walked the full length of the forest trail. The Gibbons had made their appearance, Capped Langurs had lazed on the branches, a Malayan Giant Squirrels had nibbled at ripe figs above us and Pig-tailed Macaques had done their frolicking on the branches above us. The weather was a bit sultry but the sweat felt good from the satisfaction of watching some of the most elusive animals on the planet. Bird life was prolific with a distant sight of the elusive Red-headed Trogon. Photography was difficult because of the high and dense canopy but when you sight White- browed Piculets, Dollarbirds, Emerald Cuckoos in their natural habitat, you do not complain.

The forest was alive with the chatter of birds returning to roost. Light was fading fast. The trees stood motionless crowding us from all around in the stillness. Frangipani had started blooming with the onset of the night and the sweet heady smell permeated the air. Some trees had already started shedding their leaves and they fell to the forest floor with loud plops. Parakeets streaked across the darkening sky like little green arrows. Jackals had started their haunting calls from the depths of the interspersed glades. Serenity pervaded everything when suddenly our guide stopped and pointed to a tree across a small water body towards a jamun tree. Looking carefully, we saw a group of eight Stumptailed Macaques perched on the branches. The Stump-tailed and Assam Macaques had eluded us till then.

As we watched them, the alpha male climbed down to a lower branch next to a female and sat down regally reclining on the main trunk. Soon the lady started flirting around. The male set an uninterested face but kept giving furtive glances. Then he could stand the advances no longer and the process started. The light was too low and they kept a branch of the tree in between them and me for modesty’s sake. As we watched on, suddenly a bush just below them came alive and a spotted paw shot up. By some miracle, the lovers jumped at once and the paw missed by a hair’s breadth. The rest of the group had started being vocal but I thought they had been a bit lax in their duties as watchmen. I mean, when the leader is busy with important duties, it is the duty of the foot soldiers to watch his back but they had failed miserably in their duties. The leopard did not follow up in its attack.... maybe it was not hungry enough or may be the monkeys had escaped to narrow branches which would not support its weight.

Gibbon Wildlife Sanctuary is not for the tiger specific visitor. It is for people who love offbeat destinations and cherish the biodiversity of the planet. It is for the exquisite species, the feel of a walk in an evergreen forest, the sound of dew drops falling on the leaves at night while you look for Slow Loris, savour a local brew in the evening which could give Drambuie a run for its money, where you can fill your senses to the brim with the hooting call of the Hoolock Gibbons in the morning, where you can see man trying to somewhat reverse the process of devastation he has created by constructing a pathway for the Gibbons to cross forests.

chapters.title: Fambangloh (Phambangloh) Wildlife Sanctuary: Hidden Treasure in the Eastern Himalayas
paragraphs: The road snaked up and up but it was a road.... a metalled road. Subhash, Dev and myself trudged up but it was easy going, not one of the Himalayan pathways which only the Paharis and lizards were capable of climbing. I was wondering when the difficult part would arrive as we walked past sleepy hamlets with children playing and dogs barking. The road finally arrived and lived up to my expectations. As I was admiring a lovely little wooden house in a village, Subhash veered off the road and clambered up a steep slope and looked down at me... “here goes”, I said to myself and with a sigh, left the metalled road.

A few hundred feet up the slope and the path that we were following all but vanished. The sun beat down mercilessly and we climbed and climbed. Somewhere in the undergrowth a man appeared from nowhere. Apparently, Subhash and Dev knew him and they started a conversation. Since I did not understand the dialect, I took the opportunity to lie down under a tree to catch my breath and listen to them.

This man, though wobbling under the influence of something stronger than water, flayed his hands and tried to convince my guides of something while repeatedly pointing in a direction in which the only portion visible was a dense undergrowth and an extremely uphill portion of the slope upon which we had halted.

Thad a suspicion that Subhash would take this route and sure enough, in a short while he informed me that this was the shorter route. I wondered whether it was a shorter route to my next life but thought better of expressing my feelings. Apparently if we did not take this mad route then a hike of at least 2 km more would ensue.

Led by our new lean but wobbly man, we, or rather I clambered up that sheer cliff. Subhash and Dev pirouetted from rock to rock and our Man Friday too wobbled confidently up. What hurt most was his grin whenever I flopped down drenched in sweat and totally out of breath.

This carried on and I bravely kept climbing and after sometime the land slowed down from going direct towards the heavens to a more leisurely angle. Though I had my camera, taking images was far from my mind at that time. As the slope petered out and I caught my breath and looked around, the vastness of the East Himalayan vista soaked in. The path wound across a small valley. Old trees towered around and thrust their proud heads in the cloudless sky. High mountains surrounded us on all sides. These were not the snow-capped peaks but were high enough to shut out the mighty Kanchenjunga range.
