There was no breeze and despite being inside the Himalayas, the heat simmered even in the month of March. Cicadas droned monotonously as we walked on.

Some distance covered with relative and the climb started in earnest again. There came a point where I could not advance any further without risking my camera. The undergrowth was extremely thick and climb too steep for me to hold on. When I stopped and looked around, there flitted around me from bush to bush some of the feathered beauties I had only seen in pictures and dreamt about. Various Fulvettas, Barwings, Mynas foraged in the dense undergrowth. Imagine my disappointment at being unable to capture a single one of these beauties. I just managed to remain perched on a rock and trying to twist myself at impossible angles as I tried to capture a green feather ball here or a brown feather ball there. The worst culprits were a flock of Hoary-throated Barwings who kept popping in and out of a bush which remained just out of reach of my lens courtesy my perch and my belly which refused to turn 180 degrees. I could see them taunting my clumsiness but unable to do anything about it. However, an hour of sitting on the boulder gave me time and opportunity to see some of the most elusive birds of the Eastern Himalayas and like any other wildlife photographer who has failed before me, I too started my descent with the thought - image is not everything, the experience was worth more.

Phambangloh will not be complete without the mention of the visit to Subhash’s house in hamlet at the edge of the sanctuary. It was one of the most picturesque huts I have seen. No luxury... no frills but you sit in the open balcony overlooking the valley and breathe in the pine scented pure air and all other luxuries will seem irrelevant. There was a coral tree down a steep slope and I made it there since this is one tree, which, when in bloom, attracts birds from far and near. ‘he previous evening, | was chatting with Subhash and Dev and grandly told them that Chhang (the local brew) was tepid from an alcoholic point of view, though delightful to drink. I had drunk the millet variety and had found it to be very light but refreshing on a previous trip. Subhash and Dev had given me vague smile in answer.

I clambered down the slope to a vantage point from where I could shoot the visitors to the tree from an eye level. I sat there for hours and hours mesmerised at the beauty of the surroundings. The valley beneath was illuminated with the sun’s rays. Clouds would come rolling up from time to time and gave me the feeling that I was sitting on them. The peace of the mountains seeped into the bones and I felt drowsy. Mid-day came and Subhash came down from his hamlet carrying a small lunch box and a bag. Sitting on the edge of the gorge, I gorged on the hot Maggi noodles. When I had finished, Subhash fished out a coke bottle from the bag like a magician conjuring up a rabbit from a hat. He thrust it into my hands and stood there with grin plastered on his face. The bottle did not hold any coke but was full of a milky liquid.
“Chhang”, he smiled. “Made in my home... since you are so fond of it and you don’t get drunk, I have brought it especially for you”.

I made an exception to my rule never to drink when in the field and opened the bottle and took a swig. It was made of rice and not of millet, the variety | was familiar with. ‘his is not to say that it was in anyway inferior and I finished the bottle in no time.

That evening when I returned to my base in the village of Martham, I had the pleasure of seeing two baby elephants fighting on the coral tree, the mountains somehow became hillocks and saw a drongo in a peculiar shade of green. I am not sure but I think I saw a Unicorn eating an ice-cream at the village carpenter’s shop. The Chhang was delightful.

chapters.title: Rhino and the Jumping Lady
paragraphs: It was stunning to see an old lady wearing a saree, washing plates on the ground to jump a couple of feet from the ground from a sitting position, onto a balcony while an irate rhino was charging by.

I was in the outskirts of Pobitora Wildlife Sanctuary, setting up a bird observation station just after the monsoon. Pobitora has a surprising variety of resident woodland birds apart from the hordes of migratory ones which visit every year during winter. Apart from the fairly common barbets, parakeets, Whitebreasted Waterhens, Lesser Adjutant Storks, various woodpeckers and raptors, we also have the exquisitely beautiful Scarlet-backed Flowerpeckers, the exotic Oriental Pied Hornbills, the rare Great Mynas and many more as residents. It was my primary objective to raise awareness about the avian diversity present in this beautiful little sanctuary among the people visiting this place.

Pobitora is surrounded on all sides by villages and Guwahati is just about an hour and a half driving distance. It is more famous as the place with the highest density of wild rhinos on the planet and they hog the limelight. There is a healthy population of Wild Buffaloes too but the rhinos are the species of eminence. For years they are thriving in the wonderful grasslands that dot the small sanctuary and the Forest Department have done a commendable job protecting them from poachers with the help of a willing section of the locals who proactively keep the sanctuary clean in spite of the immense pressure from picnickers, cattle grazers and some fishermen.

The previous night was a full moon one and I had lazed out in the open field, soaking in the bright moonlight which was more intense because of the clear night after the monsoon clouds had blown away. Dazed by the sound of crickets and the light breeze, I stayed awake later than that was prudent.

My friend Sougata was fast asleep when I woke at eight in the morning. The sun was already up and about but waking Sougata is a difficult proposition after a late night. I left him snoring under the whirring fan and made my way out to have a cup of tea with my friend Homen in the lawn under the shade of a tree of our resort. A high road runs right next to the lawn and skirts a part of the sanctuary.

The sun beat down and the shade of the tree was extremely pleasant and I had a couple of sips when suddenly pandemonium broke loose. A crowd of people started running along the road towards the adjoining field which borders the sanctuary, shouting at the top of their voices. A Forest Department Gypsy screamed across and came to a screeching halt nearby and guards armed with rifles spouted from every nook and corner brandishing them threateningly. I dropped my cup onto the ground and Homen, who was sitting on a branch, promptly fell down. Everybody was shouting “rhino rhino”.

Homen ran towards the crowd while I ran into our room and rudely woke up my snoring friend with nice bit of shaking and told him to grab the camera and follow me. To his credit, Sougata did not ask why or when and shot out of the bed, grabbed his camera and scooted behind me.

When we reached the guards and the crowd, the rhino was already on the road. He had been foraging in the paddy fields on the other side of the road which was outside the boundary of the sanctuary and perhaps dallied a bit too long and failed to notice that it was already late. Now the road and the field lay between him and the safety of the sanctuary. A few cars, cycles and motorbikes were already plying on the road. He threw caution to the winds and crossed the road right in front of us and the crowd. Sougata threw himself on the ground and managed to get a shot before the rhino descended onto the field.

Now this field was once a swampy lowland but over the years people had bought the land and built walls and houses scattered all around. The rhino, already furious because of the shouting from the crowd now found it difficult to navigate around the pillars. A cow was peacefully grazing and the rhino found that hard to digest. He charged the cow and it ran for its life. After wandering around for a while, the rhino found the narrow road which runs by the side of the river across which the sanctuary started. That river is easily forded by rhinos and we heaved a collective sigh of relief and returned to our tea.

Soon I was standing next to the gate of our resort having a fresh cup of tea. This area is surrounded by village homes and bamboo groves. Suddenly somebody fired a rifle shot from next the wall of the gate a few paces away. The cup left my hand along with the tea as I jumped a few feet off the ground. On landing on terra firma, I stepped outside the gate only to see the rhino break cover from the adjoining bamboo grove a few feet away. He crossed the narrow village road in front of me. Opposite is Homen’s house and an old lady was sitting in the courtyard cleaning the dinner plates from the previous night. Having heard the gunshot behind her she turned around while in a sitting position only to see the fence of the house being uprooted by the horn of an extremely agitated charging rhino. In the blink of an eye the old lady jumped from that awkward position onto Homen’s balcony while the rhino veered and thundered across the courtyard and into the thicket behind Homen’s house followed by a horde of Forest Department personnel.
A few hours later we happily learned that the staff had successfully guided the rhino back into the sanctuary and peace again reigned in our little village.

chapters.title: Kaziranga
paragraphs: “Rhinos yes.... elephants yes.... Hog Deer yes... even probably a Bengal Florican.... Tigers NO”, I said emphatically.

Ujjalda gave a sigh of resignation. He had just bought a brandnew Sony for his first sojourn to Kaziranga National Park or for that matter, any National Park and he was hoping to start his shooting spree with the striped cat. I bunged a spanner in his hopes.

Anyway, he was not going to cancel a trip to the World Heritage Site just because a tiger shot was almost out of question. I have had numerous trips to the fabled land but never got a whiff of even their tail even though they are there.... in large numbers. The terrain of Kaziranga is not conducive to the sighting of a tiger because the tall Elephant Grass can swallow up even their namesake - the elephant. Tigers in Kaziranga by nature are extremely shy and nocturnal and hence the sightings are minimal unlike the ramp walkers of Ranthambore, Kanha or Tadoba.

Ujjalda and I had company. Sanjeet Mangat, one of the most eminent wildlife photographers of India. We had toured Kazi (as I fondly call Kaziranga) previously without the benefit of a sighting of tiger but he too was not to be deterred. His ambition was to photograph at least one tiger from each National Park of India and Kazi still eluded him.

So, we landed up one cold December afternoon in Kaziranga. Soon the sun set across the sand banks of Brahmaputra. The sand grew cold fast and wisps of mist rose in the air with that smell of raw earth so typical of winter in Kaziranga. Our resort/camp was situated right adjacent to the sand banks with the jungle starting hardly hundred metres away. The cold set in and Rambabu, our man for all purposes, got a roaring log fire going with some marinated chicken by the side. The stars came out one by one and the wood smoke mingled with the smell of the roasting chicken as we discussed the stubbornness of tigers of Kazi not showing up. Gautam my friend / owner / wildlife freak and a thoroughly crazy man listened to our sighs and self-pity about the time we missed the tiger but saw the pug marks or heard the alarm calls from Hog Deer but nobody showed up. He just listened.

Next morning safari threw up the usual shots... some good... some bad.... some horrible. Afternoon safari was about to start but with a difference. Gautam was at the wheels. Now, if anybody has driven with Gautam in a safari will forever remember the experience. His usual method was to drive keeping his right foot on the accelerator while he attended to his mobile calls, sometimes holding the steering wheel, looking up at the tree tops for birds and quite often looking behind, talking to me. All the while the Gypsy seemed to drive itself by sheer psychological control. Schumacher might have taken a correspondence course from Gautam in the art of blind driving.

At the gate we picked up the best Forest Guard — Debeshwar. Then we kept driving without stopping to look at the Swamp Deer or the elephant herds. We drove past the tree where the Pallas’s Fish Eagle was building its nest and down past the water body where the otters rested. We did not ask Gautam where he was heading or what he was up to since in addition to being crazy, he does not like being questioned. Only Ujjalda was getting excited at missing all the wildlife he was failing to capture but he too was kept subdued.

At one point the forest road takes a sharp right turn and leads on toa large water body surrounded by tall grass on the right-hand side. On the left the forest was dense. When we reached a particular clearing, Gautam stopped the Gypsy and switched off the ignition. “What happened? Why did you stop?”, this time I asked.
“Something might happen here”, replied Gautam and leaned comfortably back in his driver’s seat.

I looked around. It was past three thirty and even the December sun was beating down harshly. The water body simmered in the lazy heat. A herd of Hog Deer grazed peacefully around the water. Grey-headed Fish Eagle circled overhead looking for a late lunch. A Golden-fronted Leafbird landed on a berry tree nearby with its flashy colours. Jewel bugs hummed in the bushes around with their brilliantly coloured green wings. Everything was peaceful and quiet.

I looked at Sanjeet and he looked back at me and rolled his eyes. Ujjalda had a puzzled look on his face and Debeshwar busied himself looking for a Brown Fish Owl which usually perches on a tree on the left. Time went by. Sanjeet pulled his classy hat over his eyes, gave a sigh and putting his legs up on the seat went off to sleep. [looked at my watch and it was past four. Ujjalda sat in the middle fiddling with his camera. In the winter heat I too soon nodded off.

Click click click click click... the shutter went off next to my ear like a stuttering machine gun and brought me back to this world. Startled I looked towards my left and there was Ujjalda breathing strenuously and holding his camera to his eyes. His index finger seemed like it had got glued to his camera shutter. He sensed that I was looking at him in bewilderment.

“Tiger... tiger”, he croaked hoarsely without taking his eye or finger off the camera. His lens was pointed towards the water body. Giving him a dismissive laugh, I looked towards the direction of his lens and the biggest tiger I had ever seen was right in the middle.

Picking up my camera I shook and woke up Sanjeet. The frenzy on the Gypsy at that moment would have resembled the German garrisons guarding Normandy in World War II when they sighted the Allied Armada approaching. Sanjeet groped for his massive lens frantically. My hand was shaking so badly that I could not find the shutter button. Ujjalda was trying shoot between Sanjeet’s flailing hands and mine. Gautam was groping for his bean bag to place his camera. Only Debeshwar maintained his composure.

Kazi tigers when they decide to show up generally give you three and a half seconds before vanishing but this horse of a tiger crossed the water body, went to the other side, came back to the middle and paused and then finally disappeared across the middle, giving us about three minutes. Since the washed-out light was from the wrong side and the distance between us was quite great even for large lenses, the images that came out were somewhat lacking in punch but what the heck.... a diamond is a diamond and a Kazi tiger is a Kazi tiger.

By the time we were on the way back the sun had almost set and we felt like we were high on some drug.... a tiger and on top of that a Kazi tiger sighting has that effect on you. As we were passing by a large canal-like water channel, suddenly an elephant trumpeted in alarm from the grassland on the opposite side and Gautam almost stood on the brakes. Then there was absolute quiet. Nothing moved.... nothing stirred. Gautam asked us to focus on a small barren patch of land on the edge of the water. The light was already gone. The tall grass had swallowed up the elephant. Nothing else was in sight. There wasn’t even a ripple in the calm waters of the canal. ...and then the head popped out at exactly the spot Gautam had pointed, stayed for about three and a half seconds, our camera shutters went berserk and then the stripes vanished. My camera showed an orange smudge, Ujjalda’s showed nothing but Sanjeet had a couple of shots which identified it as a young tigress.

Rarely has a roasted chicken or a thick slab of Rohu fish with lai saag cooked in wood fire tasted so delicious as the one we had in the evening. India’s National Animal had rarely shown themselves twice within a span of twenty minutes in Kazi. Since that first time after so many failed attempts, tiger sighting in Kaziranga has almost become a habit for me but the thrill of the first meet is always sweeter than the rest. Kaziranga is paradise indeed.

Just as a footnote, we went in for the next safari in the morning and there were about twenty Gypsys lined up near the first tiger sighting point. On enquiring one of the tourists told us somewhat patronisingly, “this is the place where two tigers were sighted yesterday by somebody”. is)

It was quite drizzly. Sitting in camp in Kaziranga, I watched the rain blowing across the treeline of the forest, sweeping across the open field in front and getting blown across the banks of the Brahmaputra. The camp was quite empty since we did not have too many tourists in late April. Only some tourists were coming down from Arunachal Pradesh and I awaited their arrival sipping my hot chai liberally laced with mint leaves and watching the faded horizon through the veil of raindrops. My camp is somewhat isolated being situated on the banks of the Brahmaputra just across the core area of Kaziranga Eastern Range or Agoratoli Range. There is no Wi-Fi or geysers but of pure wilderness, there is aplenty with elephants regularly passing by and Hog Deer regularly visiting, sometimes followed by the striped cat.

As I sipped my cuppa two headlights became visible through the rain in the distance. A few minutes later two Enfield Himalayas chugged into the camp through the rain. The riders were aliens.... or so they seemed. It is not every day you see people decked up in Motorbike rally gear wearing high boots and Grand Prix jackets with helmets like MIG pilots riding across the banks of the Brahmaputra. Then They took off their helmets and revealed an Indian and a blonde man. Shahwar Hussain and Ian Neubauer were travelling across Northeast testing the Enfield Himalaya. Shahwar was from Assam, settled in Delhi but settling is possibly not the right term for a person running a motorbike tour company to extremely remote parts. Ian was from Australia and an ex freelance reporter who had taken up travel writing for a living.

The following day Ian and I had a frank conversation about the problems of Kaziranga. It was that time when BBC had brewed up a storm with their report on the Shoot at Sight orders in Kaziranga and I had rather strong views on the subject. Ian wanted to explore the matter further. I suggested he take a safari ride inside the park to help him better understand the topography of the region to which he agreed. ‘heir third partner Sanjeev Monga who rather resembled a bamboo pole decked with a lot of hair, had also arrived by that time riding his Enfield Bullet.

Next afternoon we entered the National Park in our open top Gypsy. Ian, Sanjeev and myself went inside while Shahwar nursed an injured leg (which he had picked up when he came in close contact with a small pile of cow-dung strategically placed on the road in the town) in the camp. Khogen, my trusted lieutenant and forest guard accompanied us. Kaziranga was her usual shape with elephants and wild buffalos loitering around and grazing. Hog Deer were having a gala time feeding on the tender grass which had grown after the first showers drenched the grass which was burnt by the forest department for that purpose. Eastern Swamp Deer kept their distance. However, the Rhinos for some inexplicable reason, stayed hidden.

As we were not on a photography safari, we did not take very long to cover our zone. The sun was almost nearing the horizon when we reached a particular trijunction. At this particular spot, the Gypsy track converges with two large animal tracks and if you park your vehicle at a particular spot you can stay almost hidden and watch the animals crossing to and from their watering points. We still had about half an hour left.

We waited and Ian promptly fell asleep. His gentle snoring mingled with the whispering of the wind in the grasslands. The sun set fire to the waters of the beel (large water body). A herd of elephants grazed placidly far away on the bed of the swamp. I woke Ian up and asked him whether we should leave. I explained to him that if we waited there was a chance that something might happen and then again nothing might happen and we might be waiting in vain. However, if he felt sleepy, we might as well leave.

Tan said that he had a head ache but would leave the decision to me. Sanjeev was good for any of the options. While all these whisperings were going on, I somehow sensed that the elephant herd were not so placid anymore. Turning around I saw them emerging from the swamp and turn towards the Bhoot Bangla (ghost bungalow). Bhoot Bangla is a derelict and defunct forest check post built on stilts adjacent to the water body. The name was given by me many years back since the floor of the room was strewn with bones of rodents which had been consumed by some resident owls. I had spent many hours happily sitting on the staircase, watching life in Kaziranga flow by. However, the path to bungalow was no longer accessible.

I decided to wait. Ian went back to sleep. The elephant herd was in no hurry. The adults tore up bunches of grass and chomped while the babies frolicked. There is a tree near the Bhoot Bangla which is dead and bereft of any leaves. After crossing the tree, the herd suddenly swerved towards us. It was then that I realized that they were about to take the elephant corridor next to where we were parked instead of taking their normal route which was further up. A prod in Tan’s backside from Sanjeev woke him up and by the time he turned around things were rapidly turning frantic.

The elephants now converged fast and sensed us. They broke up into two groups and one group came directly from behind us while the other came from our right flank. I told our driver not to move but being a rookie, he switched the engine on and off. Now the elephants rapidly closed in and I left my camera for my cell phone cam while whispering to everybody to keep very still. The elephants were so close now that that the big matriarch probably could reach out her trunk and take a swipe at me sitting in the back seat. They shielded their babies but I could make out that they were as much afraid as we were. They kept themselves bunched tightly together and kept on touching trunks as a gesture of reassuring each other. The rumbling from their lungs made the very earth tremble but it was not the piercing trumpeting that heralds a charge. We stay put...absolutely still...not daring to breathe... the driver probably stopped breathing altogether.

The stalemate lasted for about a year and a half but practical people would say that it was probably five minutes. As the herd sensed no threat from our side, they began to move off, down their well-trodden corridor and soon were lost to view. The breath which escaped Ian was probably of the same intensity as that of Churchill after the Battle of Britain. Sanjeev’s eyes tried hard to pop out of their sockets and I was over the moon since my emotions of relief fought a running battle with the feeling of elation that I was feeling on getting a short video clip of the elephants without any tell-tale shake of my hands which were certainly feeling quite detached from the rest of my body. The driver had to be resurrected by some thorough shaking.

We still had not got our rhino. When we arrived at the exit gate next to the road, the big male chewed the grass peacefully next to the forest camp. Though the light was already gone, the majestic beast was in all his glory with the big horn sticking out as a pride reminder that Kaziranga was a success story which we need desperately to emulate in other places.

A few days later after the biker gang had receded over the horizon, the rains started in earnest. Clouds bellowed over the Brahmaputra spewing water like a fountain and the river started rising in response. It was time to visit a waterfall.

Waterfall is not exactly what you associate with Kaziranga but there is one quite near at hand and only about 20 km from my camp but only approachable in either a Gypsy with a steady driver or a long hike on foot. I had the luxury of our Gypsy and my friend Hari was a better driver than me. My friend Anupam joined us.

Kakochang waterfalls is actually in Karbi Hills and the road leading to it meanders through beautiful Assam countryside resplendent with green paddy fields, betelnut plantations and the typical bungalow style houses but just as you reach the foothills you are transported away from the villages into verdant forest covered hills all around. If you are not on a Gypsy, you would have to probably leave your vehicle here and start walking.

We drove on and soon the road was nothing but a mud track. The rains had soaked the soil but yet to make the stretches impassable for a Gypsy. Trundling on, we found torrent after raging torrent barring our way. The rains had swelled the flowing water and were dashing over the smoothened boulders. The Gypsy took them all in her stride though a couple of times it became rather scary but we clung on and in about 45 minutes we cleared the last torrent where the track too ended.

From here on the walking track led up the hill in a rather steep incline where both Anupam and I huffed and puffed due to the added luggage of our tummies but Hari almost breezed upwards. The path was not too long and as we gained altitude the roar of the waterfall became quite audible at first and then overwhelming as we reached the crest.

On the crest stood a small thatched hut where a couple of the local village lads were selling much needed water, the usual Lays packets, some vegetables and a few bunches of bananas. The hut overlooked a 360 degrees panorama of the surrounding hills and there below us...way below us...foamed Kakochang waterfalls. The extremely narrow and steep path leading down to the waterfall, under usual circumstances, would not pose much of a problem even to a geriatric but the rains had turned it into quite a challenge. We negotiated that path, if it could be called that, with extreme caution and finally stood under the waterfall... the effort was worth it.

Water foamed and frothed from a height of about 200 feet at a rough guess and fell like an avalanche in front of us. Three sides were blocked by steep and heavily forested hillside while the flow of water rushed out towards the plains through the remaining side. Even a slight breeze would blow the foaming water into myriads of rainbows till you felt like sitting in a rainbow coloured hall. The water was clear and as I plunged in; all the aches of the short trek vanished in a jiffy. The only thing that marred the beauty were the ubiquitous Lays and Uncle Chips Packets and empty plastic mineral water bottles and even a few empty whiskey and rum bottles.

Trudging back, up the path was much easier and soon we were back at the shack on the crest of the hill with our soaked clothes and we sat on the wooden bench outside the shack to not only catch our breath but also to catch the sunset over the hills. Though I am not too fond of bananas, the last remaining bunch caught my fancy. I tasted one and soon polished off the bunch... rarely have I tasted bananas tastier than those which did not have hint of pesticide of any kind. Hari and Anupam helped me out though. As the sun set across the darkening hills, a tiny bird came and sat just below us and looked suspiciously like the Cachar Wedge-billed Babbler which I consider to be one of the Unicorns of the Indian Birds, being so rare but I could not be sure in the rapidly fading light.

Soon the light vanished as we drove back. The thatched hut owners returned to their villages. No more human footprints would be heard till the next sunrise. ‘he night belonged to the wild elephants roaming the hills...the resonating call of the alarmed Barking Deer and the soft stealthy footstep of the leopard. We heard that there may still be a few striped cats too, left in the heart of the hills.

chapters.title: The Young Oak and the Old Lady
paragraphs: The Silver Oak opened its leaves for the first time in the morning and looked around in wonder. This was his first glimpse of the world around him and what he saw fascinated him. He was first on the mountain side. All around him the big trees swayed in the cool breeze and murmured together as if welcoming him to their world. There were old wise oak trees. The pines flittered their silken leaves. The Chirs rose up straight and tall in pride, their barks freckled by time. The long-tailed Minivets in their colourful plumage flitted from branch to branch like a bunch of colourful fire crackers. A slight rustle and out strode a bird which looked as if god has gone mad while creating him. Many years later, when he himself grew old and wise and saw this bird no more he heard the pilgrims going up to the temple and on the high passes, talk about this bird whom the y refer to as Jujurana as being the greatest creation among birds by god. The say that when God created the birds, he decided to create their king. He picked the most colourful feathers from all the birds and created Jujurana (“Juju” means Bird and “Rana” means King). The Forest Department people and others more learned used to refer to this bird as the Western Tragopan, however this was much later. Now the bird barely paid him any attention and probably displayed his fluorescent feathers as i went about to impress the female bird which was busy picking among the bushes a yard away. A small mountain spring gurgled and bubbled in delight as it flowed down the mountain side a few meters down. It’s clear water pale blue from the melting glaciers, further up. The boulders smoothed by the caress of the cold water sat impassively enjoying the busy flow of the little stream when the rains would start, this little stream would swell and roar down but now it was like a little girl in a very playful mood. The jungle around him vibrated and throbbed with life and laughter. Beyond the bend the slope, the mighty mountain rose in cascades, like the rise and fall of mighty ocean waves. The crest gleamed with snow. The sky was so blue that it could almost be felt. The little plant shivered with a gust of breeze and sat there absorbing the sunlight through every pore. He was hungry. He soaked up the water from the moist soil and the sunlight and the minerals in the water from the soil nourished his young tender body.

The day passed and the young chap felt happy. Then the sun turned the sky pure gold and disappeared behind the towering slopes and soon it was pitch dark. It was not dark for long, as a half-moon rose in the starlit sky. They twinkled merrily together and the moon hung in the sky like a smile. The mighty trees shone like silver. Soon he heard heavy breathing and footsteps coming down the hillside. Not knowing what it was, the little fellow wanted to go back under the soil from where he had sprung but there was no going back. Soon the heavy animal strolled out and squarely planted a heavy foot on his tender body. The little oaks body bent with the weight of the animal but being young his body was supple and did not break. Still it was painful but the foot was off his back in a moment and the heavy body of the Himalayan Black Bear waddled the slope in search of his dinner of roots and berries...maybe a tasty morsel of the left-over of some kill. The big Himalayan Wood Owl sitting on a rock nearby didn’t care two hoots about the big bear and hooted in derision. This reassured the little oak and he settled down in his moist bed as the dew condensed on his little leaves like little pearl drops.

When the sun rose again next morning, which the little oak thought will never rise again it rose in its chariot of fire and the day was wonderful. The little oak enjoyed every moment of it, feeding on the pure rays, air and water, listening to the hamming of the bees and Rosy Finches and Sunbirds. He said hello to the gloriously coloured Monal Pheasants and waved its little leaves in greeting at a Golden Eagle soaring in the mountain eddies high overhead. That evening he met a Leopard as it sat on a rock above him. The Leopard did not pay any attention to the small saplings little frame since a herd of Mountain Goats were grazing in a high pasture in the opposite mountain.

Soon the Little Oak found himself at peace with his surroundings. Fed and watered, he started growing strong and his roots clapped the loose top soil of the mountain slope. In a few days’ time he saw his brother, another Silver Oak, coming out of the earth a few meters above the boat. Being older by a couple of weeks he waved in assurance at his brother who was looking around in the same bewilderment at the mountain vista. The little fellow felt the blessings from elder brother and relaxed in the sunlight.

Both started growing tall and sturdy and soon looked fit to be proudly called “Oaks” by the neighbouring trees and denizens. The monsoon clouds crashed overhead and the mist blew up from the valley shrouding them in a veil.

Then thunders rolled across the high meadows and the storms wind across the passes but by now the two brothers were strong enough and stood their ground. Some old Chirs and Pines crashed down the slope, but the brothers stood side by side. Then autumn and winter passed by. Their young leaves were plastered with snow but their youth did not feel the cold. The Musk Deer and the Serow brushed against the trunks and shook some of the snow of their leaves. The year turned around.

When they were old enough to talk to their seniors, they stood listening to their stories about how the Gods came and played in the meadows, high up and how the gurgling stream sprang from the cold icy cliffs. On rare occasions, pilgrims came and sat at their feet and they heard them talk of some mutiny in far of places and how some white people had come and grabbed the land. They could not understand what this mutiny was all about but could sense the anger in the voices of the people. Even the ancient Chir trees who had seen much could not elaborate on the subjects of the humans and their concerns. However, the young ones did not care much about mutinies of humans. There was much happiness around them that they didn’t worry too much over the concerns of humans.

The years flew by. There was hardly anybody to take notice. In time many of the old trees died and their place were taken by younger ones who looked up to the two brothers for assurance and the birds made their nests in their branches which slowly became gnarled. They knew that those gnarled branches would give them protection from the predators and the elements. Their roots firmly gripping the slope. Rain and wind couldn’t erode the mountain slopes. 100 years passed but the jungle stood pristine. The white men left the land but up here nobody took notice. Pious pilgrims to the old shrines sat in the shade and did not harm anybody. They ate rested and prayed at peace with the old forest. Sometimes grazers would come up with their hardy cattle on their way to the pastures to feed on the juicy grass that sprang up in summer.

However, they did not notice, change was in the air...a change that would alter everybody’s life in the patch of paradise ..forever. Years had passed by and the white people had left, to be replaced by brown ones. The characters of the people now coming up slowly started to be a little different from the pious pilgrims. Small houses of hamlets started appearing down the valley formed by the little stream. The pilgrims were now slowly being replaced by a more boisterous crowd who drank and made noise. The leopards’ pugmarks were markedly less visible now. The bears came down no more to trod on the bushes, and eat their ripe red berries. The Goral and Serow stayed in the higher pastures. Some men came up with long fire emitting sticks and killed a musk deer near the two brothers. Now the twisted branches just sat looking, as the men took away the little deer presumably to make something which smelt good as far as could be gathered from their conversation. The king of the birds the Western Tragopan vanished and so did the gloriously coloured Monal. More men kept coming and digging up the forest in search of plants called Nagchhatris which supposedly cure diseases and also made old men virile. The old brothers could not make any sense out of this since they had seen the number of humans increasing and could not understand why they wanted to increase the number of their species further.

As the next century turned and the two brothers became older, they still clasped the slopes firmly and breathed out life giving oxygen. While the Musk Deer came no more and the bears and leopards had all but vanished, their trunks still offered solace and protection to the numerous birds. The spring still gurgled and danced and raised during the monsoon. An old white man and his Indian wife came and built a small house opposite the stream. Though the couple were elderly they had stepped sprightly. They were at peace with the brothers and all that surrounded them. They took as much was necessary but returned that ten folds with love and affection. The old man was from a faraway land, he hardly bore any inner resemblance to the white folks who ruled the land many years back. Sometimes other important looking people came to visit them. The pilgrims and others who came up often left behind discarded pieces of trash which the old man meticulously collected and took back. Unknown to the brothers and others, he sat in his studio and turned this apparently useless and environmentally damaging things into exquisite pieces of art. An empty bottle of cold drink and a can of sardines turned into bird in the old man’s magic hands.

It was fine morning when disaster struck. The monsoon had started but was still dilly dallying about unleashing her full fury. It was cloudy high up in the slopes but down here, the sun shone through the clouds. A large group of humans came up the narrow road leading up to the old man’s house. They clutched axes and ropes. Soon the little paradise was in utter disarray. Those ancient trees were chopped down mercilessly. The huge Chirs came crashing down. The ancient Pines were hacked to death. In death too their sap gave off the sweet smell.

The men laughed and talked about the Sainj River Valley where apparently, they had done the same to bring up some project which created power from water to supply it to the cities which were hungry to light up their shopping complexes and entertainment zones. Here they had to make a road but apparently the road was to be made further down and elder brother did not know why they were being cut down. The mighty roots were uprooted. Branches which were hundreds of years old got severed from the trunks. The nests of the birds were destroyed in moments. The top soil which had taken millions of years to form showered down in to the stream.

Then they came for the younger brother. He had nowhere to run... nowhere to hide. ‘Ihe older brother shook his huge branches in fury and then in despair. Nobody came to their rescue. Those little leaves which had shivered in the morning eons back and now were glossy green with age went flying in the air. The sturdy branches were hacked as he looked up to his elder brother asking for solace one last time before life was extinguished. The elder brother looked on tied to the earth by his massive roots and now it was his turn. The men rigged the ropes and soon the sharp axes penetrated his old skin. Pieces of wood went flying off. His body shivered wordlessly in pain. As he braced for his end, the Indian wife came running up shouting at the top of her voice. She stood under the old trunk and refused to budge. The man who was leading the team requested and then threatened her, but she stood her ground. When nothing worked the men left. The old silver oak was the only one left standing amid the devastation. The stump of the younger brother was lying lifeless. All their friends were dead. The pretty stream was no longer pretty. Branches clogged her flow and massive trunks lay across the banks. The men did not bother to remove them. The soil had turned the clear blue waters, muddy. Some trees hung on precariously from the top, half their roots severed. Sooner or later they were bound to crush down. The silver oak and the old lady wept surrounded by death.

In this twenty-first century in the age of globalization and internet, nuclear power and missiles, spacecrafts and Kyoto Protocols, perhaps the tears of the Old Silver Oak tree and those of an old lady do not hold any significance. However, in the centuries to come maybe... just maybe... the tears shed in the corner of the Great Himalayan National Park in Himachal Pradesh, India, might reflect the tears of a generation to come.

chapters.title: The Man Who Bought a Mountain
paragraphs: ‘But why did he buy a mountain?’ that was the first question which came to my mind when my cousin brother, Bubai, told me that he had been to Mathaburu (the Bald Mountain in Saontali dialect) the previous winter where a certain Chittababu had bought the mountain.
‘His passion is to sculpt huge birds on the bare face of the mountain top’, Bubai said.

‘In that case we must go and see it... if somebody has bought a mountain for sculpting birds, the least we can do is to go and see it in first person, before the gentleman was shipped off to the loony bin.’ It was the month of November 1998 and we were discussing the follies of mankind in general after a heavy lunch. Thus, a trip to the remote parts of the district of Purulia in West Bengal was settled. Bubai had a friend, Rajkumar, who wanted to see the works of the modern Michael Angelo and I had a friend Shoumitro, alias Shomu, who was interested in any sort of madness and thus a team of four was born.

The catch came two days before departure when Bubai announced that some friends of his had suggested that instead of visiting Mathaburu straight away, it would be a better trip to go toa place called Murguma, stay overnight and then proceed to Mathaburu on foot the next day. Purulia is a hilly and forested terrain.
‘Do you realise that I weigh a ton and have no wish to reduce it by boulder hopping?’ I enquired quite alarmed.
“My friends have assured me that it is only half a day’s walk from the Murguma dam and the path does not cross hills but just skirts the lower slopes,’ Bubai said disarmingly.
The die was cast and I had no way of backing off, more so because Shomu weighed at least half a ton more than me and was quite enthusiastic about the short walk. Talk about peer pressure...

Boarded the train at night and disembarked at Purulia station next morning where the body of my brand-new Nike shoe promptly severed diplomatic relations from its sole on my right foot. I bought a pair of rubber slippers for twenty rupees which incidentally lasted about a year and a half of rigorous use.

We got onto a bus bound for the hamlet of Begunkondor and took an autorickshaw after that to reach Murguma. It was a sleepy hamlet of a few huts. There was one shop which sold everything from kerosene to Kentucky fried papad. Since we were denied entry in the dilapidated forest rest house we stocked up on kerosene and enquired about possible camping grounds and were directed towards the towering wall of the dam. We climbed the embankment and stared.

In front of us was a huge water body... the water had dried up in patches, leaving small boulders and pebbles. It was surrounded on three sides by green hills and the reddish-brown earthen path from the village winded up through verdant deciduous trees. The steep bank was covered with huge boulders which we overcame and finally pitched our small tent in one of the dried-out patches after walking through a stretch of water.

It was past mid-day and we were famished. Soon the little stove got blazing and we prepared a delicious dish of Maggi noodles and a few cups of tea. After our lunch Bubai and Rajkumar returned to the village for dinner arrangements while Shomu promptly fell asleep. I too felt drowsy as the warm late autumn air blew across the lake creating little ripples. The sun shone brightly on the water of the lake and the silence was time to time broken by a Brahminy Kite calling shrilly overhead and the mooing of a few stringy cows grazing on the other side of the lake. Then a few pink fairies came sailing over the lake and I shook hands with Jim Corbett behind my tent. He started telling me a long-winded story about how he shot a goat in the jungles of Amazon.

When Jim finished his story, I opened my eyes. Shomu sat smoking at the water’s edge gazing dreamily out at the water. Of Bubai and Rajkumar there was no sign. The sky had turned bright orange. Darkness was descending. Then two figures leapt onto the boulders from the embankment and soon the two musketeers waded through the water clutching two bottles full of water wearing huge grins. ‘I'he water turned out to be Mouli, a local drink distilled from the Mohua fruit. They had arranged for our dinner which would arrive around nine in the evening.

Soon darkness descended. A small candle fought a losing battle with the darkness from a corner of our tent, shielded from the breeze. The bottles were opened and as the contents sank in volume, a huge moon rose over the hills. There was not a light in sight. The lake turned into liquid silver and the mountains sat motionless. Only the sound of lapping ripples of water at our feet mingled with our conversation. Even we did not dare to raise our voice in spite of the influence of Bacchus as if afraid that the beauty might shatter.

A gentleman carrying a lantern brought our chapattis and mutton curry from the village and we savoured the meal sitting under a million stars. Shomu faced a slight trouble trying to wash his hands after the meal, from the water of the lake, since he was taking two steps backward for every step he took forward but that did not interfere with his snoring inside the tent when we finally went to sleep which was only broken once by a pair of jackals who wanted to investigate the new construction coming up on their property.

We were supposed to start for Mathaburu by seven in the morning. We stuck to the schedule and departed a little after nine. The path winded pleasantly skirting the lake for about a kilometre and a half towards the hills. After that it reared its head like Jack’s beanstalk and headed straight up the hills. | looked at the inclination, horrified but there was no turning back. If looks could kill, I would have been one cousin brother short.

The climb started and our backpacks became heavier with every step. After about an hour and a half’s arduous climbing the path petered out and ended in a stream. We hacked a path through the jungle and eventually came upon a game path. This too had no inclination of going downhill. The sun now became hotter. Slowly the game trail widened a bit and still climbed. Water ran out. We had not seen a soul since we had departed from Murguma.

Just when the trail became wide enough and we were thinking that we had reached some sort of regular track, the trail bifurcated. Right at the bifurcation there lay a huge pile of dung.... elephant dung. It was not very old either. The first impulse was to run.... but which path to take?

Bubai and Rajkumar took the trail on the left while Shomu and I stood next to the pile of dung. Then we sat down with our back to each other to cover all the sides in case of a charge from the pachyderms.

The jungle was absolutely still. The heat simmered and the crickets kept up their monotonous whirr from the bushes. Not a waft of breeze stirred the leaves of the trees. The uncanny feel that invisible eyes were watching us, grew uncomfortably.

‘In case of a charge, fling off the backpack and run’, I spoke under my breath, ‘and in case it does not deter the charge take off your shirt and fling it to the ground. The elephant will be distracted by the shirt and you will get a few more seconds.’ ‘Tf the elephant still charges what do I take off next?’ enquired Shomu in a hoarse whisper. He sometimes becomes incorrigible.

Bubai and Rajkumar returned shortly in a jubilant mood. The path on the left had a tell-tale sign of civilization.... a torn rubber slipper draped on a lantana bush. Extremely relieved, we took the trail on the left only to come up against another bifurcation about half a kilometre up. This time the trail to the left led downhill. I was fed up of acting like Tenzing Norgay and settled for the downward trail. Soon that trail turned into a path used by wild boars. We crawled for a few yards and then beat a strategic retreat.

The other trail which I had disregarded finally showed us the end of our travails when we thought that we shall have to camp out the night in the middle of the elephant habitat. Far below use was a hut which had signs of being inhabited. When our tired, aching and parched bodies reached a date palm tree near the hut, we could not find anybody. There was a muddy pond adjacent to the hut with a dead frog floating upside down. The water quenched our thirst after being treated by a generous dose of water purifier and a silent prayer.
From the date palm tree, the trail widened and finally became a human trail. There were a few bullock cart wheel tracks on the dried mud of the trail and lo...... we met a man pushing a cycle.

He gave us directions to the nearest village.... ‘you see that small tree? Take a left turn and after sometime the road splits in three. Take the extreme left turn and then take one more left turn from a banyan tree.’ Another twenty minutes of weary steps following the direction given by our friend brought us back under the same date tree beside the hut. Since practical jokers are not very popular till they are hundred years dead and gone, it was very fortunate that neither the man nor the cycle was in sight.

We followed the tracks of the bullock cart and finally reached a small hamlet. While approaching the hamlet, apparently the whole village turned up and we entered a baked mud centre point of a cluster of huts in a procession led by the headman or mukhiya. He had already smilingly given his consent to our camping inside the village after he heard that we had traversed the hills from Murguma and had crossed the path of a herd of elephants that frequently raided the crops of the village. Our uncanny feeling of being watched inside the jungle might not have been a figment of our imagination after all.

We pitched tent under a Gulmohar tree. The rest went down to a stream to collect water and freshen up. I sat on a log and was immediately surrounded by the whole village. The mukhiya had gone somewhere and the rest of the population sat surrounding me and looking up to me with expectant and amazed eyes. I almost felt that | needed to give a speech of some sorts about the situation of the crops or the current economic situation in Namibia. It was an extremely uncomfortable situation and I squirmed in embarrassment till I was rescued by the rest of the team returning. It was my turn to go down to the stream.

Everything was red. A short distance downhill from our camping site through some patches of vegetable farms lay the small stream flowing busily down. Huge boulders lay glistening like prehistoric beasts. I sat down on one and dipped my feet into the cold flowing water. The response was miraculous to say the least. Till then I thought that I was carrying my feet and not the other way around but the water brought life into my dead limbs. I sat and looked around in a trance. The sun had just set and the water was red.... the sky was red.... the boulders were red.... the trees overhanging the stream were red.... the grass and the bushes were red.... perhaps that was an omen (now these same places are the bastions of the Maoists). Somewhere in the village a dog barked. A lady from the village gave a brief scolding to some unseen child. A Nightjar flew down and sat on a boulder and on spotting me, tried to make itself disappear by turning into a piece of rock. As I stared at it, it perhaps felt uncomfortable and flew off into the bushes with a sharp chuck.... chuck.... chuckoooo and kept repeating the haunting call at intervals. The wind, now a little cold flew down the crevices, picked up some leaves from the forest floor and danced a little foxtrot with them before discarding them like a careless lover and blew on. It was fully dark when I made my way back to the camp.

It took almost the whole of next day to reach Ayodhya Pahar, the nearest semi-town. Rajkumar and Bubai, being fleet footed, went on ahead and Shomu and I followed in a more leisurely pace. We walked through vast fields of mustard flowers and small Adivasi (original inhabitants) villages. We asked direction for Ayodhya Pahar from an old man carrying a deadly looking axe. He wanted a bidi (local made cigar made from tendu leaves) and was delighted when offered a cigarette instead. He grandly informed that Mr. Jyoti Basu (former Chief Minister of West Bengal) was a great man since he had been to Calcutta in the year 1982. He asked what my profession was and on being informed that I work on a ship, asked me whether I was the driver or the cleaner. I was taken to his small hut in the jungle and offered water and puffed rice and introduced to his family. When I left his humble abode, I was convinced that gentlemen are not a vanishing breed.

From Ayodhya Pahar we managed to hire a vehicle which dropped us at Mathaburu. A short walk from the main road brought us to the foot of the mighty hill. It was almost dark. Chittababu had constructed a small concrete house adjacent to the jungle. Since no one was at home we started pitching camp next to the building when a man with a lantern appeared. When asked we informed him that we intended to spend the night in our tent. He was shocked.

‘Elephants will descend at night. Why don’t you stay inside the hut?’ We had thought about it but had desisted since we did not have permission to stay in the building. Sleeping in the path of wild elephants clinched the issue and we promptly opened the unlocked door. The man proceeded towards the jungle.
“What about you? Where are you going inside the jungle?’ we enquired.

‘Thave a small plot of vegetable farm inside the forest. Don’t worry, I know when they are around’ and he cheerfully disappeared inside the gathering gloom. The feeble glow of the lantern was soon swallowed up by the trees.

Shortly the moon rose above Mathaburu in full glory. We looked up at the huge massif, black as coal. The colossal figures of huge birds adorned the top of the mountain, glowing white, in sharp contrast with the black rock. They looked as ifa flock of prehistoric birds had frozen in time. The small patch of field was awash in moonlight as we looked on in awe at the work of the genius who had created such a spectacle. Chittababu will be forgotten in the chapters of history but his eccentric creativity will probably live long till the time we redefine the term ‘eccentric’, The next day we returned to ‘civilization’.

chapters.title: The Fight Is On
paragraphs: He could trace his ancestry from the dinosaurs. The Pterodactyls had ceased to fly. T-Rex did the pursue the Stegosaurus across the ancient plains. His ancestors survived all the natural calamities élan. However, as the centuries passed and the Homo Sapiens started ruling the planet, their habit started to shrink alarmingly. Finally, their home range shrunk to two continents separated by oceans, mountains, deserts and forests. Huge tracts of human habitations separated his ancestors and they survived in Africa and Asia. In Asia they started beating a retreat but his magnificent horn sounded his doom. The Chinese found his horns irresistible as a means for expanding their exploding population. Quack doctors prescribed the powdered horn as a remedy for conjugal dysfunction and many other medical conditions. Their prescription was not based on any medical research but solely on the shape of the horn. The problem was that, it could not be procured without the consent of its owners. The owners flatly refused to part with their horns. The solution...? Kill them and chop their horns off.

This went on for centuries till a handful of his ancestors were left, struggling for existence in a corner of colonial India... Kaziranga In the North eastern corner of India, amid vast grasslands on the plains of the Brahmaputra River, flanked by the massive wall of the Himalayas his ancestors fought a losing battle with humans with guns. They were mighty beasts but helpless against the force of a puny bullet.

Just as it seemed that the battle was over and it looked likely that the Chinese will have to carry on expanding their population without the help of the horn of his ancestors, the then rulers led by Lady Curzon, decided that it was not really worth losing the big ones after all. They were ungainly and presumably ugly but lent a charm to the beautiful land of Assam that would be lost forever if the battle was lost.

Kaziranga, the fabled land of the One Horned Rhino was born in the beginning of twentieth century. Slowly, over the years, his ancestors made a comeback seldom seen in the short History of conservation. From just about a hundred, by the time the century turned, their numbers had gone up to more than two thousand. However, unknown to him, the battle was yet to be won. Every year men with big guns came in the dead of the night and sometimes even by day and shot his kin and the precious horn was still exported to the hungry markets of China at astronomical prices, His small brain did not register all these complexities. He was happy with the green provided by the rich soil. He was happy to survive the annual devastating Rood by the Brahmaputra. When all else was submerged by the raging torrents, he was happy if he could find a patch of dry high ground. He was happy even if he had to share his grassland with the huge Wild Buffalos who too were survivors. The herds of elephants, which too were under threat, trespassed his land and his patch of mud in which he wallowed during the peak heat of blistering summer, drew a mild rebuke but still he was happy. As he drew grew up into a fine one horned rhino with a horn which was the envy of his peers, he admiring glances from the fairer sex. People who came from far and wide went gaga over his horn. He almost became a celebrity and enjoyed his fame.

He was as happy a rhino as could be. so The flood in the year 2012 was more severe than the rest. River Brahmaputra vented his fury as only he could. Huge and ancient trees got uprooted and were swept away in minutes. Animals died in hordes. Yet he found a patch of high ground and survived till the water receded. He was happy to have survived.

The sun had set in blazing colours drenching the lakes and grassland and the forest in a burst of gold. The clouds had disappeared over the horizon with the receding monsoon. Though the ground was still soggy, the air held the promise of a new beginning for life. The flood brought death but in death it brought new life. Like the cycle of life, the flood gave back what it took.

The grass was fresh and green and tasted sweet and crunchy. As darkness descended, the Hog Deer sounded alarm calls. Perhaps a Tiger was afoot or maybe the elusive Panther A male Sambar picked up the alarm and crashed through the undergrowth voicing the metallic 'dhank dhank’ alarm sound which reverberated across the plains. After a shot pause, an Elephant mother gave a short burst of alarm. Only a Tiger could have alarmed an Elephant mother. She most probably had her calf with her and hence was wary of the striped terror. None of these really bothered the rhino.

He knew that not even the biggest of Tigers would be his match. As the fireflies blinked and danced their ghostly dance, weaving in and out through the branches of the tall trees bordering the grassland, he settled down for the night, happy.

Towards dawn he felt like emptying his bowels. However, as per instinct, genetically imprinted, he had to do this task at one place only. He had already built up a big pile day by day not far from where he was resting. This was where he headed.

The sky was faintly speckled with pink as the sun appeared on the eastern horizon. A cool wind blew down the plains ruffling the fronds of the tender green Elephant Grass. Chuck chuck chuckoooooo cried a Nightjar nestling on the ground. A waning moon smiled down om the heavens. The rhino reached his pile, emptied his bowels and... felt...

Suddenly a piercing light broke from behind a nearby tree and shone directly in his face. He felt a sense of doom. In desperation he bobbed his massive head from side to side to avoid the light when a rattling sound rang out from behind the light. The pain was searing. ‘Ihe massive folds of skin could not stop those puny metal shards and they ripped through the muscles and tissues and he felt his bones breaking inside him. As his world darkened more than the darkest night he sank to his haunches and finally toppled over. The soggy ground came up to meet him as he crashed. Hated human figures emerged from behind the light which he could still see. He was not dead yet. They came up to him. There were four of them. One of them took out a long and wicked machete.

His eyes were gradually fading and yet he could see the gleam on the sharp edges of the machete. He knew that there was no escape and yet tried one final time to threaten his tormentors with his massive horn but his muscles did not pay heed to what his brain dictated. As he lost consciousness the machete came down heavily time and again till his horn came apart and a gaping, bleeding hole was left in its place. The men collected the majestic horn ina filthy sack and departed as silently as they came.
When sense returned to him, he wished that he was dead. The pain was unbearable and made him lose consciousness again and again. The damp earth no longer smelt of mud but that of blood.

This went on for hours but his life refused to leave him till some men in uniform came with heavy guns. They took mercy on him and he went down with a bullet in his heart, smelling the earth, damp with his blood. Before he closed his eyes, he heard the wind blowing over the waters of Sohola Beel carrying with it the call of the thousands of Ruddy Shelducks. Northern Pintails, Bar Headed Geese and countless others who called this magnificent place, “Home”.
He felt ‘happy’.

Kaziranga is not just a National Park or a forest or wildlife sanctuary. It is an experience and a celebration of the success of conservation which is under threat day in and day out. It is not only the celebrated Rhino which is under threat but the less celebrated but equally, if not more endangered species like the Bengal Florican, Hog Badger, Pangolin, Eastern Swamp Deer and others. If Kaziranga disappears someday, it will herald the termination of the spirit of life. The battle is yet to be won. The fight is on... in earnest.

chapters.title: The Story of Dream the Leopard Cub
paragraphs: The little fur ball climbed up my chest and snatched away my heart.

My friend Gautam got a call from the forest department that a leopard cub had been found in the tea gardens off Margherita, Upper Assam and needed to be released and since he worked with the wildlife in Kaziranga, his advice and presence would be of help. The cub was found by one of the locals inside the garden and picked up and handed over to the forest dept. Since the Ranger was out of station nobody knew what to do and already 3 days had passed and we were losing time with every passing day. As a species, we humans are so obnoxious that even a leopard mother might not accept back her baby if there is a detectable human imprint and here the baby was being hand reared by the servants. We decided to try our luck that evening.

The plan was to take the cub to the location where she was found, try look for pug marks or some other indications of the mother’s presence and leave her there and watch from a safe distance inside our Gypsy all the night if required. We hoped that the cries of the cub would attract the mother and hopefully they would reunite. In case the cub was rejected we would bring her back. We were yet to see the cub.

Gautam, Mr. Deka the forest guard and myself drove down to Margherita town that afternoon and alighted at the ranger’s office. We thought that the cub would be housed in a cage away from prying eyes. Instead a servant boy came up holding a cardboard box and from it peered a soulful pair of blue eyes with a face that might have melted the heart of Hannibal and could only be called - DREAM. The cub was hardly 2 months old and she clambered up my arms purring like a tabby. She was not even weaned and was being fed with milk from a bottle. There was no way we could abandon her in the wild without her mother.

We set out immediately accompanied by 2 men of the forest department who knew the location. When we reached the place, it presented still further problems. The tea garden was flanked on 2 sides by dense jungle and the furrows were so deep that if we lost the cub it would be impossible to get her back in the gathering gloom. Then it started drizzling.

The cub was put down on the ground and immediately she started calling and tried to disappear inside the tea plants. We collected her and went down the middle of the plantation. Ever tried to release a leopard cub in a tea garden in the midst of a tea garden flanked by jungles in a drizzle wearing bathroom slippers and carrying a DSLR where the chances of stumbling upon an angry mother leopard are quite high? Do not try it.

At one point we got the scent of the leopard quite strongly but by that time the dusk was gathering and the risk was too high. Still we set the cub down but it immediately vanished inside the tea plants and was found more by luck than by tracking skills. We realized that this was almost a futile effort since we could not get the gypsy so far inside and leaving the cub to its fate would most likely get it killed either by other leopards or by the elephants which pass by regularly or even by snake bite. Standing guard on the ground the whole night would put all of us at high risk.

We searched for a safer location to set Dream down but soon it was dark and there was not a spot in sight where she would be out of harm’s way. The search was abandoned and we carried her back to the Ranger’s house. From that night the floods of Assam set in and after that there was no point in looking for the mother.

Before leaving I paid one more visit just to hold Dream in my arms and promise her that her story will be told even if nobody listens. Those trusting eyes will probably not see her mother again. The Forest Department did not know what to do with her. The zoos apparently were not willing take her in for lack of space. There was no plan of action in place in the state where they are found in one of the highest numbers. They do not even have a cage for such a contingency and none of the famous NGO s have thought about doing something about it. There is no awareness campaign to educate people living around the forests that leopards or other wildlife are not to be touched let alone picked up unless wounded in which case the department is to be notified. Setting dream free would have been akin to murdering her. She was nobody’s child. Forest Department was caring for her at that moment but what was the long-term solution nobody knew. Dream did not belong to a place where people would hold candle light vigils and put up posters for her survival. I too could not do anything for her except for telling her story and I am ashamed for that.

Those eyes and that purring call will haunt me forever in my dreams when my Dream calls out and find that her calls have no ears to listen to. In a far-flung corner of India in a half-forgotten place a little furball was searching for her mother. I only hoped my Dream would live.... she would be in my heart till it stops beating. After I left and came back home, Gautam called me to let me know that Guwahati zoo had finally accepted her. She would live her life behind bars.... alone.... looking at gawking visitors who would poke fun at her from the safety beyond the cage but nobody will know her story. At least dream will live and that was more than I could hope for when I left her. ...and it all started with a phone call from Gautam while I was fast asleep one Sunday afternoon - “come fast.... the floods have started in Kaziranga”. That is what I had longed to see for a long time. My association with Gautam and Kaziranga goes back for quite a few years but I was yet to see Kazi during the floods. Every time the floods started, I was held up due to some reason or the other but this time there was no stopping me. I boarded the Kamrup Express the next day and headed for Furkating, the nearest railhead for Kazi.

When J alighted at one o’clock at night Gautam and my old trusted driver Bubul were waiting for me. ‘lhe pleasantries over Gautam suggested that we travel up and down the National Highway bordering Kaziranga and that we did. From one to four we travelled up and down the highway and spotted a herd of Wild Buffalo along with the usual Hog Deer and spent the next few hours at Gautam’s house snoring away.

That morning we travelled to Gautam’s camp and this time I did not get to experience my usual roller coaster ride over the fallow fields bordering the Dhanseri river... because there were no fields... everything was under water and we travelled by boat over the same place where I usually travel by Gypsy.

Since Kaziranga was closed, I usually spent my days with the camp workers, collecting driftwood on the Brahmaputra and that is when we met a Softshell turtle on one of the river islands which was the size of a medium sized dining table. Rambabu was carrying almost half a dead tree on his incredibly strong shoulders in waist deep mud back to the boat when the turtle showed itself by moving. It was truly a magnificent species but had an injury mark near the neck which needed closer inspection for which we needed to turn it on its back. Easier said than done. As the workers tried to close in, the turtle struck at incredible speed and those open jaws could do a lot of damage. Evading those jaws in the waist deep slimy mud with camera in hand was not the easiest of jobs but the rest of the party were having a tougher time trying to turn it over. The huge lady tried to haul herself over the mud and disappear into the river. Rambabu, in a final desperate attempt, got on her shell and I had the incredible opportunity of observing a man riding a turtle. Rambabu latched on and was dragged on for about a meter and a half before a flick of the shoulders of the turtle threw him squarely into the mud. However, those few seconds had given enough time for the rest of the team to catch and turn her over. The whole incident was so jaw dropping that by the time I managed to bring my jaws back in place, Rambabu was up and we were inspecting the injury. It turned out to be a superficial one and she was put back on her feet and she gracefully reached the swirling waters and disappeared into the Brahmaputra.

When the clouds cleared in the evenings, I would sit alone on the watch tower overlooking the forest and the flooded Brahmaputra watching the clouds chase each other over the Eastern Himalayas across the river. Some nights I would sit alone on the terrace. The workers would have left and Rambabu would retire to his village after making me a flask of tea and dinner. On a clear full moon night, the moon would rise over the dark waters flooding the undulating grass. Elephants would emerge from the tree line as the night wore on and graze under the tower. A rhino mother would emerge with her baby in tow, catch the scent of a feline in the warm breeze and hurry back into the safety of the swamps. The stars would twinkle and I would watch the spectacle, a hot cup of tea in hand, as contended as a man could ever be.

Then we got an invitation from the DFO Mr. R.K. Das of Dehing Patkai Wildlife Sanctuary. Gautam and myself drove down to Digboi town and on to Mr. Das’s residence. When we reached in the afternoon, a pair of Oriental Pied Hornbills sat on the coconut tree outside his office and welcomed us with their loud calls. As we sipped our tea clouds started gathering and it started to rain. A drive into the forest in the late afternoon was rewarded with a herd of elephants bathing in the dense rainforest.... a different setting from any of my previous sightings of the gentle giants.

Four days in Dehing Patkai and it rained like Niagara. There was hardly any let up from the pouring water. Exploring a rainforest in rain has its own charm especially when the clouds kiss the towering trees and the rain seeps to every pore of your body and soul... when the puniest stream takes on the form of a raging torrent.... when the air you breathe in tastes of moss and lichens.... when in the pitch darkness you listen to the drumming of the water drops on the roof of your vehicle as you feel the presence of life all around you without seeing them.... when you feel that you have lived life to the fullest. .... and then you meet Dream and realise that there is so much more to life.

chapters.title: Tadoba: Raining Tigers
paragraphs: Just below my window, an elderly gentleman is haggling with the price of tomatoes. The driver of a bus is having a heated argument with a pedestrian about the right of way on the main road and the rest of the assorted cars and buses are honking for space. My friend Raj is waiting on the road with his son for the school bus. The sweeper is knocking at my door. My wife has just finished lecturing me about my eccentricities and has gone to get the kids ready for school. How do I describe that evening in Tadoba under such circumstances?

Blistering heat is what comes first to my mind...... blistering, boiling, burning, April heat. Sanjeet’s Toyota Fortuner was showing 46 degrees. The day was hot. Each breath was laborious.... the trees and the red earth gave out the heat... it could almost be felt. The buffer zone was our target for the day. Our memory cards were already bursting at the seams with tiger images as well as others from the core area of the forest. Hence decided to explore the buffer zone. Yes, it’s true that we did not spot any tigers but we were not after tigers either. A leopard had mauled and killed four villagers in the buffer zone and was freely roaming around. That is what we were after.... though we could not have known whether it was the rogue or not in case we sighted one.

In that blistering heat, the stripes and spots eluded us. Sambar, wild dogs, cheetal and gaurs kept us company but overall, it was quite uneventful though enjoyable. By the time we had reached the forest check post in the middle of the forest check post, the sun was getting low over the horizon. The red earth, typical of Tadoba, looked even redder after absorbing the heat of the day. It had rained the previous night and the grass around the pathway still looked green in spite of suffering the onslaught of the sun for the whole day. There was hardly any breeze. The pathway by which we came met at the main metalled road which leads to the Moharli Gate from Chandrapur Town and the first entrance to the Park. The small single storied, whitewashed forest check post stood at the cross roads surrounded by a frail looking barbed wire fence. The forest guards and a few village lads were playing cricket.

Yes, and this was the place where we saw a tiger crossing the previous evening. Since the day was not very active photography wise, we stopped our car to let our guide out and we got down too. Vinit, Indranil and Saurabh were local lads and were immediately summoned to the game. I joined in soon. Sanjeet, being of a more placid nature, doubled up as a spectator cum sports photographer. We needed some applause.

The rules and sports accessories were simple. A tennis ball, a piece of wood resembling a bat and a plastic chair for the wicket that was the sum total of our gear apart from the vigour and enthusiasm. The rules were simple though not quite cricket. If the batsman hit a ball which landed beyond a small pathway used by the gaurs then he was out and he had the additional task of fetching the ball. It was quite thrilling to watch the errant batsmen looking for the ball with one eye and scanning the surrounding forest with the other. There were no runs, since any overthrow might send the ball into the thickets. The number of balls faced counted as runs. The wicketkeeper sometimes shifted the chair which replaced the wicket, from behind to bring it in line with the trajectory of the ball. This often raised the heckle of the batsman concerned but the game was played in due spirit.

I batted for a credible 5 runs before being dastardly bowled by the keeper who had shifted the chair and I had raised my bat to a wide delivery before finding that the chair was hit. After bowling a couple of fruitless overs, I took up my position at silly mid-off and hoped that the batsmen would not hit the ball my way. I was having a pleasant time since hardly any balls came my way and I had ample time to reflect on the situation. A bunch of people ranging from fifteen to forty-five were playing cricket inside a forest where a leopard was on the prowl but nobody seemingly gave it a thought. A mixed herd of Cheetal and Sambar hurried into the jungle from the left to right crossing the pathway without even looking up towards us. A Crested Serpent Eagle was flying high overhead in a spotless orange sky calling out from time to time. The best part of it all was that the whole thing seemed so natural, as if this was the most normal thing to do.

The game was on in full swing and the sun was below the horizon when a Scorpio coming down from the Moharli Gate screeched to a halt on the road next to our cricket pitch. I was quite pleased at having some spectators other than our placid Sanjeet. All showmen including players love to be watched. I decided to pull up my socks and be a little more active in field. Then I noticed the hands of the occupants of the Scorpio pointing towards something and not towards us. I tried to spot what they were pointing towards but could not see anything.
