The rest of the players were lost in the game. So, I decided to find out the cause of the excitement of the Scorpio riders.

As the Scorpio started and sped away down the road, I walked a few meters down the road watching the forest. There was a small water tank about 30 meters from where we were playing about 20 feet inside the jungle and sitting beside the tank was a pack of Indian Wild Dogs or Dholes. I stood watching transfixed but they did not get alarmed at my presence or that of the players a short distance away. Two of them sat in the small pool of water while the rest lazed about. There was a manual water pump beside me and I sat on the parapet watching them. Shortly a man came from the forest check post with a bucket. He placed the bucket under the nozzle and started pumping water. When I brought the pack to his attention, he gave a derisive shrug and filled his bucket and went back. I watched the pack till it became too dark in the undergrowth.
I walked back feeling a little satisfied... a little bewildered and above all with a feeling of contention.

To sum up Tadoba in a few word..... tigers, lots of them, more than you can believe.... huge open cast mines resembling the sets of the film Avatar.... massive power plants... a herd of Gaurs just a few kilometres away...... a man, a woman and two little children standing at a bus stand with their meagre belongings and a leopard crossing the road a few feet away... a forest guard on foot controlling traffic on the road while two tigers play in front of him.... a Gaur looking at a man answering nature’s call.
If you want to see man and animal living with each other in spite of all the conflict of interest, come to Tadoba.

chapters.title: How to Eat a Duck in China
paragraphs: It was a duck all right and it was also true that we wanted to eat one but this was too much.

It was the first of many visits to the port of Nanjing in China. The mighty Yangtse Kiang meandered its way but at certain points, looked like Champs Elysees during rush hour. Nearing Shanghai, the river teemed with floating objects of every description. Big freighters jostled for space with tiny boats catching fish. Puny tugs chuffed and puffed pulling barge loads of coal with all their might. Cross river ferries whirred and weaved their way through the commotion as fibre glass patrol boats zig zagged over the muddy surface. The countryside changed from huge, smoke belching chimneys to sublime green paddy fields as my ship M.V. Pratapgad gently eased herself through the throng.

The port of Nanjing is about two hundred kilometres upstream and took us more than two days to reach since our old lady could not muster much speed and the traffic did not encourage speeding. As the lines were made fast ashore, the floodlights of the port came on. It was evening time.

Soon hordes of important looking officials in splendid uniforms descended on the ship. ‘hey were from the police, immigration, health, customs, guards, stevedores and god knows what other departments. The small mess room cum conference room was overflowing and it was difficult to hear above the various sounds emitted by the horde. Subratada, my senior was in charge of dealing with all the port officials and going by the number of cigarettes he was smoking (those were the days when cigarette smoking was considered an essential part of any on board dealings), it was apparent that he was finding it hard going. Captain D’Souza had given up and left everything to Subratada and myself and was avidly watching a local news channel on TV, studiously ignoring the various demands of the officials. I tried to be helpful as best as I could by passing the staplers and various stamps only to be rebuffed in Chinese. Probably, they needed water and paper clips but the total lack of English in communications proved to be a hindrance. However, matters resolved by themselves and after about two hours of furious stamping, gesticulating, signing and smoking our vessel was cleared for operations. Then all the officials departed and all operations ceased. There were no workers in sight to load our cargo.

That did not matter either to Subratada or myself. As soon the gangway (the staircase of the ship which connects it to land) was secured properly we walked down and landed on Chinese soil armed with our shore passes. The port was not too big and we walked past containers and cranes and came up to the main gate. The impressive guard with his impassive face asked for the passes. As Subratada handed them out, he hummed out a tune from the film Haqeegat which caused a twitch in the facial muscles of the guard indicating that he was appreciative of music and liked my senior’s rendering of the song.

Outside the gate was a highway and across the highway were paddy fields. The sun had already set. The highway had very little traffic and as we walked down, we saw a few shacks dotting the roadside selling various eatables. Since we did not have any local currency i.e. the Yuan, we turned back but not before taking a longing look at various portions of meat hanging by the hooks at the shops.

Next day went by and cargo operations started in earnest. The derricks (ship’s cranes worked by means of steel ropes and pulleys with hydraulic motors) whined as ifin their death throes. Pratapgad had derricks which made enough noise to raise the dead. She was an old lady and not only was showing her age but making it heard too. To top the screeching, she spouted hydraulic oil from her numerous leaks in the motor almost on a bi-hourly basis like a fountain and the Batti Saab (ship’s electrical officer who has been given this simple title universally on Indian ships signifying that he changes bulbs or batti in Hindi. The English version is Lecky) had to run around with a bucket in hand to collect the spouting oil. However, in spite of all her drawbacks, she could load and sail.

The day was hot and sultry. The Yangtse lazily flowed past. Pratapgad had no air-conditioning which is unbelievable in this day and age. Of course, in my cabin, | had the luxury of a small table fan fixed on the bulkhead (that’s what a wall is called in a ship) and there was one in the Radio Room too. Throughout the day it sent forth gushes of warm air soaking my body in sweat. The open forward porthole (window in shipping terms) did not help much. By late afternoon, thirst had built up to unmanageable levels.

As Subratada and myself walked and sweated down the gangway, the sun was setting fire to the Yangtse but the on the other side of the mighty river the sky darkened perceptibly and we thought we noticed a few flashes that warmed our hearts. As we walked up to the guard, Subratada gave another rendition of Hageegqat and this time the guard gave a positive twist to his facial muscles and waved us on.

This time the landscape was more visible than the previous evening since the sun was still on the horizon. The green paddy fields stretched away across the road and disappeared amidst lush green hillock. Small huts lay among the paddy fields surrounded by tall trees which had started to sway having caught the freshly gushing breeze. As we walked down the road, the dark clouds climbed higher in the horizon and the breeze had the fresh earthy smell and the promise of rain.

The small roadside shacks were already open for business. Actually, they were small residential houses where the front portions were converted with the aid of a few rickety tables and chairs into eateries. Nothing fancy, no decorations, you had your chair, table and food. ‘I'he food was on display hanging from hooks at the entrance. There were various vegetables, sauces and different types of meat. Among the vegetables, the easily identifiable were potatoes, green onions, spinach, carrots and great quantities of radish. Green onions and potatoes are a delicacy at our homes and were the natural choices. Sauces looked delicious with their vibrant colours. However, when it came to the meats, we were stumped. Only the chicken and duck were familiar. There were big lumps which could have been beef or lamb but were looking a bit shrivelled. Still others looked suspiciously like rodents and though none of us are really finicky about the cooked stuff, seeing the raw material was a bit unnerving. Since chicken is more of a hoi polloi staple, we decided to go gastronomic and ordered duck. The procedure for ordering was very simple in the absence of any common language. It was just a matter of pointing out the raw material and we pointed out the potatoes, green onions and a duck hanging from the hook and pulled our chairs outside under an overhanging plastic cover and sat back. The beer was brought out without us having to point them out. We were seamen and we must have beers, that was the unwritten law. We did nothing to dispel the perception. The bottles were ice cold and we were sweaty. The owner of the shop served up a few delicious fritters of something or the other and we basked in the glow of Hindi Chini bhai bhai.

Delicious smells emanated from the kitchen. The sun set, the clouds thundered and the frogs in the adjoining fields took up the cue. The dim yellow lights of our restaurant cast more shadows than light. ‘I'he paddy fields darkened and the wind shook the green stalks and the trees with more vigour and then the rain started. The earthy smell of the fields mingled with the smells from the kitchen and the beer tasted awesome. It grew quite dark.

The first dish arrived. A bowl heaped with steaming green onions and then another one with steaming potatoes. They were cooked separately. We did not exactly expect the cooking to be what was made at home with spices but a separate plate of steamed green onions and another of potatoes was unexpected. However, the smell was great and appetizing. Then the problem arose. How do you eat with a chopstick? I have always been in awe of my Chinese friends in school when I saw them eating rice with two pieces of stick and felt that some type of magic must be involved somewhere. Now was my turn. This was my first trip to China. There were no spoons.

Seeing our dilemma, the shopkeeper ventured to give a demonstration. It was quite simple, hold one stick diagonally against your palm with the stick resting between the crook of the thumb and the ring finger. With the index and middle finger hold the other stick and manoeuvre both the sticks to hold the food and pop it into your mouth. The first few attempts led to three tiny pieces of green onion and half a piece of potato reaching my mouth. The rest scattered far and wide. Subratada fared no better. However, with more attempts, we found that an. acceptable quantity was reaching our mouths and the taste was out of the world. I have no idea how the magician in the kitchen managed to just steam two vegetables and attain such heights of culinary magnificence. I always thought that the Chinese were magicians but now I knew. Then came the duck.

There was no pre announcement of its arrival. As we sipped the beer and munched on the green onions, the plate was placed on our table. On the plate lay a whole duck. Yes, a whole duck without the feathers. The yellow beak was clamped shut, the webbed feet were neatly tucked under the bloated belly and the eyes stared without seeing. The skin had goose bumps all over. The whole bird was plucked of the feathers, the intestines cleared out and then was boiled. The potato that I was about to gulp got stuck in my gullet while Subratada spluttered on his beer. The shopkeeper showed concern but the duck looked on placidly. I blinked but the apparition did not go away.

The shopkeeper was asked to take it away and cook it properly and bring it back. He did that promptly and shortly returned with the same duck but now it was sitting on a bed of cucumbers. We, the Chinese and us Indians, were neighbours but language was not the best thing we shared.

Finally, Subratada picked up the duck by the throat and marched into the kitchen, followed by the shopkeeper, myself and the full household of the shopkeeper. Subratada cut the duck in small pieces, discarded the head and feet and fried the whole thing in oil. In the absence of chilies, half a bottle of chili sauce was emptied into the pot which shook our hosts who gave out a lot of ‘oohs’ and ‘aahs’. Finally, the meal was ready. ‘his time we used our hands to tuck into the meal. Trying to hold a piece of oily duck with a pair of sticks was beyond us, mere mortals.

By the time we finished the last morsel of duck and washed it down with the last gulp of beer, we had mastered the art of handling the chopstick. As we ambled back towards the ship, along the dark road with rain pitter pattering on our shoulders and the beer sloshing around nicely in our tummies, the frogs croaked and cheered in unison at our achievement on having our first Chinese meal albeit cooked by Subratada, on Chinese soil and mastering the sticky art of using sticks.

chapters.title: Quest for the Feral Horse
paragraphs: Cooking Maggi is quite dangerous and sometimes proves to be life threatening. Since the journey was long and arduous, to say the least, provisions needed to be carried. In some places, the only provisions procurable, cook able and eatable with any degree of safety to the internal organs are still eggs and the ubiquitous Maggi. The search for the wild feral horse had to be undergone in a small wooden dinghy, floating through rivers and rivulets across dense forests, open grasslands and meadows, over huge stretches of blistering sand and quaint Mishing villages in Dibru Saikhowa National Park in the district of Tinsukia, Assam. Though the month was November, the mid days were blistering but the mornings were cool and the nights, cold.

Thad landed up on the banks of Dibru river in Dibru Saikhowa not knowing what to expect on a cold November morning, lugging my camera and lens and precious little else. I knew about the existence of the only population of feral horse in Asia in the recesses of the Dibru Saikhowa National Park and getting their images was priority number one. Little did I know that in that process I would land up in the house of an outlawed outfit’s leader who was under surveillance, to see Hoolock Gibbons, be chased by an irate wild bull buffalo while following a pair of Pied Hornbills, would almost meet my maker when trapped in a quagmire, would spend a scorching afternoon perched yards away from a herd of wild buffaloes on a wobbly dinghy and see them eat, sleep, frolic and mate and even get myself blown up and drowned while trying to cook Maggi.
Let me tell the story of the feral horse, cooking maggi and quagmire... well, not a story but the sequence of real incidents.

Two of my friends, both Punjabis and built on generous lines in the tradition of the Land of Five rivers who cannot be named because they had bunked office to join me, were my companions along with my local guide and one boatman who supposedly knew the hideout of the feral horses.

When the eastern horizon started to get light and the jagged peaks of the Himalayas of NEFA (Arunachal Pradesh) just started getting visible in the horizon, a small dinghy with a rather hoarse diesel engine huffed and puffed to the ghat to start the journey. It was a nice little boat with a few small holes at certain points which did not sink her but gave her a character. It had a nice bit of plastic cover covering the midships. However, the plastic covering was a deterrent to unhindered photography from the boat. The problem was solved to a certain extent by throwing a narrow plank of roughly hewn wood across the bow. Now I had an unhindered access for my lens but the effects showed in my hindquarters when we returned.

The swift flowing river silently glided by till we made a turn into one of the channels. This channel was liberally studded with dead stumps of trees and jutting rocks, creating swiftly flowing rapids. Our boatman skilfully guided the boat as the sky rapidly lightened. A small village retreated swiftly and we were sailing inside a forest. Flocks of parakeets flew purposefully towards the village to feed on the ripening vegetable patches. Yellow footed green pigeons thronged the tree tops and Lesser Cormorants had already started their morning fishing. Suddenly over the chatter of birds and the gurgle of the river there rose a tremendous hooting noise as if a bunch of rowdy school kids had been let loose in the local sweet shop without the owner present. Hoolock Gibbons!!! They hooted, taunted, hollered, screeched and the whole place throbbed with their racket.

Soon the Hoolock Gibbons were left behind and the river wove in and out through the jungle. The tree stumps receded and the river became slightly placid in parts but the underlying current was unmistakable since the debris in the form of branches and leaves were carried down at an alarming rate of knots. I kept myself perched on the piece of wood taking pot shots at Pied Kingfishers, Crested Serpent Eagles and one Greater Cormorant with no success. We had a long way to go and slowing down for our feathered friends was not an option. That did not stop me from getting ecstatic and exclaim at regular intervals... ‘ohhhhh a Stork Billed Kingfisher’... ‘ahhhh a Black Headed Gull till a helicopter from one of the forward airbases flew in and one of my friends from the Land of the Five Rivers sat back comfortably on my rucksack and smugly uttered, ‘wowwwww a Rotating Headed Metal Bird’. That effectively stopped my ornithological expressions.

Around mid-morning we reached a forest guard camp. We disembarked to make enquiries about animal movements from the guards. Apparently, a small herd of elephants and a few wild buffaloes were roaming the vicinity of the camp. A look around from the top of the tower yielded nothing. By the time we came back to our boat, hunger was gnawing and it was time for breakfast.
Maggi and eggs.

The boat was cast off and we sailed on. The packets of Maggi and a crate of eggs were brought out. A can of kerosene appeared and finally out came what seemed like a stove. It looked like a stove and probably was one of the earliest versions invented by man. Our guide, who doubled up as the cook, bent down, placed the stove at the bottom, poured in the kerosene and started pumping. All of us except the boatman looked on, fascinated. When we were nearing a sand bar, our guide decided that the stove has been adequately pumped up and picked up a match stick and lighted it.

All I saw was a small ball of flame and a whooooosh sound. The boatman let go of the tiller and we headed straight for the sand bar. My Punjabi friends decided not to wait till landfall and promptly jumped overboard. They did not know swimming but fortunately we were almost at the sand bar and the depth was not much but the splash my friends created woke up the jungle. A whole flock of Fruit Bats took to the air from a tree overhanging the river. A Great Egret floating lazily up the river in front of us gave a honk of alarm and rose straight up towards the sky. A flock of Little Cormorants dived for safety. A Little Heron who was trying to disguise itself quite successfully as a branch of a tree, shot across the river at supersonic speed. I, being an exmariner, could not bring myself to desert my ship because of my years of shipboard training but the boatman cut off the engine and jumped. The boat slowed down because of the current of the river and beached with a hiss of crushing pebbles. The fire was burning in the bottom of the boat. All through this my guide sat calmly on the side sill of the boat and watched. Within minutes the fire burnt off the oil that was floating at the bottom of the boat. Miraculously, the kerosene inside the stove did not catch fire. It was almost an hour before we finished cooking our breakfast on the sand bar since all of us vehemently opposed the idea of our guide who still wanted to cook the meal while underway.

We set sail again with our belies full though a little shaken. A long stretch of dense forest for next two hours abruptly opened upon a tribal village nestled serenely on the banks of the river. Hordes of buffaloes were grazing or were being milked. Naked children ran along the bank crying in delight at our lenses. Small dugout boats were setting out for the long and tortuous journey to the village from where we had started to deliver a can or two of milk. Ladies were repairing fishing nets. The hustle and bustle of the village deep inside the forest contrasted beautifully with the serene surroundings of the meandering river, the surrounding forest and the peaks of the Eastern Himalayas.

We journeyed on but at one point our boat ran aground though the river looked wide. The boatman asked us to disembark to lighten the boat and walk some distance till the river was deep enough. One of my friends flatly refused. He was already badly shaken with the explosion and his dip in the river and did not want a stroll in the forest. He was right. My other friend, our guide and myself started the walk and it was strenuous to say the least. The river had created sand banks and each step in the midday sun was an effort. However, my generously built Punjabi friend relished the stroll and charged across the vast sand banks with stunted trees growing here and there till we came to a point where a vast herd of domestic buffaloes from the village were crossing too. My friend and the herd eyed each other suspiciously and finally the herd crossed keeping an eye cocked on my friend lest he made funny business. We walked almost five kilometres till our boat found enough water to navigate and we sailed on.
After scaring a flock of Great Cormorants on a sand bar we finally made landfall at an island where our feral horses were supposed to be residing as per reports of our guide and boatman.
Here it is pertinent to give a short note on these horses.

During World War II the Japanese surrendered after retreating. The allied soldiers were using horses to transport their men and material across the inhospitable terrain. When the war ended, the soldiers returned home but left the horses to fend for themselves.
They roamed these forests with the tiger, elephant and wild buffalo and slowly the genes of their forefathers took over. They became the only population of feral horses in Asia.

They were supposed to be nearby but a thorough sweep with our binoculars did not show any. We pushed on inside the island. The island was crisscrossed with rivers and semi dry river beds and the ground was sandy and sometimes muddy. After futilely plodding for quite some distance, our guide suggested that we split in two groups and search in a circle. My group had hardly gone fifty metres when frantic whistles broke out from the other group. A mad rush across the sands and dodging in and out of the Casuarina trees and we came out in the open on the other side.

In front lay a large expanse of semi dry river bed. The sun was high up and miniature sand storms swirled and danced across the damp earth and sandy soil. On the other side of the river bed stretched the stunted Casuarina trees, green, swaying in the hot breeze. The massive Himalayas sat in grand solitude....... and in the green haze of the Casuarinas, like a few flickers of flames, roamed the feral horses, their reddish coat gleaming in the sun. Their manes which had not been cared for by the hand of man were caressed by the breeze and the wild muscles rippled under the flaming fur. My tryst was complete.

After we had our fill of shots, I sat close to them without arousing suspicion, marvelling at the beauty and timelessness of man’s fascination with wild horses. Finally, they grew suspicious and galloped away into the horizon in a thunder of hooves. It was time to turn back. This is where I had the final icing on the cake so to speak.

We were walking back in a single file. Still drunk with my close encounter with the horses, I had lagged behind somewhat from the rest of the file. My camera felt heavy and sweat poured in profusion. My throat was parched and the swirling dust did not improve things. The sand under the feet felt heavier with every stride. That is why an even and solid looking patch of tightly packed mud looked inviting enough for me to try and take a shortcut. I confidently stepped on the patch and in a blink of an eye sank waist deep in oozing mud. I was not unduly perturbed but held my camera high to avoid getting it damaged and planted my other foot to balance myself and get out of the quagmire. This foot too went down and panic set in. I was sinking in the mud fast and in panic I lost my voice. Just when I thought all was lost and thoughts of my mortal remains remaining hidden in a far corner of North East India started flashing through in my brain my guide felt something wrong, and returned back. When I was extricated and sat on a firm piece of land, I looked and shivered at the black oozing mud which had by now replaced the solid looking patch because of my exertions and suddenly felt cold and shivered.

Death and exhilaration were my companions, going hand in hand on my quest for the feral horse. The experience was humbling. The majestic blue mountains and the vast river plains, the tribal Mishings ferrying their milk in dugouts and the logs being carried down the deep blue river, the smell of the damp hot earth and the flaming horses all pointed to the fact that how small I was in front of my mother.... nature.

chapters.title: Love and Football in Mexico
paragraphs: The port of Guaymas is located on the western coast of Mexico. Surrounded by barren mountains, the small port town is quite picturesque. M.V. Evpo Ageos sailed in on a September morning just as the hills were losing the early morning glow. The little town lay placidly hemmed in between the blue bay and the brown knolls.

The clearance was given with utmost efficiency and we started discharging our cargo. The speed at which the cargo was being discharged was quite surprising. Long lines of trucks hummed around the ship. It resembled a beehive where the ship was the queen bee and the trucks came and went like the workers. The port resounded with the sound of trucks revving their engines, the vessel’s derricks whined as they manoeuvred the cargo from the cargo holds to the trucks, bulldozers and wove in and out of the trucks gathering any spilt cargo, stevedores argued with the workers, the deck officers argued with the stevedores over discharging procedures and an air of brisk activity was felt everywhere.

That evening I went out to the town. A small Mexican town has a charm which cannot be equalled by any other. The white washed buildings stood in perfect harmony under the streetlights. The neat narrow lanes were humming with men, women and children as if the whole town was out for a party.

Sombreros and moustaches jostled with tight fitting jeans and loose tops. Cars passed with people sounding the horns and playing saxophones simultaneously. In some parks there were big crowds listening to impromptu bands belting out sentimental Spanish songs. The smell of tortillas and tacos wafted in the warm breeze. Children ran around in the parks laughing and shouting.

That night when I was returning to the ship full of pizza thickly smeared with cheese and generous helpings of ham (none of the fancy Pizza Hut or Dominos overpriced ham flakes) and jalapeno and baked in a proper oven, I felt a twinge of remorse that we would be leaving this paradise soon..... at the most another day or less since the discharging was going at a fast rate.

I fell asleep listening to the sound of the heaving derricks. When I opened my eyes the next morning the silence was all pervasive. There was no sound except for the one coming from the ship’s generators in the engine room. I looked out of the porthole... the derricks were lying lifeless on the deck. The port which was swarming with men and machinery the previous night was as deserted as the Gobi Desert. When I met the duty officer who was the 3rd Officer, he told me that around five in the morning during Chief Mate’s watch all the workers just left the vessel in a hurry and the trucks departed. The stevedore’s representative also departed in a hurry without any explanation. So here we were with the ship still with cargo in a Mexican port without knowing what to do or where to go.

Around mid-day a car sped down and halted at our gangway. The agent ran up and informed the Captain that a revolution has broken out somewhere and all work had to be stopped since the trucks could not get through. Having delivered this piece of news he departed promptly as if in a hurry to join the revolution.

So that was that.... we were stuck in Guaymas and we could do nothing about it. We fortunately had enough supply of fuel to run the generators and enough food and water to last about fifteen to eighteen days. Nobody really minded the total lack of activity. The duty officers manned their stations during their hours of watch. The crew did their regular maintenance jobs. By five in the evening everything stopped and only the duty officer and engineer manned the vessel. The rest of us were out playing football.

Since the jetty was deserted and we were the only ship alongside, we had a free run of the port. At five in the evening almost the full complement of officers and crew descended on the jetty. I had bought a football from Guatemala. Two portable lightweight goalposts were fabricated in the engine room. Nets were woven out of nylon lines used for passing the ship’s ropes to tugs.

The football game was the high point of daily activity. Everybody looked forward to the game. Even the Captain, who was well advanced in years, agreed to act as the referee. The glistening blue bay surrounded by the bare hills acted as a stunning backdrop to the deserted jetty which acted as our stadium. However, any spectator coming down to watch the game would have been shocked at the spectacle of a bunch of sailors from Greece, India, Burma playing football in immaculate shorts and jerseys with a portly old Greek gentleman rolling about with a whistle in his mouth which was our Captain Vrassidas Georgakis. Among all these sportsmanlike footballers there was one footballer prancing about in his underwear. That was the Bosun.

The spectator would have to watch for some time to understand the reason behind this particular act of depravity. At some point of time an over enthusiastic kick from one of the players would result in the ball sailing across the parapet of the jetty and land in the sea. Our Bosun was an ex diver from the Navy. It was his job to dive into the water to retrieve the ball. Then he would throw the ball to us from the sea, swim across to the other side of the ship and climb up the gangway and descend from the shore side gangway and re-join the fray like a dripping stocky mermaid without fins. This went on well for quite a few days till somebody disagreed on some finer points of the game with the Bosun and in a fit of fury threw his brand-new Lee jeans into the water after which, the Bosun refused to take part in retrieving the ball anymore and the game came to a halt.

The weather was decidedly hot when we arrived in Guaymas. It remained so when the game was being played. It was during one of these days when I had gone ashore in the evening after a particularly disastrous match where I had scored a same side goal through no fault of mine. I had deftly passed the ball to the goalkeeper without noticing that the goalkeeper was emptying his bladder into the sea behind the goalpost. The goalkeeper, to his credit, was keeping an eye on the ball and helplessly watched it cross the goal line. Later on, when an irate 3rd Engineer, who was the Captain of my team confronted me, I cleverly pointed out that my goalkeeper should have been in position to receive my pass instead of polluting the sea but the goalie vehemently defended his right to de-ballast and there was a stalemate.

Be that as it may, I was in a despondent mood when I reached the town. All the running, arguing and the walk to the town had made me thirsty. There was a fruit juice shop opposite a park and the pineapples and oranges looked appealing enough to pay a visit. I entered and reached the counter when I was hit by the smile of Alma.

She was a diminutive girl who manned the counter or rather, womanned the counter. Her five feet frame was lit up by a smile which could have launched a thousand ships. Mexican ladies are in general, extremely pretty but she was the prettiest thing I had seen in twenty-five years of my existence at that time. Her brown long hair framed a perfectly oval face which became luminescent when she smiled and she greeted me with that smile and a perfect voiced ‘hola’. Cupid released his arrow. That perfectly petite slightly upturned nose, liquid bluish green eyes and a well- endowed figure made sure that the arrow hit hard.

1 did not know any Spanish during that time and usually got along fine by doing mimics to talk to people who did not know English. My mimic deserted me and I think I stood gaping without knowing exactly why I came inside. She said something which bypassed my ear. I would not have understood anyway but in my state of stupor she might have said something in Bengali and still I would not have listened. Sensing my awkwardness, she picked up a pineapple and dangled it in front of my eyes and I came back to my senses. I nodded my head in agreement and was shortly seated a few meters from her holy presence with a big glass of pineapple juice. This was followed with a few other glasses of other fruit juices till I ran out of money. When I walked back to my ship I was in a state of daze.

Next afternoon I just clenched my teeth and somehow managed to finish the game of football before rushing back to my cabin to get dressed. I do not remember exactly how I reached the juice shop but I am sure I was floating on air. I entered the shop and that smile hit me anew right in the middle of the solar plexus. This time I had come with enough money to last me through half a tropical fruit shop but alas my stomach could not hold more than four jumbo glasses. This time I picked up her name from her conversation. Alma. Later I got to know her full name. Alma Hernandez.

Next evening, I was back and this time she did not even ask me what I wanted. Pineapple juice... followed by grape.... another glass of orange and lastly a glass of strawberry milkshake. She placed them one after the other as I sipped and each time that smile broke my heart in a thousand pieces. In between attending to her customers, she shot me glances and each time | reeled as if thunderstruck. She even came up and said something in Spanish and I just grinned sheepishly without daring to talk lest she be riled by my absence of language skills.

When we had arrived in Guaymas, the weather was hot. However, within a week of us getting stuck the mercury took a steep dive. Suddenly the evenings started feeling chilly and a jacket had to be worn. Bosun still leapt into the water with the alacrity of a Gannet diving after a tuna. This was prior to his loss of his Lee jeans. The game was still being played with vim. However, the physical necessity of drinking juice from seven to nine every evening diminished drastically. However, cupid kept up his relentless barrage of arrows and I dutifully arrived at Alma’s counter every evening and departed around nine like a man possessed. She took all my dumb adulation with ease and never showed signs of picking up one of the heavy steel fruit mashers and bringing it down on my head. Rather she tried to speak to me a few times but still Spanish eluded me. After downing innumerable glasses of pineapples, strawberries, grapes and oranges liberally dosed with ice over a period of about 4 days after the chill set in I went down with a severe bout of bronchitis. Getting sick on a ship is never a pleasant experience to say the least what with no Doctors and the man in charge of cargo operations i.e. the Chief Mate or the man in charge of navigation ive. the 2nd Mate doing the duties of a doctor by referring to a medical book.

Being alongside I availed of the luxury of consulting a doctor. Only problem was that he was Mexican. Hence our exchanges were made in English from my side and Spanish from him. After the ‘consultation’ we shook hand departed with mutual appreciation of our linguistic skills and precious else. True that he gave me some medicines but since I did not know when or how much to partake of the medication, I prudently desisted from having any. As such I was quarantined for the next two days to my bunk in my cabin. On the third day I was back at the Juice shop.

There was no sign of Alma and an old wizened gentleman had taken her place. Fortunately, to the credit of the old gentleman, he knew a smattering of English. He informed me that Alma had taken the day off. I returned to the ship a dejected man.

The Chief Mate Agnel Pereira, was from Goa. He was in the South America run for most of his sailing career. He saw me smoking a lonely cigarette at two in the morning in the Aft deck. To his persistent queries I finally blurted out my secret. He was most sympathetic to my cause.

He nodded his balding head in sympathy. ‘Ask her when you meet her tomorrow donde estaba usted ayer?’ “What does that mean? Promise me that I will not be bashed up if I say that to her’, I was a bit hesitant still not sure that the Mate was not pulling one on me.
‘Listen, I have spent half my life in these places. I know my Spanish.... go ahead and ask her’.
So, the next day, armed with my first Spanish sentence, I dutifully turned up at her shop and there she was, standing in all her radiant beauty, dispensing orange juice.

‘Donde estaba usted ayer?’, I blurted out as soon as she caught my attention. Having said what I had to say, I was about to duck behind the counter to avoid getting slapped when she smiled the most heavenly smile and told me something in Spanish. The old gentleman of the previous evening was hanging around nearby and he came to my rescue. He translated Alma’s Spanish. It turned out that I had asked her that where she had been yesterday. Presumably she had some urgent shopping and had taken the day off. She chatted with me through the old man and I probably downed a few more glasses of various juices than my usual quota. She came to see me off at the door of the shop during closing time.

I walked back or rather, floated back, to my ship in ecstasy. Chief Mate was doing something at the gangway and I reported my progress. He was delighted and promised to accompany me the next day. Then he hit on a brilliant idea.
“Well, 2nd October is coming up day after tomorrow. Ask her out for dinner on that day, since it is your birthday’, he told with the gleam of a veteran of many battles in his eyes.
‘Tcan’t...... I don’t understand Spanish and she does not understand English.... and if you think that I am going with that old gentleman in tow for a date, forget it’.

After a ponderous pause the Mate volunteered to accompany me. ‘I can go as your interpreter, considering that it was me who broke the ice between you two. However, you shall have to foot the bill provided she agrees to go.” I agreed with alacrity. The next day evening the Mate and I arrived at the holy place and promptly Agnel started a conversation. He spoke rapidly in Spanish and repeatedly pointed towards me. I hoped fervently that he was saying something nice about me. She looked at me and giggled which almost made me swoon. She had agreed.

The next 48 hours were spent in agonizing ecstasy. I probably lost a few pounds in weight too. Finally, the date and time arrived. Accompanied by Agnel, I reached a little before closing time. Agnel suggested that we hit a disco (apparently Alma loved to dance like any other Mexican) before dinner to work up an appetite and we walked to the nearest night club.

Now I love to watch dancing to a certain extent but my expertise in the practical field is extremely limited to say the least. If I had tried, I could have given Fred Astaire a run for his money but I seem to have been born with two left feet. Moreover, Iam slightly allergic to high decibel levels and this is what hit me like a sledgehammer as soon as we entered the club.

A huge stage was lit up with psychedelic lights and the music that was blaring out was enough to wake the dead. People were dancing in frenzy and | reeled at the prospect of dancing in that melee. However, with a bit of prodding from Alma and Agnel I stepped on the floor. My first manoeuvre caught somebody in the shin bone. I smiled apologetically but was soon prodded in the midriffs by a hefty elbow. Next moment I trod on Alma’s toes and ran out of steam. The only way out was to get a couple of stiff ones to patch up my shattered morale. I left the dance floor while the mob kept on their movements. Alma kept on dancing.

I walked up to the bar like a Bedouin walks up to the nearest oasis. The bartender immediately understood my need for a strong one and before long I was staring down at a shot glass containing a clear fluid and slice of lemon perched on the rim. I was sure that I was not served water and presumed that it must be vodka or white rum but such a small quantity surprised me. I was waiting for some water when the bartender asked me to drink up straight from the glass. Hmm.... so, this must be something different and I am always game for something different. So, I raised the glass and gingerly took a sip. The liquid marched down my throat spreading fire all through my oesophagus and I choked. A kindly gentleman saw me in distress and beckoned me to lick on the lemon and then a bit of salt on the rim. I did as directed and immediately felt better. Tequila started the magical work straight away and I ordered one more shot.

Finishing the second shot started changing my outlook and the noise seemed heavenly. Alma waved at me and I waved back a carefree hand and ordered a third. After the third I do not remember much but was later informed that I had ordered a couple of more. The rest was dizzy and I came back to my senses... or whatever was left of it, the next morning staring at the ceiling of my cabin. Agnel told me that it was fortunate that I did not weigh much (many years back, mind you) and the crew did not have much trouble hauling me up the gangway on our return. Alma had a blast and we did have some dinner though I do not remember much of it. Agnel had footed the bill while I half slept in a Tequila laden dream. She was dropped off at her home and then both of us had returned to the ship.

The most devastating part was that, while I was busy in the juice centre and then with dancing and getting introduced to Tequila, the chaps who were conducting the revolution had run out of steam and had withdrawn dastardly. The unloading was underway in full swing with double the speed. We had to sail out that evening for Ecuador and then on to Nigeria. There was no way I could go ashore anymore.

At seven that evening, the moon rose over the Bay. Evpo Ageos cast away all her lines and sailed out never to go back to that magical small town where a young sailor had for the first time lost his heart. The lights of the city diminished and the moonlit water broke into thousands of fragments like a heart shattered by Tequila.

P.S. My wife of nineteen years knows about this and still clenches her teeth whenever I sing "Down in the West Mexican town of El Paso, I fell in love with a little Mexican girl"... I somehow tend to forget that in the original version of the song, the words were West Texan town and not my version of Mexican town. Well... women will be women...

chapters.title: Shining India
paragraphs: I believe in miracles. I never did. I do now after visiting Chandaka Wildlife Sanctuary. There is no other way of explaining Chandaka's continuing existence as a wildlife sanctuary.

Just about twenty kilometres from capital Bhubaneshwar, the city is knocking at the door of the forest. The miracle part of it is that, it is only knocking but has not broken open that door...yet. There is a massive engineering college under construction at a shouting distance from the entrance gate at Deras. All the agricultural land almost right up to the gate has got signboards dangling on bamboo poles advertising the fact that the piece of agricultural land belongs to 'Forest View Apartments’, ‘Green Heights’, Nature Villa Housing’ and so on. Empty bottles of Old Monk bob up and down in the clear waters of the Deras Dam. The waterfront in the front portion of the dam is liberally sprinkled with discarded Lays packets and plastic lunch boxes. The calm of the jungle is shattered by groups of young boys and girls from adjoining engineering colleges coming in for a spot of 'fun' and raunchy songs and yodelling ring out across the clumps of swaying bamboo fronds. The highly understaffed forest department staff do not have enough resources or the will to stop the picnic. In the forest rest house, nightlong parties are held ‘The whole of the sanctuary is hemmed in between four large cities, Bhubaneshwar, Cuttack, Khurda and Banke and the only escape route is sealed by the mighty Mahanadi river. Yet this could not stop half the elephant population of Chandaka to escape to the more bountiful Atgarh Sanctuary from this slowly tightening noose of civilization. At night, from the forest rest house, the horizon glows red from the lights of the city.

Yet... and yet, when the sun sets in a burst of red and darkness envelops the denizens of the forest in a veil of comfort and hides them from the unseeing eyes of man, a slight scraping sound comes from the undergrowth in front of the forest bungalow. A little while later the small Kotra (Mouse Deer) voices its timid call from the banks of the dam. The dead leaves of the Sal trees littering the forest floor is trampled upon by elephants and the Chital stag's mating call pierces the veil. In the tribal villages dotted inside the park, the dark faces of the old men flicker in the communal fire as they tell stories to the little children of the days when India's national animal roamed these hills and valleys before being shot to extinction by Indians. The first rays of the morning sun are still welcomed by the calls of the India's national bird. We have failed to exterminate them. Yet.

Chandaka Dampara Wildlife Sanctuary covers an area of 192 square kilometres. Bamboo and sal trees are most abundant. There are quite a few tribal huts within the sanctuary. Though half the elephant population has migrated, the other half remains, along with the Chital Deer, Mouse Deer, Barking Deer, Hyena, Jackal, Wild Boars, Common Langur, Rhesus Macaques and even a few leopards - though the latter have rarely been spotted. ‘Ihere was one crocodile in the Ambilo water hole but it has not been spotted of late and therefore it can be inferred that it has either migrated to some other water hole or migrated to the next world while trying to do so. There are reportedly a couple of them still left in Kumarkhunti water hole. It also harbours a considerable number of Peacocks, Jungle Fowl, forest birds and some migratory birds as well. Butterflies are found in abundance. The soil is reddish in colour and gives the sanctuary a lovely feel.

And yet... amidst this bounty of nature, the oppressive thought keeps recurring in my mind... how long...? Just how long...? Five years... six...? It doesn't matter. It will not be very long before these Engineering Colleges will produce brilliant engineers who will no doubt build the future of India. Those lovely apartments at the fringe of the sanctuary will rise tall above the bamboo fronds and the tribal huts and there will be beautiful people in beautiful SUVs parked in those garages. Nice parties will be held in the lake fronts where the apartment owners will drink scotch as a toast to the prosperity of the nation. Some of them will clandestinely ask a passing tribal whether a nice piece of Spotted Deer leg might be procured at a price — ‘just for the taste you know...’ India will be shining with GDP.

Among all this shining and luxury, somewhere in the forest floor, the dust will be blown by the wind and settle down again but jumbo's footprints will no longer be imprinted. The sal leaves will dry up in winter and be carried on the breeze but the elephants crunching footsteps on them will no longer be heard.

‘The Chital and the Barking Deer will still voice their alarm calls for some more time till they are also gone but the trumpeting of the herd will fall silent. The bamboo fronds will still sway and whisper to each other when the wind gusts in spring, laden with fragrance but those large ears will not flap to listen to the whispers... Will the whispers turn into tears? The peacocks will still display his tail as the storm clouds gather over the red earth promising to turn the forest into a sea of green but jumbo won't be there to delight in those showers. And then one day the peacock will display his feathers no more.

My India will be shining but at what cost, and for how long? If you love what India has stood for, for thousands of years, a deep respect of nature and its inhabitants before being swamped by Malls and Bollywood, visit Chandaka before we manage to turn it into a neighbourhood park with crows instead of peacocks and rats instead of the mouse deer.

chapters.title: The Last of the Dooars
paragraphs: A Sigma 150-500 with a Nikon D90 body is not exactly a lightweight contraption to carry on elephant back, especially if there is nothing to hold on to except for a jute sack thrown on the behemoth's back. However, I decided to take it along when going on an elephant ride inside the jungle so that a rhino or elephant won't escape my lens even at a distance. Half an hour into the elephant ride and my elephant almost tripped over a pair of rhinos inside a dense undergrowth. At a range of about 15 feet, neither the rhinos nor my elephant was prepared to budge and there was a stalemate. At this critical juncture, my big lens flatly refused to focus on such a huge subject at so short a distance and all I came out with were a few shots of grey blur which had to be identified as rhinos and not pieces of boulder. In despair I almost threw away my gear.

Next day better sense prevailed and I carried with me my 18-105 lens. This time one of my fellow travellers did not find it comfortable riding piggyback on an elephant with no railings to hold his bulk and demanded he be taken back after the elephant ride had started. By the time elephant ride was resumed, the sun had begun to tilt noticeably. After watching great swathes of grassland and dense forest without visible sign of wildlife (though there was plenty of audible signs) we turned back. The sun was down and gloom was settling in with heavy clouds gathering on the slopes of Bhutan Mountains as the glens reverberated with the plaintive cries of the peacocks. As we approached the Murti river, the grass in front of us parted to reveal a full herd of about forty Gaurs. My camera focused and this time I came up with a load of brown blobs which had to be identified in person as Gaurs. In that gathering gloom there was no chance of getting a single decent photograph of the herd at a distance of about 30 feet. I still do not know how I managed to restrain myself from throwing down my gear.

I was not particularly asleep since the rat-infested wooden cottage with a thatched roof built on a single concrete pillar, did not have electricity. North Bengal in the month of September can be a little sultry.

My son and daughter were fast asleep when a great commotion broke outside my cottage with somebody shouting and the sound of a heavy body rushing. My wife being next to the window, got up and looked out of the window and excitedly whispered to me "elephant". I rushed to the window and peered out. The pitch darkness outside was pierced by a wildly flailing torchlight. "Where is the elephant?" My wife took my head in her hands and turned it right in front of our cottage and there below me was one of the most astonishing sights I had ever witnessed.

A female elephant, with its trunk outstretched in its front like a scimitar and the tail outstretched behind her like a flag, was running madly towards the forest from the paddy field in front of us and was being pursued by an old man with a small torch, hollering for all he was worth. The man chased the scared elephant into the forest but it returned behind my bungalow and spent the rest of the night destroying the bamboo grove about 25 feet from my rear window.
Welcome to Kalipur, part of the Gorumara National Park, Media Range, Budhuram Beat near the Indo Bhutan border in Northern West Bengal.

chapters.title: Disco Dancer in a Remote Indonesian Village
paragraphs: Where is Perawang? Nobody had a clue. The closest landmark we were told was the city of Pekanbaru in Indonesia. A frantic search of the charts did not reveal the port. We were discharging in Kakogawa in Japan and had orders to sail to Nanjing, China to load for Perawang. There were a few Indonesian ships docked near our vessel and a visit to one of them revealed the location. We even managed to get a photocopy of a chart and sailed out in due course.

Loading in Nanjing was just routine since Nanjing was almost like our home port. We were almost sailing up and down the Hwang Ho once every one and a half months. This time we loaded boiler parts for some sugar factory in Indonesia. After spending about seven days in the port of Nanjing and duly consuming a vast array of delicious Chinese village food (the port of Nanjing used to be in the middle of a village by the river, surrounded by vast paddy fields and small villages) and Tsingtao, we sailed for Perawang near Pekanbaru, Sumatra, Indonesia.

South China Sea in the month of July was as beautifully terrifying as ever and we even managed to catch a glimpse of the sun a couple of days. Our old lady did a stately eight to nine knots and the days when we reached ten, the Captain held a party. The rolling and pitching sent a few of the experienced old sailors to their bunks in a nasty shade of green due to sea sickness. The sea foamed and spat and remained white with froth. The sky remained blue black throughout till we cleared South China Sea.

Storm at sea - image from internet Finally, one fine sunlit warm morning, the Pilot boarded M.V. Pratapgad at the mouth of the Kuala Siak river. We left the sea and entered the realm of a tropical paradise. Those days the radio room used to be shut after entering territorial waters for secrecy purposes. In Pratapgad, my cabin had to be accessed through the radio room. So, the radio room was open but all radio traffic ceased. Subratada, my senior Radio Officer and I picked our hammocks and headed for the boat deck. No sooner had we hooked up our hammocks, the Electrical Officer and Chief Officer came sauntering in with theirs. Radio Room of a ship We had our hammocks tied up under the lifeboat for a bit of shade. The sun shone as it should in a tropical paradise in the summer months but a breeze blew down the meandering river. The Pilot had already boarded and we were underway. The colour of the river rapidly changed from the turquoise blue of the river mouth to the colour of a rich Calypso rum. The river narrowed fast and soon was just the width of our ship with a few meters to spare. The river bank was covered in thick forest and glowed iridescent in the sunlight. The trees were so close that one could throw a stone and it would land in the forest. ‘I'he slight breeze was warm and thick with tropical smells mixed with the oily smell of the ship. The engine hummed as Pratapgad made her sedate way and handled the sharp river bends with aplomb. Some beer bottles lazed around with us in various stages of consumption in spite of being warm (we did not have freezer in cabins those days). From time to time we came across quaint villages in small clearings by the river. From the expressions of the villagers, who rushed to the bank to stare at us in awe, it was apparent that not too many ships went that way. The elders smiled and talked excitedly among themselves and the women watched demurely from the shadows of their thatched huts while the children were more boisterous in showing their interest. Passing by the rainforest - image source internet It was almost late in the mid-day when we rounded a bend in the river and further upstream lay another small village. This village had a small bathing pier which led into the water. From our position the village lay simmering in the lazy heat, nestled among the trees, bereft of any signs of life except... except that somebody was taking a bath. From the figure at a distance it looked like a lady in an extremely short flaming red piece of cloth which barely covered anything. Now, we had been sailing a long time and hardly seen a member of the fairer sex except in the only on-board video. Within seconds word spread like wild fire and the lower deck below us thronged with the crew. Our deck remained relatively less populated except for the addition of the 3rd Engineer. Above us was the wheelhouse or bridge where only the Captain, Pilot, Deck Cadet and the Able Seaman or Sukhani in Indian shipping parlance remained.

We approached the village and all hell broke loose. From below deck the cat calls and wolf whistles could rival any side alley of Southampton harbour in the early days of steam navigation. It grew rapidly in volume as we neared the pier but the lady kept on bathing. Our Chief Mate, Sanjay Nighojkar and Electrical Officer, Mangat joined in the whistling. 3rd Engineer Nagaraj too tried his non-existent whistling skills. Subratada and I refrained since we were more on family terms and I was the junior most. Suddenly a wolf whistle rang out from the bridge. Now, the Pilot was an old wizened man resembling a dried walnut and was used to travelling up and down the river regularly. The Sukhani and the Cadet was most unlikely to start whistling at bathing beauties in the presence of the Captain. That left out only the Captain and we were rather awed by his virility in his advancing years. The excitement reached its peak when we came abreast the pier and then the mermaid turned around silencing all on board. The cat calls left the cats and the wolf whistles abandoned the wolves. The mermaid was actually a slender man who was quite fair with very little body hair and a very flowing shock of hair running right up to his waist. His slit eyes were opened as wide as was possible for him to open as he gazed in amazement at a bunch of very oily, smelly and quite a few hirsute sailors who stared back in equal if not more amazement. The collective sigh of disappointment could perhaps be heard across the Java Sea.

The sun started setting. We stopped the engine, anchored in the middle of the river and settled down for the night. The sky almost blew up with colours. A bunch of clouds ran helter skelter across the tropical sky and the rum coloured river deepened in colour. Parrots streaked across the sky like fiery arrows and soon the starlit sky hung motionless over our ship flanked on both sides by the dark forest. The sound of the generators deep within the belly of the ship faded when I walked to the foc’sle and only a deep silence pervaded.

Sunset on the river - image source internet Late next morning we reached our port. It is difficult to call a jetty with two cranes and a warehouse, “port” but whatever the shortcomings of Pekanbaru as a port, the surroundings made up with room to spare. By the time the port formalities were over and unloading operations started it was time to stop operations. Pekanbaru had the delightful system of stopping work as soon as the sun was about to set. Subrata da and I, not having anything to do with unloading or loading operations, set out as the sun was about to set. Light was fading as we left the port through the non- existent gate and walked out onto a broken road.

A short walk brought us to the highway. “Highway” might be too grandiose a term. It was metalled road which came up to the river and stopped. On the other side the road continued somewhere inside the jungle. A small ferry was parked at the riverside which took any cars or trucks that came up to the river and wished to cross, across. It could just about fit one truck but I never saw it being overburdened with two, In our stay of about two weeks the ferry hardly made more than four to five trips a day. However, being a ferry wharf as well as being adjacent to a “port”, this place was of great importance and a small village consisting of about thirty huts had sprung up in the middle of the jungle. The port electrician stayed there, the crane operator stayed there, the ferry Captain stayed there...in fact all the population of Perawang stayed in those thirty huts. To serve the trucks and the twenty odd cars which came down to cross the river, there were four small shops which served as the local bar, restaurant, candy shop, in short, a miniature Indonesian village shopping mall. Indonesia was a more permissible Islamic country and having a beer or two was not frowned or kicked upon and beer was available.

Perawang port at present - the extended jetty was built later - image source internet The problem was, the beer was not cold. Subratada and I entered one of the shops and were warmly greeted by the whole family who ran the shop which included the grandmother, the middle-aged couple, their son and the two daughters Eti and Ellie. A bowl of fresh roasted peanuts and two cans of beer were served. However, in that tropical heat, drinking warm beer was not the best of refreshments. There was no electricity in the village and the huts and the shop were lit by storm lanterns.

Hence, getting a cold beer was out of question...or so we thought. As I cringed with the first gulp of the warm amber fluid, a big bowl of ground ice, which was kept in a sort of thermocol box, appeared. A handful of ice in a glass and the golden elixir on top of it and voila.... we had ice cold beer. True that the zing lessened a bit but as events unfolded...who cares.

As it became dark, the jungle loomed around us. We were the only customers in the shop. Fireflies started their dance as the storm lanterns were lit. The cranes of our ship in the distance fell silent as the workers wrapped up for the day. The lanterns in the huts of the village were lit and the liquid yellow light filtered through the tree trunks. The proprietors of our shop started a charcoal fire at the back of the shop. Soon we were enveloped in a delicious aroma of Satay Kambings and Satay Ayams, the wonderful kebabs typical of Indonesia. In a short while the workers returned from the port and like shadows disappeared in their huts. Children laughed; voices of families mingled with those of the myriads of crickets. The lanterns, which were hung outside the shops, soon attracted moths and the fiery orbs of the lantern flames soon resembled Kandahar surrounded by Apaches of the US Marines.

Soon the ship’s officers and crew too trickled in. The steaming piles of rice and satay arrived. The yellow light of the lanterns glistened on the kebabs. They were served with a beautifully balanced peanut sauce and an array of fresh pickles which looked more like a pile of colours than food. Dazzling red of red chillies, delicate green of cucumbers, fresh green and yellow herbs all vied with each other for attention and they got it. Having had to withstand the onslaught of our Ship’s cook’s culinary skills or the lack of it, where the same masala was used for cooking dal, spinach and lamb in equal amounts, we were almost starving.

Slowly the families of the village gathered in the shops. It seemed that not too many Indian ships had called at the small jetty but conversation though slightly difficult due to the absence of a common tongue (English is not a language which is understood easily inside a rainforest in Indonesia), nevertheless had a common topic as we soon found out.

It was Bollywood and Mithun, our own Bollywood hero, was at the peak of his career. They knew most of the Bollywood films and even the songs though the way they pronounced the Hindi words, it was sometimes a bit tough to understand but the tunes were spot on. When enough beer was downed and the pile of satays diminished somewhat mainly due to the strenuous efforts of our Electrical Officer Mangat, a couple of guitars came out and even an improvised drum made of bamboo and plastic. Mangat took out his handkerchief and fastened it on his forehead in the iconic style of our Mithun dada and started to sway to the song of “I am a disco dancer”.

As if on cue, the heavens opened up, lightning flashed and rain poured down in buckets. The weather immediately fell by a few degrees. The lanterns swayed in the wind reflecting the raindrops creating a strange psychedelic effect. Mangat danced away to glory in the middle of a rainforest, cheered on by a group of Indonesians and Indians, two of them strumming guitars and one a makeshift drum while we sang I am a disco dancer, Papa kehte hain, Tum se milkar...and many more making an effort to raise our voice above the splattering of raindrops. When we returned, the rain was drizzle, the road was slushy but the hearts were full and so were our tummies.

For the next two weeks this became a ritual with the guitars, drums, more singing, lanterns and fireflies, the looming rainforest and the rain which arrived almost magically around the same time. The only day the ritual was broken was the day Subratada and I took a car belonging to our agent and went for a trip to Pekanbaru, the nearest town where the only two things I remember are the first pair of jeans I bought with my salary and the fiery hot chicken wings which left us salivating and perspiring at the same time. The other thing I remember from those two weeks is my morning walks along the banks of the river, the sky brightening and the sun coming up above the treeline. The warm air, for there were no rains in the mornings, carrying with it the fragrances of the surrounding forests, the birds skimming above the honey coloured water, the young chap, the Captain of the half derelict ferry, resplendent in his fiery red or green sarong, greeting me from his deck and inviting me over for a cup of tea and some fresh boiled eggs and bananas.

chapters.title: Vodka, Apple and a Bulldozer in Siberia
paragraphs: Vanino or Vaninsky is a port lying on the eastern seaboard of Russia on the Sea of Okhotsk. In the month of August, it was just about pleasant during daytime when M.V. Nirmal Bhushan arrived alongside.

Siberia during summer is visually charming. The bay was blue with rippling waves. The coast was verdant. We were portside alongside. A short distance away a railway line disappeared inside thickly forested slopes of the undulating countryside. The city could be seen further away on the forward side. Solid and stocky Soviet built houses adorned the gentle slopes and a square was visible mainly because of an imposing statue of Lenin who had escaped Glasnost and Perestroika in this corner of the republic.

After shore passes were issued, 3rd Engineer Halder and myself walked down the gangway and after showing our yellow coloured passes to the heavily armed, imposing guard, who was still wearing the Red Army insignia of the hammer and sickle, made our way through the forested hillside to the city. It was quite a long walk. Distance in Russia can be very deceptive as many invaders have found to their cost have found most Russian or ex-Soviet cities to be fascinating.

They have a certain aura of timelessness. Vanino was no exception. The roads were smooth and straight and well shaded by trees. The residential blocks looked as if they were built to withstand ten Goerings and Wehrmachts. The traffic lacked the oomph or Dubai... the glitz of Manhattan or the mystery of Penang but had its own charm with suitably sturdy and oldworld cars moving around like portly old gentlemen who had seen better days. The parks looked immaculately green and the little children flitted in them like multicoloured butterflies. There were huge stores selling almost nothing other than some severe looking winter clothing, guitars and boots which were made for Goliaths. The weather, as we hit the town, was crisp and beautiful albeit a little cold, being peak summer.

Jalways love visiting local markets and Russian ones are even more exotic. In Vanino, the local market was just opposite the large store where hardly anything was stored. The clean road was crowded with buyers and sellers. Neatly stacked bunches of strawberries lay side by side with natty prunes. Ridiculously cheap bottles of Russian variety of Champagne were being sold by moustached gentlemen who exhaled tobacco fumes from well used pipes clenched between nicotine stained sturdy teeth. Plump elderly women with brightly coloured head scarves fought diligent battles with young men over prices of even plumper pears. The apples were almost as red as the lips of beautiful young women with flowing golden tresses. Nice fresh boiled eggs were for sale with multicoloured bottles of beer which were cheaper than water. Fresh cherries were piled high and were so cheap that I thought that they might as well pay me for buying a few bunches.

A railway station was at the end of the roadside market and looked like one of those Wild West stations, though more crowded. The people crowding the platform did not seem like passengers. There were no trains and everybody seemed to be chatting and laughing and shouting at the same time. It was just a nice big meeting place and there were no gates or any ticket checkers around. Just next to the ticket counter, which was shuttered and closed, there was a canteen. In Russia, almost everything is large and the canteen was no exception. It could house two cinema halls and huge windows with stained glass with the sun filtering through. Not many people were inside since everybody was worshipping the sun outside. We bought a few thick slices of salami, some rough rye bread and a couple of large bottles of beer and sat in a corner chatting. Time passed and we failed to notice that the sunlight filtering through the stained glass had grown considerably dim.

When we emerged from the canteen, the sky was overcast and the wind had picked up considerably. We made our way to the gate of the port as fast as possible. From the gate to our ship was half an hour’s walking distance through thickly forested hillsides. There was no traffic and we were the only hikers on the road. The rain started with a pitter-patter but soon started to show signs of turning into a deluge. When in Siberia, even during peak summer, you do not want to be caught out in the rain inside a forest, especially if you are from a country near the equator. Each drop was sharp as needle and painfully cold. Our ship was still miles away but up the slope a small log hut caught our attention.

We clambered up the sloping road and soon stood inside the shade of the awning. It was certainly not a palace being a roughly hewed stack of sturdy wood held together by nails and surrounded by huge pines and oaks which cast a deeper shadow than the cloudy overcast sky. However, it kept us relatively dry but now the rain had started in earnest and the awnings could not stop the splatter entirely. Both of us herded towards the door when loud voices and huge sounds of laughter emanated from inside the cabin. We did not waste any time and knocked loudly at the door.

The laughter stopped and somebody shouted in Russian followed by another volley. Since our knowledge of Russian was quite limited, Halder shouted back in Bengali and immediately the door was thrown open. A short stout man in frayed jeans, knee length boots and a heavy overcoat stood looking at us inquisitively.

‘Indi?’, he asked when he sized us up and when we nodded in the affirmative, we were immediately ushered in with the greatest of bonhomie. There was a single room and an attached toilet at the end of a very short passage. A huge log table stood in the middle of the room which was creaking at the seams with the weight of what looked like a tonne of bottles and glasses. The table was surrounded by equally heavy wooden chairs and on the chairs sat Russians... five of them excluding our genial host.

However, our host was the only one who looked anything human.... the rest of the gang were Russian to the core. They overflowed from every corner... the room seemed to be overflowing with muscles and boots. There was a large French window on the other side of the room. The darkness was accentuated by tobacco fumes. The rain drummed on the window pane.

Two chairs appeared from somewhere and we were asked to take our seats. Everybody wanted to speak to us at the same time but we restricted ourselves to monosyllables being restricted by language. This proved to be disastrous. The gang wanted a nice conversation and that too in Russian. To aid that two more glasses were produced, filled to the brim with the transparent liquid from the large bottles and presented with utmost ceremony, before us. Being thirstier than Halder I took the first sip in a hurry. The neat pure Russian vodka choked out all other emotions. I have never tasted lava from Mount Etna but this was the nearest thing I have ever tasted.

Halder was watching my emotions with interest with his glass poised before his lips. He slowly lowered it and looked around. So, did I when I could muster enough sense. We were being watched by critical eyes with expectant faces. The burliest of them all had a nice big moustache which dared me to say something derogatory about the national drink of Russia. When I managed a weak smile, the ice was broken and now everybody turned to Halder. He promptly tooka sip and almost fainted but he had already managed to keep a nice ear-splitting grin before he swooned.

Diplomatic relations were now restored with full vigour. However, our quota of woe was not full yet. Two large and juicy apples were placed in front of us. Neither Halder nor I were fond of fruits but having them as snacks with neat Russian vodka was pure torture. However, you do not argue with six and a half Russians wearing soiled knee length leather boots. So, we bit into the apples with due respect and the conversation now flowed. I know it started with Indira Gandhi and everybody praised her and so did we. The language differed but the spirit remained. MIGs, Raj Kapoor and Pakistan along with NATO featured in the discussion. However, whatever the point of discussion, our drinking was kept under scrutiny and whenever there was any slackness on our part, we were duly encouraged.

Now, Halder had a very interesting face. He was rather short and dark with no facial hair and his eyes, in normal circumstances, looked as if they are going to pop out. He always wore a surprised expression because of his eyes and a lack of properly defined eyebrows. Presently the vodka and apple combined together and his eyes were about to shoot off like projectiles. To cap it he started speaking a splattering of Russian which he had picked up on the west coast when he was on liner service to Odessa on the Black Sea coast. This effectively put the spotlight on him and he was the cynosure of all eyes. He was sitting a bit away from the window. I was nearer it and had kept my glass on the sill. ‘his helped me pour about a peg out into the backyard and I did this at profound risk to life and limb but Halder was enjoying himself immensely by now. He spoke expansively on Indo Soviet friendship and the 1971 war with Pakistan when the US 7th Fleet was held at bay by the Soviets. His Russian was proving inadequate for the task but he effortlessly switched to English sprinkled with Bengali. Slowly the level of vodka in his glass decreased and the splattering of Bengali increased.

Drinks were flowing freely around the room and Russian, English and Bengali, three languages separated by distance met in a corner of Siberia. I took this opportunity to pour out another peg over the window but Halder, by now was beyond care. I had managed to empty about two pegs out through the window but the remainder was beginning to take effect. The room and its occupants were distinctly becoming hazy but still I kept my cool since I was sure that at some point of time Halder was going to lose his.

When he had finished his glass and about to give the impression of the take-off sound of a MIG 21, he crash-landed in the middle and slumped on his chair. Our hosts were not taken aback but asked me the name of our ship which I fortunately still remembered. The moustached man went out and shortly the roar of engines and a loud clattering broke out above the drumming of the rain. The burly man had brought a bulldozer Halder was hand carried and I followed in a dignified, though zigzag walk. Halder was lifted behind the driver’s seat. I could barely make out the swirling steps and managed to reach the top.

There were hearty farewells and cheers from our hosts and I waved in a carefree manner hitting the side of the bulldozer bruising my hand quite severely. A short rattling drive and we were alongside M.V. Nirmal Bhushan. Halder was in no condition to get down. I too found difficulty in locating the gangway though I managed to get down with the help of my Russian Chauffer. The Bosun had spotted us from the deck and the crew mustered promptly and Halder was carried up the gangway. I got a bear hug from my host before he drove away. I took my time but reached my cabin without further mishap.

Halder survived the onslaught. Over the years, I too have made my peace with the national drink of Russia but even today I wince unknowingly before I take a sip and yes, I have never ever touched an apple when drinking.

chapters.title: Pirates of the Caribbean and Yanni Yanankis
paragraphs: Before proceeding with the pirates, an introduction to Yanni is necessary since he is so intrinsically involved with the climax of the show.

Thad joined M.V. Evpo Ageos in Buena Ventura in Colombia. Colombia is not exactly renowned for law and order. I had managed to cross the Andes from Cali (the former drug Baron Pablo Escobar’s stronghold) without getting shot or stabbed. The beauty of Andes was always foreshadowed by the feeling that something might happen. Here Andes lacked the stark beauty of the Chilean high Andes. This was the land of evergreens dripping with moss. The road wound up and down across mountains and valleys through thick forests. The car crossed waterfalls which were flowing over the road. Sturdy men wearing huge hats which resembled the Mexican Sombrero in size plodded up and down with pack mules carrying big laden sacks. Along with the hat they all wore revolvers slung in leather holsters. My car was held up at regular intervals by army barricades for check-up and every time I disembarked to be groped by burly commandos, Still I reached my vessel safely.

That evening, there was pandemonium on the vessel. The fitter, Maruti Narayan Pakar, came running up, out of breath. He was shortly followed by a man who resembled a gorilla minus the hair who too was out of breath. He was huge and resembled one of the more muscular participants in WWF.
