In facts he seemed to sprout muscles from every pore. His face was clean shaven and teeth a strange shade of greyish yellow from the nicotine of his nonstop cigarette smoking. This was Yanni Yanankis the Greek from Cyprus. He was wearing dark coloured clothes and hence the blood was not noticeable till he entered the alleyway. His Tshirt was liberally spattered. It transpired that Parkar and Yanni were walking back to the ship after a sojourn to the city. Near the dock a gang of five people suddenly started surrounding them. Since Parkar was walking a few paces behind, he sensed danger and took another road. Yanni was surrounded by the gang who were carrying iron rods. Unknown to them, Yanni always used to carry a Rambo knife on his person in dubious places. Before the gang could pounce on him, his knife was out and had slashed the belly of the man in front of him. Seeing their comrade slumping to the ground, holding his guts, the rest of the gang vanished in one direction and Yanni in the other.

Next day evening, I was chatting with the Chief Engineer, Mr. Sampath Kumar. He was short genial gentleman from Madras with a very pleasant disposition and mild manners. Yanni came and knocked at the door requesting permission for shore leave.
Bara Saab was aghast.

‘Yanni, yesterday you probably murdered a man and today you want to go out? Are you nuts? They may be waiting for you out on the streets. If they catch you again what will you do?’ asked the astonished Bara Saab.
“Chief, how many men do you have in the engine room?’ asked Yanni calmly.

‘Nine. Why?’ ‘Chief, Iam not carrying anything at the moment. You call all your men and see what I can do.” This was Yanni... a giant, dangerous but humorous man and I loved to fight with him verbally at considerable risk to my life and limb. I could not help it. He had a cigarette dangling from the corner of his mouth all the time and his silver tooth gleamed whenever his grave mouth broke into a smile, which was rare. His arguments with me generally ended with him rattling me to my bone by shaking me like a glass of Martini if I was not nimble enough to elude those hammer-like hands.

He had a childlike demeanour and used to get into ridiculous altercations. Those were the days of trunk calls and no satellite phones. Once in Curacao in the islands of Netherland Antilles Yanni and I had gone ashore to make a telephone call to our homes in the evening. We reached the telephone office and asked the young petite lady at the counter to book our calls. My call went through smoothly but Yanni’s number kept returning a busy tone. On being informed that his telephone was busy Yanni lost his temper and demanded to know in his Greek voice, why his wife’s telephone number was busy at two in the morning (that was presumably the time at his home in Limassol). Things went quite out of hand when | spotted two police officers approaching from the other side of the road which gave an extra dose of adrenaline and I was able to coax Yanni out of the telephone office before he could damage the equipment.

Late in the evening M.V. Evpo Ageos reached the British Guayan port of Georgetown which is the capital of the little Caribbean country famed for producing some extraordinary cricketers like Rohan Kanhai and Alvin Kalicharan. After steaming for a while, we anchored in the middle of the river Essequibo near the city. By the time the tedious process of initial clearance was over and I cleared all the necessary messages for Head Office in Athens it was almost two in the morning.

Next two hours I lay tossing on my bunk but sleep eluded me. The air-conditioning felt suffocating and I opened the porthole to let in some fresh air. The breeze floated lazily into my dark cabin. It was still dark outside but the eastern horizon showed the slight flicker of dawn. I could see the deck from my vantage view point. The huge deck lights illuminated the cargo holds which ran through midships. Beyond lay the dark waters of the river and the lights of the city twinkled in the distance. There were four cargo holds on the ship and the four cargo hatches half a man high ran through the middle. There were gaps in the middle of the hatches for walking from the port to starboard and vice versa. Delon, our Deck Cadet was taking soundings in the focs’le and I could make out his form. My eyes travelled back towards the accommodation when I noticed five shadowy figures near hatch number four which was nearest to our accommodation. Since | could not make out the faces, | assumed that the crew were doing something but it was bit too early since they had gone to bed pretty late and no port activity was expected at that early hour.

This is when Delon started walking back towards the accommodation. He was on the starboard side and the shadowy figures were on the port side. Only when Delon came parallel to the figures that he noticed the figures and he stopped abruptly. The figures had not noticed Delon’s arrival since the hatch cover was in the middle and they were busy doing something. Delon crouched down and started walking back towards the accommodation. I was under the impression that he was going to play some prank on the shadowy figures whom I still believed were our crew when he made contact with something metallic which made a clanging sound. Then the action really started in earnest. The shadowy figures turned around and noticed Delon who was standing petrified. One of them pulled out a gun which brought him back to his senses and galvanized into action. He kept his head low and taking shelter behind the hatch cover made a dash for the accommodation ladder. I shouted at the top of my voice from my porthole. The Mate was taking over watch from the Third Mate which probably explained why nobody noticed the pirates in the first place. He heard the commotion and looked out and shouted. The gun toting pirate was trying to shoot Delon but the hatch cover came in his way and Delon climbed the stairs in a mad rush and promptly fainted. One of the crew was in the toilet and he rushed out clutching his pants.

On finding the pirates in front of him, he started shouting invectives and then noticed the gun. He promptly let go of his pants and covered his face with his hands and sat down. The Third Mate made a rush for the alarm bell and sounded the alarm. As the mad whistle echoed the pirates jumped overboard where a small motorised boat was waiting for them and they pushed off.

Pandemonium died down and everybody went back to sleep. It was past 0800 hrs when I stood next to the ladder at the spot where Delon had fainted. Maruti Narayan Parkar, our evergreen Fitter was standing by my side. The equatorial sun shone hot and bright. Parkar had somehow slept through the early morning incident including the alarm.

I was briefing him about the happenings when both of us noticed five figures in the same spot that I had seen the pirates. This time there was nothing shadowy about them since it was broad daylight and we two were standing closest to them. The Mate had noticed them too at the same time and sounded the alarm bell. The pirates saw us and they took out nasty looking swords, short but wickedly crooked. Fortunately, this lot did not carry a gun. With the swords they heartily beckoned me and Parker but we refused to join them for a tete-a-tete and stood transfixed. Suddenly there was running sound from behind us and before we could turn around, Maung Maung Thin, our diminutive Ordinary Seaman shot out and charged the pirates. In his hand he was carrying a trident which was fabricated by Parkar before our arrival. We watched the little man dumbfounded and the pirates also watched with astonished eyes the charge of the one-man light brigade. Maung Maung Thin charged on heedless but when he had reached halfway down between us and the pirates, he suddenly seemed to remember that something was amiss. He halted in his tracks and looked back. It was then that he realised that there was a total lack of reinforcement since neither Parkar nor me had moved an inch since we were too taken aback. I must admit that the prospect of taking on five sword wielding pirates with a fifty-year-old, though sturdy man and an Ordinary Seaman who would have hardly have made it to the featherweight category, did not particularly appeal to me. Maung Maung Thin made up his mind quite fast. He threw down the trident and shot back double the speed in which he had charged and I don’t blame him.

The ship was buzzing with activity by now. All officers and crew available were running towards the aft of the ship where steel stanchions were stored to arm ourselves in emergency. Parkar and me joined the rush and as I came abreast of the starboard side accommodation door, the steel door flew open and Yanni emerged. He had fabricated a medieval looking heavy cast iron rod at the tip of which he had welded half a dozen sharp iron protrusions. I had picked it up once with both my hands to test its weight and it was “heavy”. At the moment he was brandishing it with one hand clad in nothing but a flaming red underwear which was two sizes too short for him and his bathroom slippers. He found me in his path and heaving that iron rod of his over the head asked me the whereabouts of the pirates.

I nimbly stood aside politely pointing at the sword welding chaps on the opposite side of the deck. It was like showing the red flag to a bull... probably the bull would have been slightly less aggressive. Yanni literally flew down the ladder, landed on the deck (the deck was made of rather good quality steel), with an effortless jump got on top of the hatch cover and brandishing that medieval weapon high above his head and a blood curdling ‘hoy hoy hoy hoy’ charged the descendants of Red Rackham. He did not look back for reinforcement and once again I played the role of a silent and stunned spectator to the hilt. One look at a charging 140 kg Greek in red thongs with an iron rod was enough. There were five splashes in unison as the pirates jumped overboard.
The story about Yanni won’t complete without two more incidents one in Georgetown itself and the next one pertaining to the 1994 World Cup Football in USA.

These were the days when Merchant Navy had still not shed the aura and become a floating MNC. Things used to be done differently and a ship was a ship as it used to be for ages. We were in Georgetown anchorage waiting to sail inland to Linden. As in many other ports around the world in those days, many girls had boarded the vessel. In fact, there were twice as many girls on the ship as there were officers and crew and there were catfights among the girls for getting business. I started locking myself in my cabin at night to avoid getting pulled up in the middle of the night by the girls soliciting business. I had the habit of reading books late into night.

One such night I grew hungry. The clock showed well past two in the morning and I presumed all will be quiet and I will be able to grab a quiet bite in the solitude of the Officer's Mess. I walked down the stairwell and all seemed still. The generators in the engine hummed and all was quiet. However, as I got closer to the Officer’s Mess two floors down, there came to my ears, the throbbing sound of music. Curiosity pulled me to the closed door. I should have known better.... the door is never closed except during cleaning and I should have smelt a fish and beat.

Curiosity got the better of me and I opened the door and peeped inside. The music hit me like a hammer and when I actually gathered my wits and peered in there was full blown cabaret going on with six girls in various stages of nudity swaying and shaking to the beats of MJ. The Greek officers were all present except for our Captain and their huge frames rocked to and fro. I was about to retrieve my head in a hurry when a ham like hand caught me by the neck and pulled me in, slamming the door shut behind me. I sheepishly looked up at a hugely grinning face of Yanni. There was a large settee in a corner and I was physically thrown into it. A large array of bottles was generously displayed on the tea table in various stages of consumption. Yanni picked up a beer jug filled it to the brim with the national drink of the Greeks, Ouzo made from aniseed. It is a potent drink when mixed with water though the iron clad stomachs of the Greeks are capable of coping with it without water. I looked at the glass in horror but Yanni’s stern command ‘drink it’ along with his tweaking of his fingers left no room for doubt about the outcome if I dared to insult Greece by refusing neat Ouzo. I closed my eyes and took a sip and felt the drink burning down my throat.

Yanni chuckled in delight and moved back to the dance floor but I had heard a giggle and it had not come from Yanni. I looked around and there was our diminutive Chief Engineer, Sampath Kumar sitting coyly in the other corner of the settee with a similar jug in hand. Stunned by the presence of Bara Saab, whom I knew, was above the vices of flesh, I almost blurted out, “et tu Brute’, when he started laughing.

‘Tam in the same boat as you brother’, he said gleefully. ‘I too had come down to get some snacks and had been summarily kidnapped by these mammoths.... now shut up and drink up.’ I looked at his jug in disbelief. There were less than three fourths of the content left. I knew that he was capable of drinking as any other normal sailor but drinking three fourth of a beer mug of Ouzo without the benefit of water and still able to not only sit upright but actually talking sense was certainly beyond him.

Bara Saab almost read my mind and knowingly winked at a small pot in which the Cook was trying to grow a bonsai tomato tree and which was strategically located right next to the settee. The tree seemed to be drooping mysteriously or my eyes might have been playing tricks. Bara Saab gave a furtive glance at the festivities underway, picked up his mug and deposited some of the contents inside the pot and gave me a knowing smile. I followed suit promptly. However, it would not have been prudent to empty the mug at one go. So, we took our time and as the night wore on, our mugs lost their contents and by the time they were empty we had happily drooping bonsai tomato tree by our side. At least I hoped that it was happy.

As the morning was about to break and the participants ran out of steam, we slowly crawled out of the Officer’s Mess whish truly was a mess by that time, happy to be still alive. Our last glimpse of Yanni was him drooping limply and elegantly like big happy buffalo over the lunch table knocked out of his senses.

The other incident took place when the Word Cup Football was underway in USA. We were sailing from Kotka in Finland to Guatemala. The preliminary round saw Greece taking on Argentina. We had to sail out a day before the match. Those days there was no satellite television leave alone internet which was probably still not born. There was only one way to watch the match and that was by picking up the signal from a coastal TV tower.

The Captain gave the order to take the vessel as near to the German coast as possible without having to beach. As we neared the German coast, the TV caught started receiving the signal and the stadium where the finals were being held, appeared on the screen. All officers who had gathered around the TV in the Officers Mess gave out a cry of joy. ‘I'he Greeks know how to make their joy felt and I am sure that the yodelling must have been picked up on the German coast now faintly visible and getting closer. We started steaming at dead slow ahead.

Everybody took their seats with refreshments at hand. I was the only Indian present and was surrounded by mountains of Greeks all around me. The TV soon became almost invisible through a thick pall of cigarette smoke as Maradona shook hands with his Greek counterpart whose name had failed to register in my mind. I was too engrossed at seeing my hero Maradona and totally forgot that Yanni was sitting by my side with the cigarette dangling from his mouth as if it was a part of him.

The game started and everything around me became hazy as I focussed on the only game I have ever loved. My hero was playing and nothing else mattered. The Greeks boomed in unison as their team launched their attack. I clenched my teeth and gripped the handle of the settee fearing the worst but my hero’s team survived and began attacking. The game went on to and fro without any side gaining the upper hand.

I picked up cube of cheese with a toothpick and popped it in my mouth when Maradona received the ball somewhere between the penalty box and the centre line and started running towards the Greek goal. A slight swerve towards the left and one of the Greek players lost his balance, a short run and another swerve towards the right and two Greek defenders were floored. There was only one more defender and the goalkeeper to beat and a goal seemed a certainty. | threw caution to the winds in the heat of the moment ignoring the fact that there was no other sound coming from my fellow spectators. With hoop of joy in anticipation I jumped up from my seat. To jump up from a sitting position I needed a fulcrum and found Yanni’s uncovered right knee (he was wearing a pair of short shorts) near at hand. I used it. T forgot that I was holding the sharp end of the toothpick the sharp side down.

In hindsight, I think this is what he took offence at.... being prodded in his bare knee with a toothpick. Maradona’s shot went wide by a whisker and I resumed my seat but the stony silence made have a look around. What I saw at a glance was not a sight for the faint hearted. An army of Greeks each weighing about a tonne were gazing wistfully at me and the absence of mercy of any kind in their looks would have disconcerted even Napoleon. However, I was not given much time to gaze into their eyes. I short growl, some heavy breathing next to my ear and the next moment I was dangling in thin air, having been caught by my shirt collar by a pair of pincer-like hands. The words that Yanni emitted would have done credit to any ancient mariner, though they were in Greek. This was one time when I understood the language quite thoroughly. He caught picked me up with one hand and dangling, brought me to the door of the Mess, opened the door with his other hand and the next moment I sailed smoothly through the air and landed in the alleyway. The door banged shut and I could hear the delirious hoops of joy from the appreciative audience.
I spent watching the rest of the match in the Crew Mess with the deckhands and the stewards and enjoyed the match thoroughly since Argentina won.
It is a different matter that I studiously avoided Yanni for the next few days.

chapters.title: Paradise Regained
paragraphs: Tandoori paneer, gobi pakora, brandy with hot water and lemon, crispy chicken nibbles kept appearing and disappearing. Afterwards, for the main course, the pasta sat in a bed of cheese, the tandoori chicken gave off an aroma that was enough to make the most ardent vegetarian swoon, the rajma had a nice sheen of ghee floating on top and finally when the gulab jamuns appeared, they were the size of cannon balls but tastier.

Being a guest of the Indian Army was not an easy affair on the digestive system and I was generously invited by Col. J.S. Rawal of 134 Eco Task Force of the Indian Army, stationed at Nameri for reclamation and reforestation of the buffer zone (which they are doing very creditably, having already planted more than 2.5 million saplings against heavy opposition from local land grabbers and militant outfits), on the occasion of his daughter's birthday.

Since I was not expecting a dinner in the Officers’ Mess in the middle of a jungle trip, I turned up in my jungle uniform of a shirt, shorts and a pair of generously soiled jungle boots and sat playing tambola and sipping brandy and hot water amid smartly attired ladies and gentlemen. While the game was in progress and I managed to scratch out three numbers in three different lines on my chit of paper, a great volume of trumpeting broke out behind the administrative building. ‘he Commanding Officer Col. Rakesh Singh mumbled "bloody elephants" without looking up from his numbers and nobody else paid any attention till ] jumped up, threw down my pencil and rushed towards the source of the sounds. Somebody mentioned something about the crankiness of wildlife photographers but I did not look back.

As I reached the electrified fence behind the administration building, I saw their shadows passing down the jungle path. There were five adults and a calf. They passed so close that I could see the glint of white on the tusk of a tucker in the waning moonlight. What took the breath away was the trumpeting, one of them let off from time to time. I felt like standing in front of a bagpipe as the Highland Regiment was about to charge. The electrified fence kept us separated and they hardly paid a heed to my tiny frame as they slowly shuffled down the jungle path towards my bungalow which did not have the luxury of an electrified fence and on their way to one of the adjoining paddy fields.

The party was over by eleven at night. As the C.O. Col. Rakesh Singh, Col. J.S. Rawal and Col. Jaggi came down to see me off at the gate with the Unit's mascot dog, Lucky, the huge German Shepherd, I dithered at the prospect of walking down that dark forest road for about 200 metres to my bungalow, where the pachyderms might be taking a snack from a roadside bamboo clump, alone. The C.O., noticing my hesitation, graciously offered to escort me to my residence along with the two officers. Lucky, too behaved like an officer and a gentleman and joined my escort.

As our party came around a bend in the path, something jumped onto the path from the undergrowth. I shone my flashlight in its direction. It looked like a jackal but too big to be one. Lucky did not wait for identification. He charged straight at the supposed jackal. Lucky was almost twice its size but it did not budge. The pink tongue lolled and the ferocious eyes glistened steadfastly at the charging German Shepherd. When Lucky was about a couple of feet away, it dawned on him as well as me that his unflinching adversary was no ordinary Jackal but the braveheart of the jungle, the Indian Wild Dog or the Dhole, known to have successfully attacked tigers in a pack. Lucky ground to a halt and the two siblings separated by evolution, stood face to face sizing up each other. Finally, brotherly relations prevailed and Lucky luckily returned to our group. I kept shining the torch into the eyes of the dhole till he decided to return back to his domain to join his group which must have been inside the foliage.

Whenever I feel that there is no hope for the future of our jungles and its inhabitants, I think about Nameri and Indian Army's Eco Task Force and the successful battle being waged by our soldiers for our future and hope returns once again. Those deep dark electricity-less nights with a star studded sky and the sound of the wind whispering sweet nothings to millions of leaves mingled with the sound of trumpeting elephant herds, those brilliantly fiery skies at sunset with the flocks of Red Breasted Parakeets flying like green and pink arrows, the audible swish of the wings of the Great Pied Hornbills as they come in to roost for the night on the branches of an ancient tree, the mighty snow-capped peaks of the Himalayas sitting like white bearded sadhus behind the smaller peaks by the side of the serene Jia Bhorali river, keeps repeating "all is not lost..."

chapters.title: Only Footsteps in the Dark Jungle: My "Ghost" Experience
paragraphs: Someone kept walking up and down the gravel and boulder “road” in the starlit night of Chandaka Dampara Wildlife Sanctuary. Whoever it was, he/she should have been plainly visible. He/she was not.

It is a small wildlife sanctuary very near Bhubaneshwar, the capital of Orissa or Odisha state of Eastern India. Hardly anybody other than bawdy college students looking for a place to party, showed up at the gates. In my eight days in the sanctuary I saw only two groups of tourists who had actually come to see the sanctuary and stayed for a night.

Chandaka has two places to stay, one is a small cluster of log huts atop a hillock and the other is the forest bungalow...a small bungalow with two rooms. I was staying at the bungalow. It had electricity but due to a recent storm, there was no electric supply for a major part of the time which suited me fine. The forest was said to be home to a herd of elephants, chital deer, rhesus macaques, leopards and one crocodile in one of the water bodies. There was plenty of bird life though. The sanctuary is dominated by a huge lake. The post monsoon forest was verdant green with the reddish forest tracks winding around the trees.

The dam in daylight However, the main route to the waterhole was closed to tourists. I got permission from the Forest Department for that route. Pradeep, the chowkidar of the bungalow, cooked a packet of Maggi early in the morning and then we rode on his motorbike to the waterhole. The waterhole had a watchtower but it was under lock and key since tourism was banned in the area. Water hole There was a guard who had a little hut by the side. He had a small patch of garden where he had planted brinjals, ladies’ fingers, and red spinach. At that particular point of time only the red spinach had survived the onslaught of wild boars. After an initial introduction Pradeep departed promising to pick me up after duty hours before sundown. He had given the chowkidar the responsibility of taking care of my lunch. “Sir, what would you like for lunch?” “What would you suggest?” “Red spinach and rice”, said he expansively as if presenting me with the menu of Raffles. “Do you have any other option?”, I enquired.
He looked vaguely around towards his vegetable patch as if looking for some foie-de-gras and on not finding any he suggested some chillies which he had in stock. I agreed to his suggestion.

I set up my camera next to the water hole and started my lone vigil. Certainly saw a few deer and rhesus macaques but the elephants were absent. A couple of hours later the chowkidar prepared my rice and spinach and left in his bicycle for his home leaving me to my own devices. The day ended uneventfully except for the fact that the spinach tasted heavenly with the rice and fresh chillies.

Peafowl in Chandaka This routine was repeated for the next nine days. The elephants stayed away from the waterhole and the monotony was broken only once by a male monkey and a deer. The young deer was grazing peacefully under the shade of a tree oblivious of the monkey sitting on a low branch just above him. I sat across the water hole lazily watching them when the monkey, with an effortless swing vaulted across the branch and still swinging from the branch, landed a juicy kick on the deer’s backside making him almost lose his balance. Next moment the monkey was sitting nonchalantly on the branch with an air of innocence looking intently at his finger nails while the deer, having regained his balance, glared with his beautiful liquid eyes balefully at the criminal. Deer and Langurs licking minerals and salt at water hole However, the monotony of the night was extremely broken up on the fourth night. The bungalow had small veranda with a collapsible gate which | used to lock after sundown to prevent incursions from any curious leopard. That evening I had done the same, lit my candle and settled comfortably in a chair in the covered veranda listening to the sounds of the jungle in the dark. In front of me, beyond the gate lay a small fallow land and the pebbled red earthen road with the forest gate about a kilometre to my left and road disappearing in the dark to my right. Beyond the road lay a dense patch of vegetation and short trees. The dam lay behind the vegetation and was not visible from where I sat though the distance was not more than fifty metres. My Forest Rest House in Chandaka There was no moon but the stars twinkled brightly throwing a faint yellowish light on the path which competed with my candle. There was no wind. Jungle fowls bade farewell to the day with their piercing cries and retired for the night. A pair of Spotted Owlets had their abode on a tree behind my bungalow. They were not the happiest of couples and had quarrels almost on a daily basis and that night was no exception. Cheeeeyaaaak chyazak alleged probably the female. Chyak Cheeeeeeeeek cheeeeek vehemently denied probably the male. They had bout of about ten to fifteen seconds with a rest periods ranging from two to twenty minutes. Jackals howled from the hillock down the road.

Around seven thirty or eight as the wind picked up, I heard the crunch of footsteps coming down the road. It was a bit early for Pradeep to come with my dinner. He might be coming to fetch something. I waited but couldn’t see the light of his lantern which he usually carried. The crunch on the gravel came closer and reached the path in front of my bungalow. To my utter surprise, no human figure materialized. The footsteps were clearly audible and whoever it was, was moving up the path towards the interior of the forest but of a tangible human form there was a complete absence. As I sat dumbfounded, the footsteps moved up the road and disappeared in the darkness of the forest. The wind blew strong and steady, the path was still lit by the stars, the jungle loomed dark and menacing and my candle light flickered. Whoever the disembodied soul was, he certainly had guts to walk along that path at that time of the night where wild elephants roamed. Pradeep coming with my breakfast As I sat musing on the guts of my invisible neighbour, I heard the steady steps of footsteps returning down the path. I heaved a sigh of relief assuming that the owner of the footsteps had realised the folly of walking on a forest trail at night without lights or human body with wild elephants around and was returning to the forest gate. This time I was ready with my torch to see the trespasser and as soon as the footsteps reached the front of my gate, I switched it on. The strong beam cut across the path and illuminated nothing more than the empty path and the trees and bushes beyond. Yet the footsteps continued down the path. T sat down heavily in my chair but I didn’t have to sit for long. The footsteps returned.

This time I can say without hesitation that my hand did quiver as I switched on the torch and as before saw nothing but heard the footsteps. The sound, like before went up the path, disappeared and reappeared only to pass down again. This kept on occurring, I sat very still in my dark corner without daring to switch on the light anymore. The candle died out and the darkness inside my balcony was total. The wind blew and the jungle became very quiet. I felt a bit cold which I told myself was due to the wind and not because of the figureless night walker outside my gate. For some reason the hair at the back of my neck kept on standing which too was due to the cold.

Pradeep returning down the path About three quarters of an hour passed when I saw the light of a storm lantern coming down the forest path from the gate. This surely was Pradeep and so it was. He had brought my dinner.
“Listen Pradeep, somebody is walking up and down the path for quite some time. Any idea who is he because I can’t see him by the light of my torch”, my voice, I am afraid, was not steady.

Pradeep gave me a strange look. “Nobody crossed the gate since sundown. I was at the gate.” “Wait a few seconds and you can hear for yourself.” Surely enough the footsteps returned. Pradeep took out his big torch and flashed and again there was nobody. The footsteps disappeared up the path. The look on his face could put Buster Keaton to shame.

“Wait a bit and he will return”, I assured Pradeep. I am not sure how much he got assured but he picked up a small, homemade spear from his room and in the other hand held his torch and waited at the gate. I, not having a spear, picked up a rather solid looking piece of wood though I was not sure how to tackle a footstep without a body with a spear or stick. However, it gave me something to hold on to instead of just hope and with my torch in the other hand I too waited.

The footsteps returned and both of us rushed out to the path in unison, torches blazing, spear and stick ready. On reaching the path we realised that the steps were coming not from the path but from behind the trees and bushes from the side of the dam. This was still more flabbergasting and we rushed headlong into the thicket like a pair of miniature elephants rushing to a waterhole.

Coming out on the other side of the thicket we probably expected to find a spectral body probably marching down wearing a pair of jackboots with blood dripping from the mouth but there was nothing...absolutely nothing. The water in the vast dam reflected back the starlight breaking into a million parts, the tress stood dark silhouetted against the sky swaying slightly in the breeze, the grassy knoll lay silent but of ghosts of any sort there was an acute shortage. However, the footsteps were quite audible in the stillness of the night and they were coming from the direction of the water.

Our torches revealed the secret of the footsteps. There was a row of fishing boats tied to stakes on the water’s edge. The “beach” had taken a horseshoe shape at this place and the wind had blown up a small ripple which travelled across the water’s edge from one end to the other and then the same ripple returned. The boats rose and plopped back on the water with the passing of the wave one after the other and that sound produced the effect of a footstep. As the boats rose and fell one after the other from one end of the miniature bay to the other the “footsteps” too arrived and faded till the wave returned.
My evening meal had gone cold by this time but yet tasted delicious and the footsteps lulled me to a blissful sleep soon after.

chapters.title: Lost in the Sands of Persia
paragraphs: Islamic Republic of Iran Shipping Lines (IRISL) was looking for Radio Officers on an urgent basis. I too was looking for a change from my previous company. We were made for each other. The day I came across the advertisement in the Marine Club in Bombay I popped into their Bombay agency and applied for the job. I was assured that I shall get a favourable reply shortly. I enquired about my status after a few days in the morning and was asked to report in the evening. I reported in the evening and was asked to come round in the morning. I reported next morning and .... the “Ringa Ringa Roses” continued and it continued for a month. The day I counted that I had just enough money to buy me a rail ticket home and a couple of meals, I dropped in at the office and told them that I was returning home and to let me know over phone in case the authorities in Tehran found my application letter and decided on anything positive. That night I caught the Howrah Mail back home.

It was early December morning when I reached home. After giving in to my one-year old son’s demands, since he hadn’t seen me for over a month, for attention, I fell asleep in the mellow winter sunshine at eleven in the morning. The phone rang in a couple of minutes and the voice from the other end was that of the agency in Bombay.

“You are flying out tomorrow early morning to join a ship. Come to Bombay immediately.” I reached Bombay that evening and was briefed that I shall be travelling to Bandar Imam Khomeini via Tehran and Abadan. I knew about Tehran and had heard about Abadan but Bandar Imam Khomeini was unknown. Those were pre-Google Earth days and I was quite used to travelling to unheard of destinations and hence did not bother much.

The first hurdle came up unexpectedly at the immigration counter in Sahar airport. When I produced my air ticket and passport, the official thumbed through the pages and finally asked for the visa. Now, Seamen usually don’t carry visas which are generally granted on arrival at the airports on arrival. I pointed this out to the concerned officer and showed him the letter issued by the company but he was adamant that though this was the case with most other countries, for Iran I need a visa. Finding arguments to be futile I asked him to enquire from his senior officer. To his credit, he took my advice.

‘We are letting you go but in case anything happens there, it will be your responsibility’, was his tart advice to me as he handed back my passport and documents after consulting with somebody higher up the hierarchy. It sounded like the voice of doom as if I was going to Pluto’s domain rather than going to just another country. How prophetic his words were, I failed to realise.
The aircraft was not overly crowded and there were plenty of vacant seats but I was given the aisle seat. I hate to travel on aisle seats but I was tired and fell asleep.

When the pilot informed over the PA that we were approaching Tehran, I woke up and took a peek over the shoulder of my fellow window side passenger. The scene down below was enough to make me sit up. Jagged mountain peaks covered with snow extended towards the horizon, the sky was so blue that it hurt the eyes and below lay Tehran. Since, in the row opposite to mine, there was a seat empty by the window side, I asked permission from the airhostess to take that seat so as to get a better look outside. When I asked her, the response startled me since her face showed all the emotions of horror as if I had asked something very indecent of her. However, further questioning revealed that since there was a lady sitting in the same row, the good airhostess had doubted my intentions. When I made my intention a little clearer, I was allowed to take another vacant seat next to the window. Tehran in the late morning light looked lovely indeed from above.

As I came out of the international terminal, the agent at Tehran was waiting for me to arrange for my transfer to the domestic airport. The domestic airport of Tehran was a revelation. It resembled V.T. Station of Bombay during peak hours. There were people running helter-skelter, families who did not get a chair in the lounge had arranged themselves comfortably on the floor and were having their meals. Mothers shouted at their children, grandpas and grandmas quarrelled, the men argued and made their views clear with elegant flourishes of their hands and there was a general air of relaxed pandemonium. Since the gentlemen at the counter understood little English, I, having fortuitously bagged a seat near the check in counter, settled down and monitored the display of scheduled flights which, fortunately, were in Persian and English.

By the time my flight was announced, the sun had gone down and it was evening. The aircraft which was supposed to take me to Abadan looked anything but airworthy and of a vintage Soviet make. However, having some knowledge of working with Russian equipment on various ships, I knew that they were not the best looking but did their job. Only the clasp of my seatbelt was broken and just managed to stay fastened. As the Pilot revved up the engine, the aircraft shuddered as if suffering from acute malaria. As it picked up speed, I felt that the wing will come apart at the seams. After an agonizing run it finally defied gravity and was airborne.

It was almost eight in the evening when the lights of Abadan came into view. The touchdown was gentle and the tired aircraft rolled into the bay. The small lounge was almost empty by the time my baggage consisting of a suitcase came up. Normally an Agent representing the company meets all seafarers coming to join or leave a ship with some sort of transportation. I sauntered out of the airport building expecting somebody waiting for my arrival as usual. There was nobody. Perhaps he was delayed due to some reason. I parked my suitcase in a corner and waited. ‘There was hardly any light. ‘he street lights threw a diffused glow having been obscured by some trees. In a very short time, the area was deserted. All the passengers had left. The security personnel were nowhere to be seen. A few cars went up and down the road in front of the building. The weather was cold... quite cold.

After a fifteen to twenty minutes wait, a man, emerged from the shadows of one of the side alleys and walked up to me. He wore a suit, which was in need of urgent repair, and he had just about managed to squeeze himself in it.
‘Joining ship?’, enquired the man narrowing his eyes as if he knew that I had ulterior motives.
‘Yes’, replied I. ‘Come’.

The brief formalities over, he walked back towards the alley with a wave of his hand asking me to tag along which I did. Shortly we came up to an old dilapidated container, the type which are transported by ships. It was made into a makeshift accommodation and was well lit. Doors and windows had been cut into the sides and looked quite like a shoe box of fables.

We entered the box and I was asked to sit in a comfortable chair while my friend rifled through a stack of papers. After a bit of excavation, he managed to locate a bunch of serious looking forms and we started back for the airport.

This time the airport building was totally forlorn... nobody was around; many lights had been switched off. My friend walked round a corner and a very old gentleman was sitting in a small cubicle slumped on a desk snoring lightly. He was awakened with a bit of difficulty and a conversation ensued in which I was the main point of attraction. The old man seemed to understand and with a look of glee, went through the forms and then thrust it towards me. With a stumpy finger, he pointed at a dotted line and asked me in to sign. Everything in the form was written in Persian and it could have been my life I signed away but I had no other option.
When my signature was taken, the old man and my friend gathered up the papers, got up and walked away. They walked out of the airport without a glance back.

I sat back in the chair, put up my feet on the desk and about to close my eyes when the sound of a suitcase being rolled on the floor came to my ears. A young man in jeans and heavy woollens was passing by. Both of us looked at each other and then he asked me something in Persian. ‘No Persian, only English’. A look of understanding dawned on his face. Joining ship?’.
‘Yes’.
‘Bandar Imam Khomeini”.
‘Yes’.
‘First time joining?’.
‘Yes’, “You know how to join?’ ‘No’.
“Come, | also join ship in Khomeini’.

I did not know him form Adam’s but followed him out of the airport on an instinct. Once outside, he whistled twice and a cab emerged from one of the side roads. My Good Samaritan’s and my suitcase were loaded onto the luggage hold, he sat in front beside the driver and I drooped on the back seat and we were off.

Shortly the lights of Abadan vanished behind us and the road stretched unending in front dissecting the desert. There were no lights on the highway. A few trucks passed and the engine droned on making a lullaby like humming noise. The car’s FM radio was playing a Persian song and the tune acted like a sleeping pill. My eyes kept on shutting and opening.

After about an hour and a half of driving, our cab started slowing down. I got up and looked out of the window, expecting to see the port of Bandar Imam Khomeini. There was pitch darkness outside except for a small lamp which glowed at a distance by the wayside. Slowly the car came to a halt as my Good Samaritan and the driver talked between themselves. This was bewildering. I could not understand a word that they were saying and was feeling groggy. The small light shone in front of a very dilapidated building which was about 50 metres from the road. There was no way of knowing why we stopped in the middle of nowhere. The driver and the Good Samaritan got off the car and walked across the dune to the building and entered. I kept sitting inside the cab.

Sometime later, shouting broke out inside the old building. Some heated altercation was going on inside. I sprinted across the dunes and entered the building. My Good Samaritan and the driver were now in the company of an old man with grey beard and they were trying to outshout each other. As the pitch rose, I stood by like a zombie understanding nothing. Finally, when I thought that some amount of blood spilling must start anytime, the Good Samaritan and the driver turned around in terrible fury and stormed out of the house with the grey beard shouting invectives behind their back and glaring at me. I gave him a sheepish grin and legged it.

My two companions had by then reached the cab. In front of my astonished eye the driver opened the baggage hold and pulled out my suitcase and summarily dumped it on the sand. The Good Samaritan looked on. As I was about to protest at being left deserted without prior notice, the Good Samaritan’s luggage too was fished out and similarly dumped. The small light of the old house behind us was switched off and the driver started the cab, turned around and drove back the way we had come.

The situation was too hopeless for me to care anymore. I nestled in the sand and leaned against my suitcase. My light jacket was hardly enough to keep the cold winter wind of the desert away. The sand gave just enough warmth to make it bearable without chattering my teeth. The wait began. The luminous dial of my wristwatch showed it about to be eleven. The sky was moonless. The millions of stars that had seen the passage of Cyrus and Daryus’s armies march across these sands and had stood mute witness to Alexander's invasion looked on at two insignificant creatures from their high pedestal. Intermittently a cold draught caught the sands in little whirlwinds and scattered across the dunes. About half an hour passed. I looked at the Good Samaritan who was sitting on his haunches chewing gum with knitted brows. “Any problems?’ I opened the conversation tentatively. ‘No problems. “Any car coming?’, I quizzed again after a thoughtful pause. ‘No car coming’.

This effectively killed our little chitchat. Since there was no problem and no car would be coming and I was somewhere in the vast deserts of Persia without knowing anything about my exact location in the dead of the night, I rolled up in my little sandy nest and looked up again at the stars. They seemed to be enjoying the situation.

Midnight passed and when I was about to seriously fall asleep with exhaustion, I saw the Good Samaritan jump to his feet. Fearing an attack on me driven by mental condition, I too got up prepared to defend myself when two headlights became visible on the highway miles down the road. They were approaching from the direction of Abadan. The Good Samaritan gesticulated to me to pick up my luggage and he gathered his and ran to the highway. I followed suit. It was a bus... or rather, was a bus. It had an engine, four tyres and a chassis which threatened to fall apart. It stopped when our forms became visible in the headlights. We bundled our luggage inside and boarded. Since there was an entire absence of seats except for the driver’s, we sat on our luggage.

It was past one thirty when we reached the huge port of Abadan. The Good Samaritan’s vessel was berthed next to the entrance but mine took other half hours of assiduous searching. The bus rattled away and I stood in front of my going to be office, waiting for some crew to pick up my luggage. No operations were going on and except for the bright cargo lights, the ship looked dead. She was a Korean made vessel and like many other Korean made ships, was tall for her tonnage. The huge accommodation towered overhead. Manly I lugged my luggage up the interminable wobbly gangway and six flights of steep stairs to reach the top deck where generally the Captain and Radio Officer’s accommodations are located. ‘I'he whole ship looked deserted. There were no watchmen or duty officer to be found anywhere. I stood outside the Captain’s cabin but all doors were shut as was to be expected. I contemplated my next move but before I could reach any conclusion heavy footsteps were heard in the alleyway and shortly, what appeared like a heavy shrubbery, walked up. There were two parts which were visible of the man. A belly to rival the bulbous bow of a super tanker and a moustache which certainly sprouted during the Neolithic Age and had not touched the edge of any impudent scissors. That was Captain Zahre Ashraf Ali, in command of the vessel.
‘Ah.... new Raaadio Officer?’ he boomed looking at me from behind his camouflage of bushes.
‘Yes’. I apologetically replied. He did not look convinced in the least. ‘First time in the company?’ ‘Yes’.
‘Then you cannot join,’ and with this final verdict I was summarily dismissed as he turned to vanish down the alleyway.

Thad travelled across the Indian Subcontinent, traversed the Arabian Sea, got lost in the Iranian desert, had gone without food or water for the last eight hours (the flight to Abadan had supplied a small piece of sandwich and about 200ml of mineral water), survived the flight to Abadan, travelled in a contraption, which almost fell apart, to the port and climbed a multi-storeyed building and a flight of extremely wobbly ladder with all my luggage and here I was in the dead of the night, dismissed without notice by the Captain, without knowing where to go next. I could not get off the ship since I did have the clearance to go on board but not to get off it. I had not been allotted any cabin and I just could not walk into anybody’s cabin and dump my luggage and sleep off the incident as a bad nightmare. There were two options. I could stand in the middle of the alleyway for the rest of the night wailing to high heavens or I could try and plant a well-aimed kick on the seat of the pants of the receding human shrubbery. I was seriously contemplating the second option when Vinod, the man I was supposed to relieve, appeared on the scene.

A few quick words between us and he ran after the Old Man with speed and determination having realized that if I was unable to join then his chances of getting off the vessel would be extremely slim. Shortly he returned with a grin splitting his face.
“The Old Man thought that you have never sailed before. I told him that you have more time out at sea than him and now it is okay for you to join’ he informed.

Later when I cried on his shoulders and told him of my woes, he shrugged me off saying that it was one of the smoothest joinings. I did not believe him till he went home and wrote back to me saying that the agent, with great thoroughness and planning, had taken him to Abadan airport in a cab, one hour after the flight had taken off. He was then put on a rickety bus and left to his devices to traverse the sands of Persia, all the way north to Tehran. He did not elaborate how he survived the journey but assured me that it was eventful.

chapters.title: Orang National Park
paragraphs: Ina rather roller coaster career spanning over thirty years, I have been privy to some astonishing experiences. From watching Killer Whales chasing seals in Bering straits to losing my way in the deserts of Persia. ..getting chased by an irate wild buffalo up a tree to watching a cigarette packet dance in a dilapidated forest bungalow in the dead of the night inside the heart of the Himalayas, I have had my fair share of experiences in a journey called life. However, watching a charging rhino getting poked in the nose by the barrel of a gun is definitely an experience worth sharing.

Mid-January, Orang National Park, Assam...for some strange reason, the Shimuls (Cotton tree) had flowered early in unison turning swathes of the forest into a sea of red with a fluorescence which almost hurt the eyes...morning fog took time to dissipate and even at nine in the morning the wild tusker was hardly visible as he shuffled in the shadows of the trees gorging on the Elephant Apples...the Hog Deer grew fat from the early bounty of the fleshy Shimul flowers which lay strewn all over the forest floor like Red Carpet in Cannes and the night cold bit through the layers of wool but the winter sun during the day folded you like mother’s warmth. However, Orang’s claim to fame is not the Shimuls or the Deer or the rhinos and elephants but the striped cat. Orang holds the record for the highest density of tigers in the world as of 2018, In an area covering approximately 80 sq. km there are said to be more than 35 tigers. Just on a whim, in the month of December, from a grassy knoll overlooking a waterbody surrounded by grasslands in the middle of noon, I had imitated the call of a tiger and was astonished to be get a reply immediately. My call was replied about six to seven times before I fell silent.

Mid-January, Karel and Carina my old friends from Holland and myself checked into Assam Tourism’s Proshanti Lodge, the only staying option at present in Orang. Nothing much can be said about the lodge except that it is separated from the forest by a high wall and the food is delightful. Another private lodge is coming up shortly which probably is a good thing. We had planned to spend the last night in the Satshimuli Forest Bungalow belonging to the forest department on the last night of our stay but within a couple of hours of our check in, my local friend Salam da announced that the bungalow was available only that night. Some high-ranking officials were supposed to visit the next day onwards and Satshimuli would be out of bounds.

The sun had set and our headlight wove between the trees and the grasslands and by the time we reached Satshimuli, the full moon was up. To understand the night, I need to give a feel of the place though human language is inadequate to describe a small neat bungalow without any fencing whatsoever, situated in the middle of a forest with the highest density of tigers in the world. The back of the bungalow has a small grassy knoll and the land drops sharply to a half dry wetland and the grassland down below stretches away to the horizon. ‘The front of the bungalow as well as the grassy knoll is surrounded by Shimul trees which were in full bloom.

That night was a full moon. We sat in comfortable chairs on the knoll sipping hot milk tea prepared over a wood fire. The full moon shed an ethereal light over the vast grasslands in front of us. Huge fruit bats fought over eating rights in the Shimul trees overhead. The cold crept up from the wetland below us and tugged at our heavy woollens. A rhino moved silently slowly across the grassland, a huge shadow in the silvery light. Mist rose from the water and started forming a layer like a fine greyish silver veil as Hog deer barked out their shrill staccato alarm calls from all sides announcing that multiple big cats were on the move emphasizing the fact that this forest was home to more tigers per square km than any other place on the planet. They were still calling when, after a beautiful hot dinner, we crept into our warm blankets and promptly fell asleep.

Orang is a miniature Kaziranga but with a fraction of the number of tourists which suited us fine as we had the jungle more or less to ourselves. Khalil Chacha, my guard, was an old veteran forest guard with an even more ancient gun (he swore that it belonged to his grandfather from the British era) of which he was inordinately fond. It took careful aligning to load the bullets in line with the barrel but it looked grand and along with his beautifully henna-ed beard and all of five feet height he posed an impressive figure. The problem was he was scared of rhinos.

Khalil Chacha had worked in the park for the last thirty years and spoke no other tongue than the local version of Assamese. He was good at tracking and knew every nook and cranny of the forest but a mere suspicion of a rhino in the bushes sent him into a tizzy. We needed the shots but Khalil Chacha needed to put as much distance as possible between our Gypsy and the horns. The clash of interests was somewhat solved by me keeping one restraining and reassuring hand on his shoulder till we managed to at least hear our shutters, click.

We managed to pick up the fresh pug marks of tigers, a male, and one female with her two cubs in the morning on a dusty road and three more pug marks in the evening. However, sighting the tiger remained elusive though we crisscrossed the forest roads as much as we could. However, it was the beauty of the forest that was stunning. Kaziranga, Manas, Pobitora, Dibru Saikhowa, Tadoba, Pench etc. the places I frequent have a heavy flow of tourists which sometimes tend to mar the experience but Orang is a quiet backwater in spite of the incredible diversity of flora and fauna. The only lodging at the moment is rather shabby except for the food, which discourages tourists (the new private lodging hopefully will change the status quo) and the forest department is most laid back. The Gypsy drivers at the moment are more interested in phone calls while the safari is underway (I am trying to change that attitude. ..time will tell whether I have succeeded or failed) and most of the other guards try to complete the safari as soon as possible (not Khalil Chacha) and get back to their quarters. All said and done it is truly a beautiful place with very little disturbance from unruly and unethical tourists.

And then that fateful afternoon we were driving down a game track with barely a couple of feet to spare on either side of the tyres. Daud, the best driver, was at the wheels with Khalil Chacha by his side. Carina sat on the right in the middle row and I on her right. Karel, her husband took up the back seat. We were merrily driving down the track and winter afternoon shone golden on our backs. The golden hour was approaching and the green grass had taken on a yellowish tinge. Calls of the Brain Fever bird reverberated across the grassy glens and we were passing a huge patch of grassy plain where the grass was very short and we could see quite a distance except for a small clump of tall elephant grass on the left-hand side of the track.

We came up to the clump of grass quite slowly since the track was pockmarked with elephant, rhino and wild buffalo footprints and as we were about to pass the clump of grass, he was standing right by the wayside about 15 to twenty feet away. There was no indecision, there was no hesitation. He charged. He was positioned slightly below us since our track was a bit raised above the field. I have never seen a battle tank attacking enemy position but any battle tank can take a correspondence course from that rhino. In a split second he was merely inches from me and the mouth opened to show the huge razor-sharp teeth as he prepared to bite me since I was nearest to him. Shamelessly I turned and offered his teeth my backside simultaneously trying even more shamelessly to climb onto Carina’s lap. ‘I'he teeth whisked past and missed the seat of my trousers. Khalil Chacha acted. There was no time to release the safety catch and fire now. In front of my astonished eyes he jabbed the barrel of his grandfather’s gun right into the nose of the charging rhino. This deflected the massive head for just those seconds that Daud needed to press the accelerator and even over the jagged track we were gone with the wind.

Iam quite accustomed to working with rhinos in Kaziranga and Pobitora where I have had close encounters before but never have I been forced to try and climb onto the lap of a lady and offer my bum to a charging rhino before. This was a whole new ball game and I now fully empathise with Khalil Chacha’s sentiments - see rhino, leave rhino.
